Linear Functions.description: Functions of the form f(x) = mx + b where m is the slope and b is the y-intercept, producing straight-line graphs.
Linear Functions.hint: The coefficient of x tells you how steep the line is, and the constant tells you where it crosses the y-axis.

Polynomial Functions.description: Functions formed by sums of power functions with non-negative integer exponents, written as f(x) = aₙxⁿ + aₙ₋₁xⁿ⁻¹ + ... + a₁x + a₀.
Polynomial Functions.hint: The highest power (degree) determines the end behavior and maximum number of turning points.

Rational Functions.description: Functions expressed as the ratio of two polynomials, f(x) = p(x)/q(x), where q(x) ≠ 0.
Rational Functions.hint: Look for values where the denominator equals zero — these create vertical asymptotes or holes.

Algebraic Functions.description: Functions constructed using algebraic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, roots) on polynomials.
Algebraic Functions.hint: If you see a root symbol or fractional exponent combined with polynomials, you likely have an algebraic function.

Exponential Functions.description: Functions of the form f(x) = bˣ where b > 0, b ≠ 1, with the variable in the exponent.
Exponential Functions.hint: The base stays constant while the exponent varies — this creates rapid growth (b > 1) or decay (0 < b < 1).

Logarithmic Functions.description: Functions of the form f(x) = logᵦ(x), the inverse of exponential functions, answering 'what power gives x?'
Logarithmic Functions.hint: Remember logᵦ(x) asks 'b to what power equals x?' and is only defined for positive x.

Trigonometric Functions.description: Functions including sine, cosine, tangent and their reciprocals, originally defined from right triangle ratios and extended to all real numbers.
Trigonometric Functions.hint: These functions repeat their values in regular intervals (periods) — use the unit circle for angles beyond 0 to 90 degrees.

Power Functions.description: Functions of the form f(x) = xⁿ where n is any real number, with the variable as the base.
Power Functions.hint: Unlike exponentials, here the base changes while the exponent stays fixed — even powers create parabola-like shapes.

Piecewise-Defined Functions.description: Functions defined by different formulas on different intervals of the domain, often written with cases.
Piecewise-Defined Functions.hint: Evaluate by first determining which interval your x-value falls in, then use only that formula.

Absolute Value Function.description: The function f(x) = |x| that returns the distance from zero, creating a V-shaped graph.
Absolute Value Function.hint: Split into cases: |x| = x when x ≥ 0, and |x| = -x when x < 0.

Limit of a Function.description: The value that f(x) approaches as x approaches a specific value, denoted lim(x→a) f(x) = L.
Limit of a Function.hint: Focus on where f(x) is heading as x gets close to a, not necessarily what f(a) equals.

One-Sided Limits.description: Limits approached from only the left (x→a⁻) or only the right (x→a⁺) of a point.
One-Sided Limits.hint: Check behavior separately from each direction; left-hand and right-hand limits may differ at discontinuities.

Limit Existence Condition.description: A limit exists at x = a if and only if both one-sided limits exist and are equal.
Limit Existence Condition.hint: If lim(x→a⁻) f(x) ≠ lim(x→a⁺) f(x), the two-sided limit does not exist.

Numerical Limit Estimation.description: Approximating limits by evaluating f(x) at values progressively closer to the target point.
Numerical Limit Estimation.hint: Create a table with x-values approaching from both sides and observe the pattern in f(x) values.

Graphical Limit Analysis.description: Determining limits by examining the height a graph approaches as x nears a point.
Graphical Limit Analysis.hint: Trace along the curve toward the point; look at the y-coordinate the graph is heading toward, ignoring holes or jumps.

Limits at Discontinuities.description: Behavior of limits at points where the function has holes, jumps, or vertical asymptotes.
Limits at Discontinuities.hint: The limit can exist even when f(a) is undefined; check if both sides approach the same finite value.

Infinite Limits.description: When f(x) grows without bound (approaches ±∞) as x approaches a finite value.
Infinite Limits.hint: Look for vertical asymptotes; if f(x) increases or decreases without bound, write lim = ∞ or -∞.

Limit Notation.description: The symbolic representation lim(x→a) f(x) = L, including variants for one-sided and infinite limits.
Limit Notation.hint: Read 'lim(x→a) f(x) = L' as 'f(x) approaches L as x approaches a'; the arrow shows direction.

Limits of Piecewise Functions.description: Evaluating limits at boundary points where the function formula changes.
Limits of Piecewise Functions.hint: Apply the appropriate piece formula for each one-sided limit, then check if they match.

Function Definition and Notation.description: A function is a rule that assigns each element in the domain exactly one element in the range, written as f(x) or y = f(x).
Function Definition and Notation.hint: Check if each input value produces exactly one output value; vertical line test works on graphs.

Domain and Range.description: The domain is the set of all possible input values for a function, while the range is the set of all possible output values.
Domain and Range.hint: For domain, find x-values that don't cause division by zero or negatives under even roots; range follows from domain.

Function Composition.description: Function composition (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x)) means applying one function to the result of another function.
Function Composition.hint: Work from inside out: first evaluate the inner function g(x), then use that result as input for f.

Even and Odd Functions.description: A function is even if f(-x) = f(x) for all x (symmetric about y-axis) and odd if f(-x) = -f(x) (symmetric about origin).
Even and Odd Functions.hint: Substitute -x into the function and simplify; compare the result to the original function or its negative.

Vertical Line Test.description: A graph represents a function if and only if no vertical line intersects the graph more than once.
Vertical Line Test.hint: Imagine or draw vertical lines through the graph; if any line hits two or more points, it's not a function.

Function Transformations.description: Transformations including vertical/horizontal shifts, reflections, and stretching/compression modify the graph of a parent function.
Function Transformations.hint: Changes inside f(x) affect horizontal direction (opposite of what you expect); changes outside affect vertical direction directly.

Inverse Functions.description: A function f has an inverse f⁻¹ if f⁻¹(f(x)) = x and f(f⁻¹(x)) = x; graphically, inverse functions reflect over the line y = x.
Inverse Functions.hint: To find an inverse algebraically, swap x and y then solve for y; only one-to-one functions have inverses.

One-to-One Functions.description: A function is one-to-one if each output value corresponds to exactly one input value, verifiable by the horizontal line test.
One-to-One Functions.hint: Use the horizontal line test: if any horizontal line intersects the graph more than once, the function is not one-to-one.

Radian Measure.description: An angle measurement where one radian is the angle subtended by an arc length equal to the radius of a circle.
Radian Measure.hint: Remember that π radians equals 180 degrees, so multiply degrees by π/180 to convert to radians.

Unit Circle Definition.description: The definition of trigonometric functions using coordinates of points on a circle of radius 1 centered at the origin.
Unit Circle Definition.hint: For angle θ, the point (cos θ, sin θ) lies on the unit circle, making the x-coordinate cosine and y-coordinate sine.

Sine and Cosine Functions.description: The fundamental trigonometric functions where sin θ gives the y-coordinate and cos θ gives the x-coordinate on the unit circle.
Sine and Cosine Functions.hint: Sketch the unit circle and mark the angle from the positive x-axis to find the coordinates directly.

Tangent and Cotangent Functions.description: Trigonometric functions defined as tan θ = sin θ/cos θ and cot θ = cos θ/sin θ.
Tangent and Cotangent Functions.hint: Tangent is undefined when cosine equals zero (at odd multiples of π/2), so check the denominator first.

Secant and Cosecant Functions.description: Reciprocal trigonometric functions defined as sec θ = 1/cos θ and csc θ = 1/sin θ.
Secant and Cosecant Functions.hint: These functions are undefined wherever their reciprocals equal zero, creating vertical asymptotes.

Periodicity of Trigonometric Functions.description: The property that trigonometric functions repeat their values at regular intervals, with sine and cosine having period 2π.
Periodicity of Trigonometric Functions.hint: Use the fact that sin(θ + 2π) = sin θ to simplify expressions by subtracting multiples of 2π from large angles.

Pythagorean Identities.description: Fundamental trigonometric identities including sin²θ + cos²θ = 1, derived from the unit circle.
Pythagorean Identities.hint: When you see sin²θ or cos²θ, try substituting using sin²θ = 1 - cos²θ to simplify expressions.

Graphs of Trigonometric Functions.description: The visual representations of sine, cosine, and tangent functions showing their periodic behavior and key features like amplitude and period.
Graphs of Trigonometric Functions.hint: Start by plotting key points at 0, π/2, π, 3π/2, and 2π, then connect them with the appropriate wave shape.

Domain and Range of Trigonometric Functi.description: The sets of allowable inputs and corresponding outputs for each trigonometric function, with sine and cosine having range [-1, 1].
Domain and Range of Trigonometric Functi.hint: Check where denominators become zero to find excluded domain values for tangent, cotangent, secant, and cosecant.

Horizontal Line Test.description: A function is one-to-one if every horizontal line intersects its graph at most once.
Horizontal Line Test.hint: Draw horizontal lines across the graph; if any line hits the curve twice, the function is not one-to-one.

Inverse Function Definition.description: A function f⁻¹ that reverses the mapping of f, where f⁻¹(f(x)) = x and f(f⁻¹(x)) = x.
Inverse Function Definition.hint: To find an inverse, swap x and y in the equation, then solve for y.

Domain and Range of Inverses.description: The domain of f⁻¹ equals the range of f, and the range of f⁻¹ equals the domain of f.
Domain and Range of Inverses.hint: If (a,b) is on the graph of f, then (b,a) is on the graph of f⁻¹.

Reflection Property of Inverses.description: The graph of f⁻¹ is the reflection of the graph of f across the line y = x.
Reflection Property of Inverses.hint: Plot several points on f, then swap coordinates to get points on f⁻¹.

Restricting Domains for Invertibility.description: A non-one-to-one function can be made invertible by restricting its domain to a one-to-one portion.
Restricting Domains for Invertibility.hint: For functions like x², restrict to x ≥ 0 to make it one-to-one.

Verifying Inverse Functions Algebraicall.description: Verify two functions are inverses by showing f(g(x)) = x and g(f(x)) = x for all appropriate x.
Verifying Inverse Functions Algebraicall.hint: Compose both ways and simplify completely; both compositions must equal x.

Finding Inverse Functions Algebraically.description: To find f⁻¹, replace f(x) with y, swap x and y, then solve for y in terms of x.
Finding Inverse Functions Algebraically.hint: Start by writing y = f(x), exchange x and y, then isolate the new y.

Inverse Trigonometric Functions.description: Functions like arcsin, arccos, and arctan that reverse trigonometric functions on restricted domains.
Inverse Trigonometric Functions.hint: Remember arcsin has range [-π/2, π/2] and arccos has range [0, π].

Exponential Function Properties.description: Exponential functions have form f(x) = b^x where b > 0, b ≠ 1, with domain all reals and range (0,∞).
Exponential Function Properties.hint: Remember the base must be positive and not equal to 1; sketch y = 2^x to see why range is always positive.

Natural Exponential Function.description: The function f(x) = e^x uses Euler's number e ≈ 2.718 as the base, appearing naturally in growth and decay.
Natural Exponential Function.hint: Think of e^x as the 'natural' choice for continuous growth; its derivative equals itself.

Exponential Growth and Decay.description: Models of form f(t) = A·b^t describe growth (b > 1) or decay (0 < b < 1) over time.
Exponential Growth and Decay.hint: Check whether your base is greater than or less than 1 to determine if quantity increases or decreases.

Logarithm as Inverse Exponential.description: The logarithm log_b(x) is the inverse function of b^x, answering 'what power gives x?'
Logarithm as Inverse Exponential.hint: If you're stuck on log_2(8), ask yourself '2 to what power equals 8?'

Natural Logarithm Properties.description: The natural logarithm ln(x) = log_e(x) is the inverse of e^x with domain (0,∞) and range all reals.
Natural Logarithm Properties.hint: Remember ln and e^x undo each other: ln(e^x) = x and e^(ln x) = x for valid inputs.

Logarithmic Function Properties.description: Key properties include log_b(xy) = log_b(x) + log_b(y), log_b(x/y) = log_b(x) - log_b(y), and log_b(x^r) = r·log_b(x).
Logarithmic Function Properties.hint: Use these properties to break complex logarithms into simpler pieces; multiplication inside becomes addition outside.

Change of Base Formula.description: The formula log_b(x) = ln(x)/ln(b) converts any logarithm to natural logarithms for calculation.
Change of Base Formula.hint: When your calculator only has ln, use this formula to compute log_b(x) by dividing two natural logs.

Graphs of Exponential Functions.description: Exponential graphs pass through (0,1), are always positive, increase for b > 1, decrease for 0 < b < 1, with horizontal asymptote y = 0.
Graphs of Exponential Functions.hint: Plot the key point (0,1) first, then check if your base makes the function rise or fall as x increases.

Graphs of Logarithmic Functions.description: Logarithmic graphs pass through (1,0), have domain (0,∞), vertical asymptote x = 0, and are reflections of exponential graphs over y = x.
Graphs of Logarithmic Functions.hint: Start by plotting (1,0) and drawing the vertical asymptote at x = 0; remember the graph only exists for positive x.

Tangent Line Problem.description: Finding the slope of a line that touches a curve at exactly one point without crossing it nearby.
Tangent Line Problem.hint: Start by calculating slopes of secant lines between the point of tangency and nearby points, then observe what happens as the nearby point gets closer.

Secant Line Approximation.description: A line through two points on a curve that approximates the tangent line as the points move closer together.
Secant Line Approximation.hint: Calculate the slope using the difference quotient (f(x+h)-f(x))/h where h is the horizontal distance between points.

Instantaneous Rate of Change.description: The rate at which a function is changing at a specific instant, found by taking the limit of average rates of change.
Instantaneous Rate of Change.hint: Think of this as your speedometer reading at one moment, not average speed over a trip—use limits to find it from average rates.

Area Under a Curve.description: The region between a function and the x-axis over an interval, calculated by summing infinitely many infinitesimally thin rectangles.
Area Under a Curve.hint: Start by approximating with a finite number of rectangles, then increase the number of rectangles and observe the pattern as their width approaches zero.

Riemann Sum Approximation.description: A method of approximating area under a curve by summing the areas of rectangles with heights determined by function values.
Riemann Sum Approximation.hint: Divide your interval into n equal parts, calculate rectangle areas using function values at chosen points, then sum them up.

Differential Calculus Overview.description: The branch of calculus concerned with rates of change, slopes of curves, and derivatives.
Differential Calculus Overview.hint: Focus on how limits transform the tangent line problem into a systematic method for finding instantaneous rates.

Integral Calculus Overview.description: The branch of calculus concerned with accumulation, areas, and antiderivatives.
Integral Calculus Overview.hint: Recognize that finding area under curves requires summing infinitely many pieces, which is accomplished through limits.

Sum Law for Limits.description: The limit of a sum equals the sum of the limits: lim[f(x) + g(x)] = lim f(x) + lim g(x).
Sum Law for Limits.hint: Find the limit of each function separately, then add the results together.

Difference Law for Limits.description: The limit of a difference equals the difference of the limits: lim[f(x) - g(x)] = lim f(x) - lim g(x).
Difference Law for Limits.hint: Evaluate each limit individually, then subtract the second from the first.

Constant Multiple Law for Limits.description: The limit of a constant times a function equals the constant times the limit: lim[c·f(x)] = c·lim f(x).
Constant Multiple Law for Limits.hint: Factor out the constant, find the limit of the remaining function, then multiply by the constant.

Product Law for Limits.description: The limit of a product equals the product of the limits: lim[f(x)·g(x)] = lim f(x)·lim g(x).
Product Law for Limits.hint: Calculate each limit separately, then multiply the results together.

Quotient Law for Limits.description: The limit of a quotient equals the quotient of the limits when the denominator limit is nonzero: lim[f(x)/g(x)] = lim f(x)/lim g(x), provided lim g(x) ≠ 0.
Quotient Law for Limits.hint: Check that the denominator's limit is nonzero before dividing the limits separately.

Power Law for Limits.description: The limit of a power equals the power of the limit: lim[f(x)]^n = [lim f(x)]^n for any positive integer n.
Power Law for Limits.hint: Find the limit of the base function first, then raise that result to the given power.

Root Law for Limits.description: The limit of a root equals the root of the limit: lim[f(x)]^(1/n) = [lim f(x)]^(1/n) when the root is defined.
Root Law for Limits.hint: Evaluate the limit inside the radical first, then take the nth root if it exists.

Limit of Polynomial Functions.description: For polynomial functions, the limit as x approaches a equals direct substitution: lim(x→a) p(x) = p(a).
Limit of Polynomial Functions.hint: For polynomials, simply substitute the value of a into the function to find the limit.

Limit of Rational Functions.description: For rational functions p(x)/q(x), if q(a) ≠ 0, then lim(x→a) p(x)/q(x) = p(a)/q(a) by direct substitution.
Limit of Rational Functions.hint: Substitute the value directly, but first verify the denominator does not equal zero at that point.

Combining Multiple Limit Laws.description: Complex limit problems require sequential application of multiple limit laws in the correct order.
Combining Multiple Limit Laws.hint: Break down complex expressions into simpler parts, apply the appropriate law to each part, then combine results.

Continuity at a Point.description: A function f is continuous at point a if lim(x→a) f(x) = f(a), requiring the limit exists, f(a) is defined, and they are equal.
Continuity at a Point.hint: Check three things: does f(a) exist, does the limit exist, and are they the same value?

Discontinuity Types.description: Discontinuities are classified as removable (hole), jump (different one-sided limits), or infinite (vertical asymptote).
Discontinuity Types.hint: Draw the graph and look for holes (removable), jumps (different left/right limits), or vertical asymptotes (infinite).

One-Sided Continuity.description: A function is continuous from the right at a if lim(x→a+) f(x) = f(a), and from the left if lim(x→a-) f(x) = f(a).
One-Sided Continuity.hint: For endpoints of intervals, check only the appropriate one-sided limit equals the function value.

Continuity on an Interval.description: A function is continuous on an interval if it is continuous at every point in that interval, using appropriate one-sided limits at endpoints.
Continuity on an Interval.hint: Check continuity at each interior point and use one-sided continuity at any endpoints.

Continuity of Polynomial and Rational Fu.description: Polynomial functions are continuous everywhere; rational functions are continuous on their domains (wherever the denominator is nonzero).
Continuity of Polynomial and Rational Fu.hint: Polynomials never have discontinuities; for rational functions, just avoid division by zero.

Continuity of Composite Functions.description: If g is continuous at a and f is continuous at g(a), then the composition f∘g is continuous at a.
Continuity of Composite Functions.hint: Check the inner function's continuity first, then check the outer function at the inner function's output value.

Intermediate Value Theorem.description: If f is continuous on [a,b] and N is between f(a) and f(b), then there exists c in (a,b) where f(c) = N.
Intermediate Value Theorem.hint: To show an equation has a solution, find two points where the function is above and below your target value.

Removing Discontinuities.description: A removable discontinuity at a can be eliminated by redefining f(a) to equal lim(x→a) f(x).
Removing Discontinuities.hint: Calculate the limit at the discontinuity; if it exists, redefine the function value to match that limit.

Epsilon-Delta Definition of Limit.description: A limit L exists at c if for every ε > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that |f(x) - L| < ε whenever 0 < |x - c| < δ.
Epsilon-Delta Definition of Limit.hint: Draw vertical bands at L ± ε and find a horizontal band around c that keeps outputs within those bounds.
Epsilon-Delta Definition of Limit.section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

Epsilon as Error Tolerance.description: Epsilon (ε) represents how close we want f(x) to be to the limit value L.
Epsilon as Error Tolerance.hint: Think of epsilon as your acceptable error: smaller epsilon means you demand the function gets closer to L.
Epsilon as Error Tolerance.section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

Delta as Input Constraint.description: Delta (δ) represents how close x must be to c to guarantee f(x) is within ε of L.
Delta as Input Constraint.hint: After choosing epsilon, solve the inequality |f(x) - L| < ε for |x - c| to find a suitable delta.
Delta as Input Constraint.section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

Proving Limits with Epsilon-Delta.description: Formally proving a limit involves showing that for any given ε, you can find a corresponding δ that satisfies the definition.
Proving Limits with Epsilon-Delta.hint: Start by assuming ε is given, manipulate |f(x) - L| < ε algebraically to isolate |x - c|, then choose δ based on your work.
Proving Limits with Epsilon-Delta.section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

One-Sided Epsilon-Delta Limits.description: One-sided limits use the epsilon-delta definition with the restriction that x approaches c from only one direction.
One-Sided Epsilon-Delta Limits.hint: For right-hand limits use 0 < x - c < δ, and for left-hand limits use 0 < c - x < δ in your proof.
One-Sided Epsilon-Delta Limits.section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

Geometric Interpretation of Epsilon-Delt.description: The epsilon-delta definition creates a rectangular region where if x is within δ of c, then f(x) stays within ε of L.
Geometric Interpretation of Epsilon-Delt.hint: Sketch horizontal lines at y = L ± ε and find vertical lines at x = c ± δ that keep the graph between the horizontal lines.
Geometric Interpretation of Epsilon-Delt.section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

Delta Depends on Epsilon.description: The value of δ typically depends on the chosen ε, often requiring δ to be smaller as ε decreases.
Delta Depends on Epsilon.hint: When proving limits, express delta as a function of epsilon, like δ = ε/2 or δ = min(1, ε/3).
Delta Depends on Epsilon.section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

Infinite Limits Precise Definition.description: The limit of f(x) as x approaches c is infinity if for every M > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that f(x) > M whenever 0 < |x - c| < δ.
Infinite Limits Precise Definition.hint: Replace the ε condition with requiring f(x) to exceed any large value M when x is close to c.
Infinite Limits Precise Definition.section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

Average Rate of Change.description: The ratio of change in function values to change in input values over an interval, computed as [f(x+h)-f(x)]/h.
Average Rate of Change.hint: Draw a secant line connecting two points on the curve and find its slope using rise over run.

Difference Quotient.description: The expression [f(x+h)-f(x)]/h that measures average rate of change and forms the basis of the derivative definition.
Difference Quotient.hint: Substitute x+h into the function, subtract f(x), then divide everything by h before taking the limit.

Derivative at a Point.description: The limit of the difference quotient as h approaches 0, representing the instantaneous rate of change at a specific x-value.
Derivative at a Point.hint: Simplify the difference quotient algebraically to cancel the h in the denominator before applying the limit.

Derivative Function Notation.description: Symbols f'(x), dy/dx, df/dx, or d/dx[f(x)] all representing the derivative as a function of x.
Derivative Function Notation.hint: The prime notation f'(x) and Leibniz notation dy/dx are interchangeable ways to denote the same derivative.

Differentiability at a Point.description: A function is differentiable at x=a if the limit defining f'(a) exists and is finite.
Differentiability at a Point.hint: Check that both left-hand and right-hand limits of the difference quotient exist and are equal.

Differentiability Implies Continuity.description: If a function is differentiable at a point, it must also be continuous there, but continuity does not guarantee differentiability.
Differentiability Implies Continuity.hint: Remember that sharp corners and vertical tangents break differentiability even when the function is continuous.

Tangent Line as Derivative Application.description: The derivative f'(a) gives the slope of the tangent line to the curve at x=a, used in point-slope form.
Tangent Line as Derivative Application.hint: Use the point-slope formula y - f(a) = f'(a)(x - a) with the derivative as the slope.

Velocity as Derivative.description: When position is s(t), the derivative s'(t) represents instantaneous velocity at time t.
Velocity as Derivative.hint: Position's rate of change is velocity; differentiate the position function to find how fast location changes.

Derivative Function Definition.description: The derivative f'(x) is defined as the limit of the difference quotient as h approaches zero for all x where the limit exists.
Derivative Function Definition.hint: Start with the difference quotient [f(x+h)-f(x)]/h and take the limit as h→0, treating x as a variable.

Differentiability on an Interval.description: A function is differentiable on an interval if its derivative exists at every point in that interval.
Differentiability on an Interval.hint: Check whether the derivative limit exists at each point; use one-sided derivatives at endpoints.

Non-Differentiable Points.description: Points where a function has corners, cusps, vertical tangents, or discontinuities are non-differentiable.
Non-Differentiable Points.hint: Look for sharp turns, breaks, or vertical slopes where the left and right derivative limits don't match.

Higher-Order Derivatives.description: The second derivative f''(x) is the derivative of f'(x), and higher derivatives follow by repeated differentiation.
Higher-Order Derivatives.hint: Apply the derivative operation multiple times: differentiate the first derivative to get the second.

Derivative Notation Equivalence.description: The notations f'(x), dy/dx, df/dx, and Dx[f] all represent the same derivative operation.
Derivative Notation Equivalence.hint: Prime notation f'(x) is convenient for functions; Leibniz notation dy/dx emphasizes the rate of change interpretation.

Graphing Derivative Functions.description: The graph of f'(x) shows the slope of f(x) at each point, revealing where f is increasing, decreasing, or has horizontal tangents.
Graphing Derivative Functions.hint: Where f has a horizontal tangent, f' crosses zero; steep sections of f correspond to large absolute values of f'.

Derivative of Power Functions.description: For f(x) = x^n, the derivative is f'(x) = nx^(n-1), derived from the definition using algebraic manipulation.
Derivative of Power Functions.hint: Apply the difference quotient to (x+h)^n, expand using binomial theorem, and cancel the h in the denominator.

Derivative of Constant Functions.description: The derivative of any constant function f(x) = c is zero because there is no change in output.
Derivative of Constant Functions.hint: The difference quotient (c-c)/h equals zero, so the limit is zero regardless of x.

Constant Rule for Derivatives.description: The derivative of a constant function is zero: if f(x) = c, then f'(x) = 0.
Constant Rule for Derivatives.hint: Remember that constants don't change, so their rate of change is always zero.

Power Rule for Derivatives.description: The derivative of x^n is nx^(n-1): d/dx[x^n] = nx^(n-1) for any real number n.
Power Rule for Derivatives.hint: Bring the exponent down as a coefficient, then subtract 1 from the exponent.

Constant Multiple Rule.description: The derivative of a constant times a function equals the constant times the derivative: d/dx[cf(x)] = c·f'(x).
Constant Multiple Rule.hint: Factor out the constant first, then differentiate what remains.

Sum Rule for Derivatives.description: The derivative of a sum equals the sum of derivatives: d/dx[f(x) + g(x)] = f'(x) + g'(x).
Sum Rule for Derivatives.hint: Differentiate each term separately and add the results together.

Difference Rule for Derivatives.description: The derivative of a difference equals the difference of derivatives: d/dx[f(x) - g(x)] = f'(x) - g'(x).
Difference Rule for Derivatives.hint: Differentiate each term separately and subtract the results.

Product Rule for Derivatives.description: The derivative of a product is d/dx[f(x)g(x)] = f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x), not the product of derivatives.
Product Rule for Derivatives.hint: Use the pattern: first times derivative of second, plus second times derivative of first.

Quotient Rule for Derivatives.description: The derivative of a quotient is d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [g(x)f'(x) - f(x)g'(x)]/[g(x)]^2.
Quotient Rule for Derivatives.hint: Remember 'low d-high minus high d-low, over low squared' as a mnemonic.

Combining Differentiation Rules.description: Complex functions require applying multiple differentiation rules in sequence or simultaneously.
Combining Differentiation Rules.hint: Break the problem into parts, identify which rule applies to each part, then combine results.

Derivative of Exponential Functions.description: The derivative of e^x is e^x itself, and d/dx[a^x] = a^x·ln(a) for constant base a.
Derivative of Exponential Functions.hint: The natural exponential e^x is its own derivative; other bases need a ln(a) factor.

Position-Velocity-Acceleration Relations.description: For position function s(t), velocity is v(t)=s'(t) and acceleration is a(t)=v'(t)=s''(t).
Position-Velocity-Acceleration Relations.hint: Take one derivative to get velocity, take another derivative of velocity to get acceleration.

Marginal Cost in Economics.description: The marginal cost C'(x) approximates the cost of producing one additional unit when x units are already produced.
Marginal Cost in Economics.hint: The derivative of the cost function tells you how much one more item will cost to produce.

Population Growth Rate.description: The derivative P'(t) of a population function P(t) gives the instantaneous rate at which the population is changing at time t.
Population Growth Rate.hint: A positive derivative means population is growing; negative means declining; find magnitude for growth rate.

Related Rates Setup.description: When two quantities are related by an equation and both change with time, their derivatives are related through the chain rule.
Related Rates Setup.hint: Differentiate the entire equation with respect to time, treating all variables as functions of time.

Speed versus Velocity Distinction.description: Speed is the absolute value of velocity: speed = |v(t)|, so speed is always non-negative while velocity can be negative.
Speed versus Velocity Distinction.hint: Velocity includes direction (sign matters); speed is just how fast, regardless of direction.

Interpreting Sign of Derivative.description: A positive derivative indicates the function is increasing, negative indicates decreasing, and zero indicates a possible local extremum or inflection point.
Interpreting Sign of Derivative.hint: Check the sign of f'(a) to determine if f is going up or down at x=a.

Units of Derivatives.description: The units of f'(x) are the units of f divided by the units of x, reflecting rate of change interpretation.
Units of Derivatives.hint: If position is in meters and time in seconds, velocity has units meters/second.

Derivative of Sine Function.description: The derivative of sin(x) equals cos(x), proven using the limit definition and trigonometric identities.
Derivative of Sine Function.hint: Use the limit definition with sin(x+h) expanded via angle sum formula, then apply the special limits of sin(h)/h and (cos(h)-1)/h as h approaches zero.

Derivative of Cosine Function.description: The derivative of cos(x) equals -sin(x), proven similarly to sine using limit definition.
Derivative of Cosine Function.hint: Expand cos(x+h) using the angle sum formula, then factor and apply the same special trigonometric limits used for sine.

Special Trigonometric Limits.description: The fundamental limits lim(h→0) sin(h)/h = 1 and lim(h→0) (cos(h)-1)/h = 0 enable derivative proofs.
Special Trigonometric Limits.hint: Memorize these two limits as they are essential for proving all trigonometric derivatives; use the squeeze theorem or geometric arguments to understand why they hold.

Derivative of Tangent Function.description: The derivative of tan(x) equals sec²(x), derived using the quotient rule on sin(x)/cos(x).
Derivative of Tangent Function.hint: Write tan(x) as sin(x)/cos(x) and apply the quotient rule, then simplify using the Pythagorean identity sin²(x) + cos²(x) = 1.

Derivative of Secant Function.description: The derivative of sec(x) equals sec(x)tan(x), found by differentiating 1/cos(x) using the quotient rule.
Derivative of Secant Function.hint: Express sec(x) as 1/cos(x), apply the quotient rule with constant numerator, then rewrite in terms of secant and tangent.

Derivative of Cotangent Function.description: The derivative of cot(x) equals -csc²(x), obtained from differentiating cos(x)/sin(x).
Derivative of Cotangent Function.hint: Write cot(x) as cos(x)/sin(x) and apply the quotient rule, simplifying the result using trigonometric identities.

Derivative of Cosecant Function.description: The derivative of csc(x) equals -csc(x)cot(x), derived from differentiating 1/sin(x).
Derivative of Cosecant Function.hint: Express csc(x) as 1/sin(x) and use the quotient rule, then convert to cosecant and cotangent form.

Higher-Order Trigonometric Derivatives.description: Successive derivatives of trigonometric functions follow cyclic patterns, particularly useful for sine and cosine.
Higher-Order Trigonometric Derivatives.hint: Notice that differentiating sin(x) four times returns to sin(x); use this pattern to find any order derivative quickly.

Composite Function Structure.description: A function formed by applying one function to the result of another, written as f(g(x)) or (f ∘ g)(x).
Composite Function Structure.hint: Identify the 'outer' function f and the 'inner' function g separately before attempting differentiation.

Chain Rule Formula.description: The derivative of a composite function f(g(x)) equals f'(g(x)) · g'(x), the derivative of the outer function evaluated at the inner function times the derivative of the inner function.
Chain Rule Formula.hint: Write out the outer function's derivative first, then multiply by the inner function's derivative as a separate factor.

Identifying Inner and Outer Functions.description: The process of decomposing a composite function into its constituent parts to apply the chain rule correctly.
Identifying Inner and Outer Functions.hint: Ask yourself: what operation happens last when evaluating the function? That's your outer function.

Chain Rule with Power Functions.description: Applying the chain rule when the outer function is a power, giving d/dx[u(x)]^n = n[u(x)]^(n-1) · u'(x).
Chain Rule with Power Functions.hint: Bring down the exponent, reduce it by one, keep the base unchanged, then multiply by the derivative of the base.

Chain Rule with Trigonometric Functions.description: Applying the chain rule when the outer or inner function involves trigonometric expressions like sin(u(x)) or cos(u(x)).
Chain Rule with Trigonometric Functions.hint: Remember the basic trig derivatives, then multiply by the derivative of whatever is inside the trig function.

Multiple Composition Chain Rule.description: Extending the chain rule to functions with three or more nested compositions, requiring repeated application.
Multiple Composition Chain Rule.hint: Work from the outermost function inward, applying the chain rule at each layer and multiplying all derivative factors together.

Chain Rule Combined with Other Rules.description: Using the chain rule together with product rule, quotient rule, or sum rule in a single differentiation problem.
Chain Rule Combined with Other Rules.hint: First determine which rule applies to the overall structure, then use the chain rule on any composite parts within.

Leibniz Notation for Chain Rule.description: Expressing the chain rule as dy/dx = (dy/du)(du/dx), emphasizing the cancellation of differentials.
Leibniz Notation for Chain Rule.hint: The 'du' appears to cancel like fractions, which can help you remember the correct order of multiplication.

Inverse Function Derivative Theorem.description: If f is differentiable at a and f'(a) ≠ 0, then the inverse function g is differentiable at f(a) and g'(f(a)) = 1/f'(a).
Inverse Function Derivative Theorem.hint: The derivative of the inverse is the reciprocal of the derivative of the original function, evaluated at the corresponding point.

Power Functions with Rational Exponents.description: Functions of the form f(x) = x^(p/q) where p and q are integers can be differentiated using the power rule extended to rational exponents.
Power Functions with Rational Exponents.hint: Apply the power rule: bring down the exponent p/q and reduce the exponent by 1.

Derivative of Inverse Sine.description: The derivative of arcsin(x) is 1/√(1-x²) for -1 < x < 1.
Derivative of Inverse Sine.hint: Use the inverse function theorem with sin(y) = x, remembering that cos²(y) + sin²(y) = 1.

Derivative of Inverse Cosine.description: The derivative of arccos(x) is -1/√(1-x²) for -1 < x < 1.
Derivative of Inverse Cosine.hint: Similar to arcsin but with a negative sign because cosine is decreasing on [0, π].

Derivative of Inverse Tangent.description: The derivative of arctan(x) is 1/(1+x²) for all real x.
Derivative of Inverse Tangent.hint: Use the inverse function theorem with tan(y) = x and the identity sec²(y) = 1 + tan²(y).

Chain Rule with Inverse Trigonometric Fu.description: When differentiating compositions like arcsin(u(x)), apply the chain rule: multiply the derivative of arcsin by u'(x).
Chain Rule with Inverse Trigonometric Fu.hint: Find the derivative of the outer inverse trig function, then multiply by the derivative of the inside function.

Implicit versus Explicit Functions.description: An explicit function has y isolated (y = f(x)), while an implicit function has x and y mixed together in an equation (e.g., x² + y² = 25).
Implicit versus Explicit Functions.hint: Check if you can write y = something with only x on the right side; if not, it's implicit.

Implicit Differentiation Technique.description: The method of differentiating both sides of an equation with respect to x while treating y as an implicit function of x.
Implicit Differentiation Technique.hint: Differentiate term-by-term on both sides, remembering that every y term needs the chain rule with dy/dx.

Differentiating y Terms.description: When differentiating terms containing y with respect to x, multiply by dy/dx using the chain rule since y depends on x.
Differentiating y Terms.hint: For any function of y like y², use the chain rule: d/dx[y²] = 2y·(dy/dx).

Solving for dy/dx Algebraically.description: After differentiating implicitly, collect all dy/dx terms on one side and factor them out to solve for dy/dx.
Solving for dy/dx Algebraically.hint: Move all terms with dy/dx to the left, all other terms to the right, then factor out dy/dx and divide.

Implicit Differentiation of Products.description: When differentiating products like xy implicitly, use the product rule and apply dy/dx to y terms.
Implicit Differentiation of Products.hint: For xy, use product rule: d/dx[xy] = x(dy/dx) + y(1) = x(dy/dx) + y.

Tangent Lines to Implicit Curves.description: Finding the slope of a tangent line to an implicitly defined curve by evaluating dy/dx at a specific point.
Tangent Lines to Implicit Curves.hint: First find dy/dx generally, then substitute both x and y coordinates of the point into your expression.

Second Derivatives via Implicit Differen.description: Finding d²y/dx² by implicitly differentiating the expression for dy/dx, treating it as an implicit function.
Second Derivatives via Implicit Differen.hint: After finding dy/dx, differentiate it implicitly with respect to x, then substitute your original dy/dx expression.

Implicit Curves and Relations.description: Equations relating x and y that may not define y as a function of x globally but can be differentiated locally.
Implicit Curves and Relations.hint: Even if the equation fails the vertical line test, you can still find slopes at specific points using implicit differentiation.

Derivative of e^x.description: The derivative of e^x equals itself: d/dx(e^x) = e^x.
Derivative of e^x.hint: This is the unique function that is its own derivative; memorize this fundamental property.
Derivative of e^x.section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Derivative of a^x.description: For any positive constant a, d/dx(a^x) = a^x ln(a).
Derivative of a^x.hint: The natural log of the base appears as a constant factor; when a=e, ln(e)=1 giving the e^x case.
Derivative of a^x.section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Derivative of Natural Logarithm.description: The derivative of ln(x) is 1/x for x > 0.
Derivative of Natural Logarithm.hint: Remember the domain restriction; this formula only works for positive x values.
Derivative of Natural Logarithm.section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Derivative of log_a(x).description: For positive constant a ≠ 1, d/dx(log_a(x)) = 1/(x ln(a)).
Derivative of log_a(x).hint: Convert to natural logarithm using the change of base formula: log_a(x) = ln(x)/ln(a).
Derivative of log_a(x).section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Chain Rule with Exponential Functions.description: For composite exponentials like e^(g(x)), use chain rule: d/dx(e^(g(x))) = e^(g(x)) · g'(x).
Chain Rule with Exponential Functions.hint: First take the derivative of the outer exponential, then multiply by the derivative of the exponent.
Chain Rule with Exponential Functions.section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Chain Rule with Logarithmic Functions.description: For composite logarithms like ln(g(x)), use chain rule: d/dx(ln(g(x))) = g'(x)/g(x).
Chain Rule with Logarithmic Functions.hint: The derivative is the inner function's derivative divided by the inner function itself.
Chain Rule with Logarithmic Functions.section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Logarithmic Differentiation Technique.description: Taking ln of both sides before differentiating simplifies products, quotients, and variable exponents.
Logarithmic Differentiation Technique.hint: Use when you have complicated products or functions like x^x; take ln first, then differentiate implicitly.
Logarithmic Differentiation Technique.section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Derivative of Variable Base and Exponent.description: For f(x)^(g(x)), rewrite as e^(g(x)ln(f(x))) then differentiate using chain rule.
Derivative of Variable Base and Exponent.hint: When both base and exponent contain x, use the exponential-logarithm identity before differentiating.
Derivative of Variable Base and Exponent.section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Related Rates Problem Structure.description: A problem where multiple quantities change over time and are connected by an equation, requiring finding one rate given others.
Related Rates Problem Structure.hint: Identify all changing quantities, write an equation relating them, then differentiate both sides with respect to time.

Implicit Time Differentiation.description: Differentiating both sides of an equation with respect to time t, treating variables as functions of t.
Implicit Time Differentiation.hint: Use the chain rule on every variable that changes with time, writing dx/dt, dy/dt, etc.

Related Rates Solution Strategy.description: A systematic five-step approach: sketch, identify variables, write relating equation, differentiate with respect to time, substitute known values.
Related Rates Solution Strategy.hint: Always differentiate the equation before substituting numerical values to avoid losing variable relationships.

Geometric Related Rates.description: Related rates problems involving changing geometric quantities like radius, volume, area, or side lengths of shapes.
Geometric Related Rates.hint: Draw a clear diagram labeling all changing quantities, then use geometric formulas like V = (4/3)πr³ or A = πr².

Pythagorean Related Rates.description: Related rates problems using the Pythagorean theorem to relate changing distances or positions.
Pythagorean Related Rates.hint: If objects move at right angles or distances form a right triangle, write x² + y² = z² and differentiate.

Similar Triangles in Related Rates.description: Using proportional relationships from similar triangles to eliminate variables in related rates problems.
Similar Triangles in Related Rates.hint: If triangles remain similar as they change, write a proportion to express one variable in terms of another before differentiating.

Sign Interpretation in Related Rates.description: Determining whether rates are positive or negative based on whether quantities are increasing or decreasing.
Sign Interpretation in Related Rates.hint: If a quantity is decreasing, its rate of change is negative; use the correct sign when substituting known rates.

Rate Units and Consistency.description: Ensuring all rates and measurements use compatible units (e.g., meters and meters per second, not mixed with centimeters).
Rate Units and Consistency.hint: Convert all given quantities to consistent units before starting calculations to avoid errors.

Tangent Line as Linear Approximation.description: The tangent line at point (a, f(a)) approximates function values near x = a using L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a).
Tangent Line as Linear Approximation.hint: Start by finding f(a) and f'(a) at your point, then plug into the linearization formula.

Linearization Function L(x).description: The linear function L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a) that best approximates f(x) near x = a.
Linearization Function L(x).hint: Evaluate both the function and its derivative at the base point a before constructing L(x).

Approximation Error Analysis.description: The difference |f(x) - L(x)| between actual function values and linear approximation increases as x moves away from a.
Approximation Error Analysis.hint: Check how far x is from your base point a; closer means better approximation.

Differential dy Definition.description: The differential dy = f'(x)dx represents the change in the tangent line when the independent variable changes by dx.
Differential dy Definition.hint: Think of dy as the rise along the tangent line, while dx is the horizontal change.

Differential dx as Independent Variable.description: The differential dx represents an arbitrary small change in the independent variable x and equals Δx.
Differential dx as Independent Variable.hint: Choose dx to match your actual change in x, then compute dy using the derivative.

Differential versus Actual Change.description: The differential dy approximates the actual change Δy = f(x + dx) - f(x) in function values.
Differential versus Actual Change.hint: Calculate dy = f'(x)dx first, then compare to Δy by evaluating f at both points.

Propagated Error Estimation.description: Using differentials to estimate how measurement errors in input values affect calculated output values.
Propagated Error Estimation.hint: Identify the measurement error as dx, then use dy = f'(x)dx to find the propagated error.

Relative and Percent Error.description: Relative error is dy/y and percent error is (dy/y) × 100%, measuring error as a fraction of the quantity.
Relative and Percent Error.hint: Divide your differential error by the actual function value to get relative error.

Absolute Extrema Definition.description: A function has an absolute maximum at c if f(c) ≥ f(x) for all x in the domain, and absolute minimum if f(c) ≤ f(x) for all x.
Absolute Extrema Definition.hint: Compare the function value at c to ALL other points in the domain, not just nearby points.

Local Extrema Definition.description: A function has a local maximum at c if f(c) ≥ f(x) for all x near c, and local minimum if f(c) ≤ f(x) for all x near c.
Local Extrema Definition.hint: Local extrema only compare to nearby points within some small interval around c.

Critical Point Identification.description: A critical point occurs where f'(c) = 0 or f'(c) does not exist, and c is in the domain of f.
Critical Point Identification.hint: Find where the derivative equals zero OR where the derivative is undefined but the function is defined.

Fermat's Theorem on Local Extrema.description: If f has a local extremum at c and f is differentiable at c, then f'(c) = 0.
Fermat's Theorem on Local Extrema.hint: Every local max or min where the derivative exists must be a critical point, but not every critical point is an extremum.

Extreme Value Theorem.description: A continuous function on a closed interval [a,b] must attain both an absolute maximum and absolute minimum on that interval.
Extreme Value Theorem.hint: Check endpoints and critical points in [a,b] to find where the absolute extrema occur.

Closed Interval Method.description: To find absolute extrema on [a,b]: evaluate f at critical points and endpoints, then compare values to identify max and min.
Closed Interval Method.hint: Make a list of all candidates (critical points plus endpoints), evaluate f at each, then pick the largest and smallest values.

Endpoint Evaluation Strategy.description: When finding extrema on closed intervals, always evaluate the function at the boundary points a and b in addition to interior critical points.
Endpoint Evaluation Strategy.hint: Absolute extrema on closed intervals can occur at endpoints even when the derivative is nonzero there.

Rolle's Theorem Statement.description: If f is continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), and f(a)=f(b), then there exists c in (a,b) where f'(c)=0.
Rolle's Theorem Statement.hint: Check all three conditions: continuity on closed interval, differentiability on open interval, and equal endpoint values.

Mean Value Theorem Statement.description: If f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), then there exists c in (a,b) where f'(c) equals the average rate of change (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a).
Mean Value Theorem Statement.hint: The instantaneous rate at some point c equals the average rate over the entire interval—draw the secant line and look for a parallel tangent.

MVT Hypothesis Verification.description: The process of checking continuity and differentiability conditions before applying the Mean Value Theorem.
MVT Hypothesis Verification.hint: Explicitly verify continuity on the closed interval and differentiability on the open interval before concluding MVT applies.

Finding c in MVT.description: Solving the equation f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a) for the specific value(s) of c guaranteed by the Mean Value Theorem.
Finding c in MVT.hint: Set the derivative equal to the slope of the secant line, then solve for c and verify it lies in the open interval (a,b).

Corollary: Zero Derivative Implies Const.description: If f'(x)=0 for all x in an interval, then f is constant on that interval.
Corollary: Zero Derivative Implies Const.hint: Apply MVT to any two points: if f'(c)=0 always, then (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)=0, so f(b)=f(a) for all pairs.

Corollary: Same Derivative Implies Diffe.description: If f'(x)=g'(x) for all x in an interval, then f(x)=g(x)+C for some constant C.
Corollary: Same Derivative Implies Diffe.hint: Consider the function h(x)=f(x)-g(x) and show its derivative is zero everywhere.

MVT Geometric Interpretation.description: The Mean Value Theorem guarantees at least one point where the tangent line is parallel to the secant line connecting the endpoints.
MVT Geometric Interpretation.hint: Visualize the secant line from (a,f(a)) to (b,f(b)); MVT says there's a point where the tangent has this same slope.

MVT Application to Inequalities.description: Using the Mean Value Theorem to establish bounds or inequalities involving function values based on derivative constraints.
MVT Application to Inequalities.hint: If you know bounds on f'(x), use MVT to write f(b)-f(a)=f'(c)(b-a) and substitute the bounds.

First Derivative Test for Increasing/Dec.description: If f'(x) > 0 on an interval, then f is increasing there; if f'(x) < 0, then f is decreasing there.
First Derivative Test for Increasing/Dec.hint: Find where the derivative is positive or negative by testing values in intervals between critical points.
First Derivative Test for Increasing/Dec.section: 4.5 Derivatives and the Shape of a Graph

First Derivative Test for Local Extrema.description: At a critical point c, if f' changes from positive to negative, f has a local maximum; if f' changes from negative to positive, f has a local minimum.
First Derivative Test for Local Extrema.hint: Check the sign of f' on both sides of the critical point to see if the function changes from increasing to decreasing or vice versa.
First Derivative Test for Local Extrema.section: 4.5 Derivatives and the Shape of a Graph

Concavity from Second Derivative.description: If f''(x) > 0 on an interval, f is concave up there; if f''(x) < 0, f is concave down there.
Concavity from Second Derivative.hint: Calculate the second derivative and determine its sign on intervals between inflection point candidates.
Concavity from Second Derivative.section: 4.5 Derivatives and the Shape of a Graph

Inflection Point Definition.description: A point where the concavity of f changes from up to down or down to up.
Inflection Point Definition.hint: Find where f'' changes sign, and verify that f is continuous at that point.
Inflection Point Definition.section: 4.5 Derivatives and the Shape of a Graph

Second Derivative Test for Local Extrema.description: At a critical point c where f'(c) = 0, if f''(c) > 0 then f has a local minimum; if f''(c) < 0 then f has a local maximum.
Second Derivative Test for Local Extrema.hint: After finding critical points with f'(c) = 0, evaluate f''(c) to quickly classify the extremum without checking sign changes.
Second Derivative Test for Local Extrema.section: 4.5 Derivatives and the Shape of a Graph

Sign Chart for Derivatives.description: A number line showing where a derivative is positive, negative, or zero to analyze function behavior.
Sign Chart for Derivatives.hint: Mark critical points and discontinuities on a number line, then test the derivative's sign in each resulting interval.
Sign Chart for Derivatives.section: 4.5 Derivatives and the Shape of a Graph

Curve Sketching Strategy.description: A systematic approach using derivatives to sketch graphs by finding critical points, intervals of increase/decrease, concavity, and inflection points.
Curve Sketching Strategy.hint: Work through steps: find domain, intercepts, asymptotes, critical points, f' analysis, f'' analysis, then sketch.
Curve Sketching Strategy.section: 4.5 Derivatives and the Shape of a Graph

Limit at Infinity Definition.description: The value L that f(x) approaches as x approaches positive or negative infinity, written as lim(x→∞) f(x) = L or lim(x→-∞) f(x) = L.
Limit at Infinity Definition.hint: Evaluate what happens to f(x) as x gets arbitrarily large (positive or negative) by analyzing dominant terms.

Horizontal Asymptote y=L.description: A horizontal line y = L that the graph of f(x) approaches as x approaches positive or negative infinity.
Horizontal Asymptote y=L.hint: Find limits at infinity; if they exist and equal L, then y = L is a horizontal asymptote.

End Behavior of Rational Functions.description: The behavior of rational functions as x approaches infinity, determined by comparing degrees of numerator and denominator.
End Behavior of Rational Functions.hint: Compare degrees: if numerator degree equals denominator degree, divide leading coefficients for the horizontal asymptote.

Infinite Limit at a Point.description: When f(x) approaches positive or negative infinity as x approaches a finite value a, written as lim(x→a) f(x) = ±∞.
Infinite Limit at a Point.hint: Check if denominator approaches zero while numerator remains nonzero; determine sign from one-sided limits.

Vertical Asymptote x=a.description: A vertical line x = a where f(x) approaches positive or negative infinity as x approaches a from one or both sides.
Vertical Asymptote x=a.hint: Find values where denominator equals zero but numerator doesn't; verify the function is unbounded there.

Limit Laws at Infinity.description: Extended limit laws that apply when x approaches infinity, including properties for sums, products, and quotients of limits.
Limit Laws at Infinity.hint: Apply standard limit laws treating infinity as the limit point; divide by highest power of x in rational expressions.

Asymptotic Behavior of Exponentials.description: Exponential functions e^x grow faster than any polynomial as x→∞, while e^(-x) approaches zero.
Asymptotic Behavior of Exponentials.hint: Remember e^x dominates polynomials for large x; e^(-x) decays to zero faster than any polynomial grows.

Indeterminate Forms at Infinity.description: Expressions like ∞/∞ or ∞-∞ that require algebraic manipulation or L'Hôpital's Rule to evaluate limits at infinity.
Indeterminate Forms at Infinity.hint: For ∞/∞ forms in rational functions, divide numerator and denominator by the highest power of x present.

Optimization Problem Setup.description: Process of identifying the quantity to optimize and the constraints from a word problem.
Optimization Problem Setup.hint: Start by clearly labeling what you want to maximize or minimize, then list all given constraints.

Primary and Secondary Equations.description: The objective function (to optimize) and constraint equations (relating variables) in an optimization problem.
Primary and Secondary Equations.hint: Write one equation for what you're optimizing and separate equations for geometric or physical constraints.

Single Variable Reduction.description: Using constraint equations to express the objective function in terms of one independent variable.
Single Variable Reduction.hint: Solve a constraint equation for one variable and substitute into your objective function to eliminate it.

Domain from Physical Constraints.description: Determining the valid interval for the independent variable based on real-world limitations.
Domain from Physical Constraints.hint: Consider what values make physical sense: lengths must be positive, angles have bounds, etc.

Critical Point Optimization.description: Finding optimal values by setting the derivative equal to zero and testing critical points.
Critical Point Optimization.hint: After finding critical points, check endpoints too if your domain is a closed interval.

Endpoint versus Interior Extrema.description: Comparing function values at critical points and interval endpoints to find the absolute extremum.
Endpoint versus Interior Extrema.hint: Always evaluate your function at boundary values of the domain, not just where the derivative is zero.

Geometric Optimization Problems.description: Applications involving maximizing or minimizing area, volume, perimeter, or distance using geometric relationships.
Geometric Optimization Problems.hint: Draw a diagram and label all quantities; use formulas like area = length × width or volume = πr²h.

Business and Economics Optimization.description: Applications involving cost, revenue, or profit functions where marginal analysis uses derivatives.
Business and Economics Optimization.hint: Revenue = price × quantity; Profit = Revenue - Cost; maximize profit by setting its derivative to zero.

Indeterminate Form 0/0.description: A limit that approaches 0/0, requiring further analysis beyond direct substitution.
Indeterminate Form 0/0.hint: Check if both numerator and denominator approach zero independently at the limit point.

Indeterminate Form \u221e/\u221e.description: A limit that approaches ∞/∞, requiring techniques beyond basic limit laws.
Indeterminate Form \u221e/\u221e.hint: Verify both numerator and denominator grow without bound in the same direction.

L'H\u00f4pital's Rule for 0/0.description: If lim f(x)/g(x) gives 0/0, then it equals lim f'(x)/g'(x) if conditions are met.
L'H\u00f4pital's Rule for 0/0.hint: Differentiate numerator and denominator separately, not as a quotient rule.

L'H\u00f4pital's Rule for \u221e/\u221e.description: If lim f(x)/g(x) gives ∞/∞, then it equals lim f'(x)/g'(x) if conditions are met.
L'H\u00f4pital's Rule for \u221e/\u221e.hint: Apply the rule repeatedly if needed, checking for indeterminate form each time.

Indeterminate Products 0\u00b7\u221e.description: A limit of the form 0·∞ that requires rewriting as a quotient before applying L'Hôpital's Rule.
Indeterminate Products 0\u00b7\u221e.hint: Convert to 0/0 or ∞/∞ by rewriting as a fraction with one factor in the denominator.

Indeterminate Differences \u221e\u2212\u.description: A limit of the form ∞−∞ requiring algebraic manipulation to create a quotient form.
Indeterminate Differences \u221e\u2212\u.hint: Find a common denominator or use conjugate multiplication to eliminate the difference.

Indeterminate Powers 0\u2070, 1^\u221e,.description: Indeterminate forms involving exponents, solved using logarithms to convert to quotients.
Indeterminate Powers 0\u2070, 1^\u221e,.hint: Take the natural logarithm of the expression first, then apply L'Hôpital's Rule to the exponent.

Growth Rate Comparison via L'H\u00f4pita.description: Using L'Hôpital's Rule to compare relative growth rates of functions as x approaches infinity.
Growth Rate Comparison via L'H\u00f4pita.hint: Exponentials grow faster than polynomials, which grow faster than logarithms.

Newton's Method Iterative Formula.description: The formula x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n) that generates successive approximations to a root of f(x)=0.
Newton's Method Iterative Formula.hint: Start with an initial guess x_0, then repeatedly apply the formula using both the function value and its derivative at each step.

Tangent Line Approximation for Roots.description: Newton's method finds where the tangent line at (x_n, f(x_n)) crosses the x-axis to get the next approximation x_{n+1}.
Tangent Line Approximation for Roots.hint: Draw the tangent line at your current point and see where it hits the x-axis — that's your next guess.

Initial Guess Selection.description: Choosing a starting value x_0 close to the actual root to ensure Newton's method converges.
Initial Guess Selection.hint: Graph the function or evaluate it at a few points to find where it changes sign, then pick a nearby starting value.

Newton's Method Convergence Conditions.description: Newton's method converges when the initial guess is sufficiently close to a root and f'(x) is nonzero near the root.
Newton's Method Convergence Conditions.hint: Check that your starting point isn't near a horizontal tangent (where f'≈0) or far from the actual root.

Newton's Method Failure Cases.description: Situations where Newton's method fails or diverges, including f'(x_n)=0, poor initial guess, or cycling between values.
Newton's Method Failure Cases.hint: If your derivative equals zero at some iteration, the method breaks down — try a different starting point.

Iterative Root-Finding Process.description: The systematic procedure of repeatedly applying Newton's formula until successive approximations differ by less than a tolerance.
Iterative Root-Finding Process.hint: Keep calculating new x values until |x_{n+1} - x_n| is smaller than your desired accuracy threshold.

Convergence Rate of Newton's Method.description: Newton's method typically exhibits quadratic convergence, meaning the number of correct digits approximately doubles with each iteration.
Convergence Rate of Newton's Method.hint: Once you're reasonably close to the root, expect the decimal accuracy to improve dramatically with each step.

Applied Newton's Method Problems.description: Using Newton's method to approximate roots of equations arising from real-world problems in physics, engineering, or optimization.
Applied Newton's Method Problems.hint: First set up your equation as f(x)=0, compute f'(x), choose a reasonable starting guess, then iterate.

Antiderivative Definition.description: A function F is an antiderivative of f if F'(x) = f(x) for all x in the domain.
Antiderivative Definition.hint: To verify an antiderivative, take its derivative and check if you get back the original function.

Family of Antiderivatives.description: The general antiderivative of f is F(x) + C where F is any particular antiderivative and C is an arbitrary constant.
Family of Antiderivatives.hint: Remember that two functions with the same derivative differ only by a constant, so add +C to every antiderivative.

Power Rule for Antiderivatives.description: The antiderivative of x^n is (x^(n+1))/(n+1) + C for n ≠ -1.
Power Rule for Antiderivatives.hint: Increase the exponent by 1, then divide by the new exponent; don't forget +C at the end.

Antiderivatives of Exponential Functions.description: The antiderivative of e^x is e^x + C, and the antiderivative of a^x is a^x/ln(a) + C.
Antiderivatives of Exponential Functions.hint: For e^x, the antiderivative is itself; for other bases a^x, divide by ln(a).

Antiderivatives of Trigonometric Functio.description: Basic antiderivatives include ∫sin(x)dx = -cos(x) + C and ∫cos(x)dx = sin(x) + C.
Antiderivatives of Trigonometric Functio.hint: Remember sin integrates to negative cosine, and cosine integrates to positive sine.

Antiderivative of 1/x.description: The antiderivative of 1/x is ln|x| + C for x ≠ 0.
Antiderivative of 1/x.hint: This is the special case where the power rule fails (n = -1); use natural logarithm with absolute value.

Initial Value Problems.description: Finding a specific antiderivative by using a given point (x₀, y₀) to determine the constant C.
Initial Value Problems.hint: First find the general antiderivative with +C, then substitute the given point to solve for C.

Rectilinear Motion from Acceleration.description: Finding velocity and position functions by antidifferentiating acceleration a(t), using initial conditions.
Rectilinear Motion from Acceleration.hint: Antidifferentiate acceleration to get velocity, then antidifferentiate velocity to get position; use initial conditions to find both constants.

Riemann Sum Definition.description: A sum of rectangular areas that approximates the area under a curve by dividing the region into subintervals.
Riemann Sum Definition.hint: Start by dividing your interval into equal parts and decide where to sample the height in each subinterval.

Left Endpoint Approximation.description: A Riemann sum using the left endpoint of each subinterval to determine rectangle height.
Left Endpoint Approximation.hint: Use f(x₀), f(x₁), ..., f(xₙ₋₁) as your heights and multiply each by the width Δx.

Right Endpoint Approximation.description: A Riemann sum using the right endpoint of each subinterval to determine rectangle height.
Right Endpoint Approximation.hint: Use f(x₁), f(x₂), ..., f(xₙ) as your heights and multiply each by the width Δx.

Midpoint Rule Approximation.description: A Riemann sum using the midpoint of each subinterval to determine rectangle height.
Midpoint Rule Approximation.hint: For each subinterval [xᵢ, xᵢ₊₁], evaluate f at (xᵢ + xᵢ₊₁)/2 to get better accuracy than endpoints.

Sigma Notation for Sums.description: The compact notation Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ f(xᵢ)Δx for representing Riemann sums as summations.
Sigma Notation for Sums.hint: Identify the pattern in your sum, then write it as Σ with clear limits and index variable.

Partition Refinement.description: The process of increasing the number of subintervals to improve area approximation accuracy.
Partition Refinement.hint: As n increases and Δx decreases, your rectangular approximation becomes more accurate.

Upper and Lower Sums.description: Riemann sums using maximum and minimum function values on each subinterval to bound the true area.
Upper and Lower Sums.hint: For upper sum use the highest point in each subinterval; for lower sum use the lowest point.

Limiting Process for Exact Area.description: Taking the limit as n→∞ of a Riemann sum to obtain the exact area under a curve.
Limiting Process for Exact Area.hint: Set up your Riemann sum formula first, then take lim(n→∞) to transition from approximation to exact value.

Definite Integral Definition.description: The definite integral of f from a to b is the limit of Riemann sums as partition size approaches zero, written ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx.
Definite Integral Definition.hint: Start with Riemann sums and imagine making the rectangles infinitely thin—the limit is the definite integral.

Integrable Functions Criterion.description: A function is integrable on [a,b] if the limit of Riemann sums exists and is the same for all choices of sample points.
Integrable Functions Criterion.hint: Check if upper and lower sums approach the same value as partition size decreases.

Properties of Definite Integrals.description: Definite integrals satisfy linearity: ∫(cf)dx = c∫f dx, ∫(f±g)dx = ∫f dx ± ∫g dx, and additivity over intervals.
Properties of Definite Integrals.hint: Split complicated integrals using constant multiples and sums, or break the interval into pieces.

Geometric Interpretation of Definite Int.description: The definite integral represents signed area: positive where f(x)>0, negative where f(x)<0.
Geometric Interpretation of Definite Int.hint: Draw the curve and shade regions above and below the x-axis separately, tracking signs.

Comparison Properties of Integrals.description: If f(x)≤g(x) on [a,b], then ∫ₐᵇ f dx ≤ ∫ₐᵇ g dx; useful for estimation.
Comparison Properties of Integrals.hint: Find simpler functions that bound your integrand above and below to estimate the integral.

Average Value of a Function.description: The average value of f on [a,b] is (1/(b-a))∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx, representing the constant height with same area.
Average Value of a Function.hint: Divide the definite integral by the width of the interval to find average height.

Integral Notation and Dummy Variables.description: In ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx, x is a dummy variable; ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = ∫ₐᵇ f(t)dt produces the same number.
Integral Notation and Dummy Variables.hint: The variable of integration can be renamed freely—only the function and endpoints matter.

Definite Integral of Constant Function.description: For constant c, ∫ₐᵇ c dx = c(b-a), the area of a rectangle.
Definite Integral of Constant Function.hint: The integral of a constant is just that constant times the width of the interval.

Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 1.description: If f is continuous on [a,b], then the function g(x) = ∫[a to x] f(t)dt is differentiable and g'(x) = f(x).
Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 1.hint: Remember that taking the derivative of an accumulation function returns the original integrand—differentiation undoes integration.

Accumulation Function.description: A function defined as the integral from a constant to a variable upper limit, A(x) = ∫[a to x] f(t)dt.
Accumulation Function.hint: Think of this as accumulating area under the curve f as x increases from the starting point a.

Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 2.description: If F is an antiderivative of f on [a,b], then ∫[a to b] f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a).
Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 2.hint: To evaluate a definite integral, find any antiderivative F, then compute the difference F(b) - F(a).

Net Change Theorem.description: The definite integral of a rate of change equals the net change in the quantity over the interval.
Net Change Theorem.hint: If F'(x) represents velocity, then ∫[a to b] F'(x)dx gives the displacement from time a to time b.

Evaluation Notation F(x)|[a to b].description: The notation F(x)|[a to b] or [F(x)]^b_a represents F(b) - F(a) for evaluating definite integrals.
Evaluation Notation F(x)|[a to b].hint: Always substitute the upper limit first, then subtract the value at the lower limit.

Chain Rule with Accumulation Functions.description: When differentiating ∫[a to g(x)] f(t)dt with variable upper limit g(x), apply the chain rule: d/dx = f(g(x))·g'(x).
Chain Rule with Accumulation Functions.hint: First apply FTC Part 1 to get f(g(x)), then multiply by the derivative of the upper limit g'(x).

Variable Lower Limit in Accumulation.description: For ∫[h(x) to b] f(t)dt with variable lower limit, rewrite as -∫[b to h(x)] f(t)dt before differentiating.
Variable Lower Limit in Accumulation.hint: Use the property that ∫[a to b] = -∫[b to a] to convert variable lower limits to upper limits.

Total Area versus Net Area.description: Net area accounts for regions below the x-axis as negative, while total area uses absolute value |f(x)| to count all regions positively.
Total Area versus Net Area.hint: If the function crosses the x-axis, split the integral at zeros and take absolute values of each piece for total area.

Basic Integration Formulas.description: Collection of standard antiderivative formulas including power, exponential, and trigonometric functions applied to definite integrals.
Basic Integration Formulas.hint: Apply the Fundamental Theorem Part 2: integrate using antiderivative formulas, then substitute limits.
Basic Integration Formulas.section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

Integration of Even Functions.description: For even functions f(-x)=f(x), the integral from -a to a equals twice the integral from 0 to a.
Integration of Even Functions.hint: Check if f(-x) equals f(x), then use symmetry to compute only from 0 to a and double the result.
Integration of Even Functions.section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

Integration of Odd Functions.description: For odd functions f(-x)=-f(x), the integral from -a to a equals zero due to symmetric cancellation.
Integration of Odd Functions.hint: Verify f(-x)=-f(x) over a symmetric interval; the positive and negative areas cancel exactly.
Integration of Odd Functions.section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

Net Change from Rate.description: The definite integral of a rate function F'(t) from a to b gives the net change F(b)-F(a) in quantity F.
Net Change from Rate.hint: Identify what quantity is changing, then integrate its rate of change over the time interval.
Net Change from Rate.section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

Displacement from Velocity.description: The integral of velocity v(t) over time gives net displacement (final position minus initial position).
Displacement from Velocity.hint: Integrate velocity to find displacement; this is net change, not total distance traveled.
Displacement from Velocity.section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

Total Distance from Velocity.description: The integral of |v(t)| gives total distance traveled, accounting for direction changes.
Total Distance from Velocity.hint: Split the integral at points where velocity changes sign, then integrate absolute values separately.
Total Distance from Velocity.section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

Net Change in Physical Quantities.description: Applications of net change theorem to problems involving mass, population, volume, or other accumulating quantities.
Net Change in Physical Quantities.hint: Set up the integral of the rate function with correct units; result has units of the accumulated quantity.
Net Change in Physical Quantities.section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

Symmetry Properties in Integration.description: Using function symmetry (even/odd) to simplify integrals over symmetric intervals centered at origin.
Symmetry Properties in Integration.hint: Before computing, check for symmetry about the y-axis (even) or origin (odd) to reduce calculation work.
Symmetry Properties in Integration.section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

u-Substitution Method.description: A technique to simplify integrals by substituting u for a function inside a composite function, transforming the integral into a simpler form.
u-Substitution Method.hint: Choose u as the inner function of a composition, then express dx in terms of du using the derivative.

Choosing u in Substitution.description: The selection of an appropriate substitution variable u to simplify an integral, typically the inner function of a composite or a function whose derivative appears.
Choosing u in Substitution.hint: Look for a function whose derivative (or a constant multiple) appears elsewhere in the integrand.

du/dx Relationship.description: The differential relationship du = (du/dx)dx used to transform dx into du in substitution, derived from the derivative of u with respect to x.
du/dx Relationship.hint: After choosing u, differentiate it to find du/dx, then solve for dx in terms of du.

Substitution in Definite Integrals.description: Applying u-substitution to definite integrals by either changing the limits of integration to u-values or substituting back before evaluating.
Substitution in Definite Integrals.hint: When substituting in definite integrals, transform both limits using u = g(x), or revert to x before applying bounds.

Changing Limits of Integration.description: The process of converting integration bounds from x-values to u-values when performing substitution on definite integrals.
Changing Limits of Integration.hint: Substitute the original x-limits into your u-function to get the new u-limits.

Recognizing Composite Function Integrand.description: Identifying when an integrand contains a composite function f(g(x)) where the derivative g'(x) also appears, making it suitable for substitution.
