Recognizing Composite Function Integrand.hint: Look for patterns like f(g(x))·g'(x) where the derivative of the inner function is present as a factor.

Back-Substitution After Integration.description: The step of replacing u with the original expression in terms of x after integrating with respect to u in indefinite integrals.
Back-Substitution After Integration.hint: After integrating in terms of u, substitute back using your original u = g(x) equation before adding the constant.

Substitution with Adjusting Constants.description: The technique of multiplying and dividing by constants to match the exact form needed after substitution when du differs from the differential in the integrand by a constant factor.
Substitution with Adjusting Constants.hint: If du = k·f(x)dx but you have f(x)dx, factor out 1/k outside the integral and use k·f(x)dx = du inside.

Integral of e^x.description: The antiderivative of e^x is e^x plus a constant, making exponential base e particularly convenient for integration.
Integral of e^x.hint: Remember that e^x is its own derivative, so it's also its own antiderivative.
Integral of e^x.section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Integral of a^x.description: The antiderivative of a^x (where a > 0, a ≠ 1) is a^x/ln(a) plus a constant.
Integral of a^x.hint: Convert a^x to e^(x·ln(a)) first, then use substitution with u = x·ln(a) to derive the formula.
Integral of a^x.section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Integral of 1/x.description: The antiderivative of 1/x is ln|x| plus a constant, where absolute value ensures the domain includes negative x.
Integral of 1/x.hint: Always include absolute value bars: ln|x|, not just ln(x), to handle negative domains.
Integral of 1/x.section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Substitution with Exponential Functions.description: Using u-substitution when the integrand contains exponential expressions, often where u equals the exponent.
Substitution with Exponential Functions.hint: Let u be the exponent in e^(something), then find du and replace all x-terms.
Substitution with Exponential Functions.section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Substitution with Logarithmic Functions.description: Using u-substitution when the integrand contains logarithmic expressions, often letting u = ln(x) or the argument of ln.
Substitution with Logarithmic Functions.hint: If you see ln(x) in the integrand, try u = ln(x) so du = 1/x dx, which often appears nearby.
Substitution with Logarithmic Functions.section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Integrals Yielding Logarithmic Functions.description: Recognizing integrands of the form f'(x)/f(x) which integrate to ln|f(x)| plus a constant.
Integrals Yielding Logarithmic Functions.hint: When you see a fraction with the derivative of the denominator in the numerator, the answer involves ln of the denominator.
Integrals Yielding Logarithmic Functions.section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Exponential Growth and Decay Integrals.description: Evaluating definite integrals of exponential functions that model growth or decay processes over a time interval.
Exponential Growth and Decay Integrals.hint: For ke^(rt), factor out constants first, then integrate e^(rt) using the formula for exponential integrals.
Exponential Growth and Decay Integrals.section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Combining Exponential and Algebraic Term.description: Integrating expressions that multiply exponential functions with polynomial or algebraic terms, requiring substitution or pattern recognition.
Combining Exponential and Algebraic Term.hint: Look for whether the algebraic part is the derivative of the exponent, which signals u-substitution.
Combining Exponential and Algebraic Term.section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

Integral of 1/\u221a(a\u00b2-x\u00b2).description: The integral ∫1/√(a²-x²)dx equals arcsin(x/a) + C, deriving from the derivative of inverse sine.
Integral of 1/\u221a(a\u00b2-x\u00b2).hint: Recognize the pattern a²-x² under the square root and check if the numerator is constant 1.
Integral of 1/\u221a(a\u00b2-x\u00b2).section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

Integral of 1/(a\u00b2+x\u00b2).description: The integral ∫1/(a²+x²)dx equals (1/a)arctan(x/a) + C, deriving from the derivative of inverse tangent.
Integral of 1/(a\u00b2+x\u00b2).hint: Look for a sum of squares in the denominator and factor out a² to match the standard form.
Integral of 1/(a\u00b2+x\u00b2).section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

Integral of 1/(x\u221a(x\u00b2-a\u00b2)).description: The integral ∫1/(x√(x²-a²))dx equals (1/a)arcsec(|x|/a) + C, deriving from the derivative of inverse secant.
Integral of 1/(x\u221a(x\u00b2-a\u00b2)).hint: Notice the x²-a² pattern under the square root with x in the denominator, indicating arcsecant form.
Integral of 1/(x\u221a(x\u00b2-a\u00b2)).section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

Completing the Square for Integration.description: Rewriting quadratic expressions ax²+bx+c in the form a(x-h)²+k to match inverse trigonometric integral patterns.
Completing the Square for Integration.hint: Complete the square to transform the quadratic into a form matching a²±x² patterns.
Completing the Square for Integration.section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

Algebraic Manipulation for Inverse Trig.description: Factoring constants and rearranging expressions to match standard inverse trigonometric integral forms.
Algebraic Manipulation for Inverse Trig.hint: Factor out constants from denominators to isolate a²±x² patterns before integrating.
Algebraic Manipulation for Inverse Trig.section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

Inverse Trigonometric Integration with u.description: Combining substitution with inverse trigonometric formulas when the integrand involves composite functions.
Inverse Trigonometric Integration with u.hint: After substitution, check if the result matches one of the three basic inverse trig integral forms.
Inverse Trigonometric Integration with u.section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

Domain Restrictions in Inverse Trig Inte.description: Understanding when integrals yielding inverse trig functions are valid based on domain constraints like |x|<a or |x|>a.
Domain Restrictions in Inverse Trig Inte.hint: Check that x values satisfy the domain requirements (e.g., a²-x²>0) before applying the formula.
Domain Restrictions in Inverse Trig Inte.section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

Choosing Between Inverse Trig Forms.description: Identifying which inverse trigonometric function (arcsin, arctan, or arcsec) applies based on the integrand structure.
Choosing Between Inverse Trig Forms.hint: Look at whether you have a difference under a square root (arcsin), a sum of squares (arctan), or a product with square root (arcsec).
Choosing Between Inverse Trig Forms.section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

Area Between Two Curves.description: The region bounded by two functions f(x) and g(x) over an interval [a,b] is found by integrating the difference of the functions.
Area Between Two Curves.hint: Identify which function is on top and which is on bottom, then integrate (top - bottom)dx from a to b.

Top Function Minus Bottom Function.description: For vertical strips, the integrand is the upper curve minus the lower curve: ∫[f(x) - g(x)]dx where f(x) ≥ g(x).
Top Function Minus Bottom Function.hint: Sketch both curves to visually determine which function has larger y-values in your interval.

Finding Intersection Points.description: Setting f(x) = g(x) and solving determines where curves meet, providing integration limits or partition points.
Finding Intersection Points.hint: Set the two functions equal and solve algebraically or graphically to find x-values where curves cross.

Splitting Integration at Crossover Point.description: When curves cross within [a,b], split the integral into subintervals where one function stays consistently above the other.
Splitting Integration at Crossover Point.hint: Break your integral at each intersection point and reverse which function is on top in each piece.

Horizontal Strip Integration.description: For regions better described by x as a function of y, integrate with respect to y using ∫[g(y) - h(y)]dy where g(y) is rightmost.
Horizontal Strip Integration.hint: If solving for x is easier than solving for y, consider switching to dy and integrating along horizontal strips.

Area Between Curve and x-axis.description: A special case where g(x) = 0, giving area = ∫|f(x)|dx to ensure positive area values.
Area Between Curve and x-axis.hint: This reduces to finding the definite integral, but remember to account for regions where f(x) is negative.

Deciding Between dx and dy Integration.description: Choose integration variable based on which orientation gives simpler function expressions and fewer subintervals.
Deciding Between dx and dy Integration.hint: If vertical lines through the region hit the boundary twice (top and bottom), use dx; if horizontal lines work better, use dy.

Setting Up Limits from Region Descriptio.description: Translate geometric descriptions of bounded regions into concrete integration limits a and b (or c and d for dy).
Setting Up Limits from Region Descriptio.hint: Draw the region, mark all boundary curves, and identify the leftmost/rightmost (or bottom/top) extent of the region.

Volume by Cross-Sectional Slicing.description: Finding volume by integrating cross-sectional areas perpendicular to an axis: V = ∫[a to b] A(x)dx.
Volume by Cross-Sectional Slicing.hint: Identify the axis of slicing, find the area formula A(x) at each slice, then integrate over the interval.

Cross-Section Area Function A(x).description: The area of a slice perpendicular to the axis at position x, which varies with x.
Cross-Section Area Function A(x).hint: Express the cross-section dimensions in terms of x using the boundary curves or shapes.

Disk Method for Revolution.description: Finding volume when a region revolves around an axis using circular cross-sections: V = π∫[a to b] [R(x)]²dx.
Disk Method for Revolution.hint: The radius R(x) is the distance from the axis of rotation to the curve; square it and multiply by π.

Washer Method for Revolution.description: Finding volume when a region between two curves revolves around an axis: V = π∫[a to b] ([R(x)]² - [r(x)]²)dx.
Washer Method for Revolution.hint: Identify outer radius R(x) and inner radius r(x) from the axis; subtract their squares inside the integral.

Revolution About Horizontal Axis.description: Volume calculation when rotating about y = k, requiring vertical distances as radii.
Revolution About Horizontal Axis.hint: Express radii as |curve - k| to measure vertical distance from the axis of rotation.

Revolution About Vertical Axis.description: Volume calculation when rotating about x = k, often requiring dy integration with functions x(y).
Revolution About Vertical Axis.hint: Rewrite curves as x in terms of y, then integrate with respect to dy using horizontal distances.

Non-Circular Cross-Sections.description: Computing volume when slices are squares, triangles, or other shapes with area formulas in terms of base dimensions.
Non-Circular Cross-Sections.hint: Find the base dimension from the region boundaries, apply the shape's area formula, then integrate.

Choosing Axis of Integration.description: Deciding whether to integrate with dx or dy based on slice orientation and problem setup.
Choosing Axis of Integration.hint: Slice perpendicular to your integration variable: vertical slices use dx, horizontal slices use dy.

Cylindrical Shell Method.description: A technique for finding volumes of revolution by integrating the surface areas of nested cylindrical shells.
Cylindrical Shell Method.hint: Picture peeling an onion layer by layer—each shell has radius (distance from axis), height (function value), and thickness (dx or dy).

Shell Radius and Height.description: The radius is the perpendicular distance from the rotation axis to the shell, and the height is the length of the shell parallel to the axis.
Shell Radius and Height.hint: If rotating about the y-axis using dx, radius is x and height is f(x); sketch a single shell to see these dimensions.

Shell Volume Formula 2\u03c0rh.description: The volume of a thin cylindrical shell is approximated by 2πrh·thickness, where r is radius and h is height.
Shell Volume Formula 2\u03c0rh.hint: Think of 'unrolling' the shell into a thin rectangular slab with dimensions 2πr (circumference) by h by thickness.

Shell Method Integration Setup.description: The integral for shell method is ∫2π(radius)(height)dx or dy, where radius and height are functions of the integration variable.
Shell Method Integration Setup.hint: Identify which variable is parallel to the axis of rotation—that determines whether you use dx or dy.

Choosing Between Shell and Washer.description: Shell method is often simpler when slicing parallel to the rotation axis, while washer method slices perpendicular to it.
Choosing Between Shell and Washer.hint: If washer method requires splitting into multiple integrals or solving for x, try shell method instead.

Revolution About Non-Coordinate Axes.description: When rotating about lines like x=a or y=b, the shell radius becomes |variable - a| or |variable - b|.
Revolution About Non-Coordinate Axes.hint: The radius is always the perpendicular distance from the shell to the axis of rotation, not to the origin.

Shell Method for x-Axis Rotation.description: For rotation about the x-axis using shells, integrate with respect to y, where radius is y and height is expressed as x(y).
Shell Method for x-Axis Rotation.hint: When the axis of rotation is horizontal, your shells are horizontal rings, so use dy and express everything in terms of y.

Shell Method with Multiple Functions.description: When a region is bounded by multiple curves, the shell height is the difference between the rightmost and leftmost (or top and bottom) boundaries.
Shell Method with Multiple Functions.hint: Shell height works like 'top minus bottom' in area problems—it's the extent of the region at a given radius.

Arc Length Formula.description: The arc length of a curve y=f(x) from x=a to x=b is given by L = ∫[a,b] √(1 + [f'(x)]²) dx.
Arc Length Formula.hint: Start by finding the derivative f'(x), then square it, add 1, take the square root, and integrate.
Arc Length Formula.section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

Arc Length Differential Element.description: The infinitesimal arc length element ds is given by ds = √(1 + (dy/dx)²) dx, representing a tiny segment of the curve.
Arc Length Differential Element.hint: Think of ds as the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs dx and dy using the Pythagorean theorem.
Arc Length Differential Element.section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

Arc Length for x as Function of y.description: When x is expressed as a function of y, the arc length is L = ∫[c,d] √(1 + [dx/dy]²) dy.
Arc Length for x as Function of y.hint: If solving for x in terms of y is easier than y in terms of x, use this form with dx/dy instead.
Arc Length for x as Function of y.section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

Surface Area of Revolution About x-axis.description: The surface area when rotating y=f(x) about the x-axis from x=a to x=b is S = ∫[a,b] 2πf(x)√(1 + [f'(x)]²) dx.
Surface Area of Revolution About x-axis.hint: The formula combines the circumference 2πr (where r=f(x)) with the arc length element.
Surface Area of Revolution About x-axis.section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

Surface Area of Revolution About y-axis.description: The surface area when rotating x=g(y) about the y-axis from y=c to y=d is S = ∫[c,d] 2πg(y)√(1 + [g'(y)]²) dy.
Surface Area of Revolution About y-axis.hint: Use radius r=g(y) for rotation about the y-axis, similar to the x-axis case but with roles switched.
Surface Area of Revolution About y-axis.section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

Simplifying Arc Length Integrands.description: Techniques for simplifying √(1 + [f'(x)]²) include algebraic manipulation, trigonometric identities, and recognizing perfect squares.
Simplifying Arc Length Integrands.hint: Look for perfect square patterns under the radical that might simplify before integrating.
Simplifying Arc Length Integrands.section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

Numerical Approximation of Arc Length.description: When arc length integrals cannot be evaluated analytically, numerical methods like Simpson's rule or technology must be used.
Numerical Approximation of Arc Length.hint: If the integrand with the square root looks intractable, set up the integral correctly and use a calculator or computer.
Numerical Approximation of Arc Length.section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

Surface Area Element dS.description: The differential surface area element dS = 2πr·ds where r is the distance from the axis of rotation and ds is the arc length element.
Surface Area Element dS.hint: Surface area combines revolution (2πr) with the stretched length of the curve (ds), not just dx or dy.
Surface Area Element dS.section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

Work Done by Variable Force.description: Work is the integral of force over displacement when force varies with position.
Work Done by Variable Force.hint: Set up the integral as W = ∫F(x)dx where F(x) is the force function and integrate over the displacement interval.

Hooke's Law Spring Work.description: Work to compress or stretch a spring uses F(x) = kx where k is the spring constant.
Hooke's Law Spring Work.hint: Remember the natural length is where force is zero; integrate kx from initial to final displacement.

Work Against Gravity.description: Work to lift an object involves integrating weight times distance, accounting for variable mass or position.
Work Against Gravity.hint: If mass varies with height (like a rope or chain), express mass as a function of position before integrating.

Pumping Liquid Work.description: Work to pump liquid from a tank integrates the weight of each slice times the distance it travels.
Pumping Liquid Work.hint: Slice horizontally, find volume of each slice, multiply by weight density and lifting distance, then integrate.

Hydrostatic Force on Submerged Surface.description: Pressure increases with depth, so force on a submerged plate is the integral of pressure times area over depth.
Hydrostatic Force on Submerged Surface.hint: Use pressure P = ρgh at depth h, slice the surface horizontally, and integrate ρgh·width(h)·dh.

Mass from Variable Density.description: Total mass is the integral of density function over the region when density varies with position.
Mass from Variable Density.hint: Express density as ρ(x) and integrate over the length, area, or volume of the object.

Center of Mass via Integration.description: The center of mass coordinates are found by integrating position times density and dividing by total mass.
Center of Mass via Integration.hint: For a rod, use x̄ = (1/m)∫x·ρ(x)dx where m is total mass; set up separate integrals for numerator and denominator.

Moment About an Axis.description: Moment is the integral of distance from axis times the mass element, measuring rotational tendency.
Moment About an Axis.hint: Identify the axis, express perpendicular distance as a function, then integrate distance times dm.

Point Mass Moment.description: The moment of a point mass is the product of mass and perpendicular distance from an axis.
Point Mass Moment.hint: Multiply the mass by its distance from the axis you're taking the moment about.

System of Point Masses Moment.description: The total moment of a system is the sum of individual moments of all point masses.
System of Point Masses Moment.hint: Add up all the individual mass×distance products for each point in your system.

Center of Mass of Point Masses.description: The center of mass is located at x̄ = M_y/m and ȳ = M_x/m, where m is total mass and M are moments.
Center of Mass of Point Masses.hint: Divide the moment about each axis by the total mass to find the coordinate.

Moment of Lamina About x-axis.description: For a thin plate (lamina), M_x = ∫∫ y·ρ(x,y) dA, where ρ is density per unit area.
Moment of Lamina About x-axis.hint: The y-coordinate of each infinitesimal mass element gets multiplied by its mass, then integrated.

Moment of Lamina About y-axis.description: For a thin plate, M_y = ∫∫ x·ρ(x,y) dA, measuring tendency to rotate about the y-axis.
Moment of Lamina About y-axis.hint: The x-coordinate of each infinitesimal mass element gets multiplied by its mass, then integrated.

Centroid of Region.description: The centroid is the center of mass when density is constant (uniform), depending only on geometry.
Centroid of Region.hint: For uniform density, factor it out and the centroid depends only on area and first moments of area.

Centroid Using Vertical Strips.description: With vertical strips, x̄ = (1/A)∫x·[f(x)-g(x)]dx and ȳ = (1/A)∫½[f(x)²-g(x)²]dx.
Centroid Using Vertical Strips.hint: The midpoint of each strip is at y = ½[f(x)+g(x)], and its height provides the weight.

Pappus's Theorem for Volume.description: The volume of a solid of revolution equals the area of the region times the distance traveled by its centroid.
Pappus's Theorem for Volume.hint: Calculate V = 2πr̄A where r̄ is the distance from centroid to axis of rotation and A is area.

Symmetry in Center of Mass.description: If a region is symmetric about a line, its center of mass lies on that line of symmetry.
Symmetry in Center of Mass.hint: Before computing integrals, check for symmetry to reduce calculations by setting one coordinate immediately.

Exponential Growth Integral.description: Integrals of the form ∫e^(kx)dx equal (1/k)e^(kx) + C for constant k.
Exponential Growth Integral.hint: Factor out the constant from the exponent, integrate e^u, then divide by the coefficient of x.

Natural Logarithm Integration.description: The integral ∫(1/x)dx equals ln|x| + C, the fundamental logarithmic antiderivative.
Natural Logarithm Integration.hint: Remember the absolute value in ln|x| to handle negative x values in the domain.

Logarithmic Integrands with Linear Denom.description: Integrals of the form ∫1/(ax+b)dx equal (1/a)ln|ax+b| + C using substitution.
Logarithmic Integrands with Linear Denom.hint: Let u = ax+b, then du = a dx, and factor out 1/a before integrating 1/u.

Integration by u-substitution for Expone.description: Transforming exponential integrals using u-substitution when the exponent is a composite function.
Integration by u-substitution for Expone.hint: Identify the inner function as u, compute du, and verify all terms convert properly before integrating.

General Exponential Base Integration.description: The integral ∫a^x dx equals a^x/ln(a) + C for any positive base a ≠ 1.
General Exponential Base Integration.hint: Rewrite a^x as e^(x ln a) first, then use the exponential rule with k = ln(a).

Logarithmic Function as Integrand.description: Computing ∫ln(x)dx requires integration by parts with u = ln(x) and dv = dx.
Logarithmic Function as Integrand.hint: Set u = ln(x) so du = (1/x)dx, and let dv = dx so v = x, then apply the integration by parts formula.

Area Under Exponential Curves.description: Finding areas bounded by exponential functions y = e^(kx) over an interval [a,b].
Area Under Exponential Curves.hint: Set up the definite integral with exponential growth formula and evaluate at the bounds.

Volume with Logarithmic Cross-Sections.description: Applying volume methods when the radius or height function involves logarithms.
Volume with Logarithmic Cross-Sections.hint: Express the volume element using ln(x) in the appropriate formula, then integrate carefully with proper bounds.

Exponential Growth Model.description: A model where the rate of change of a quantity is proportional to its current value, expressed as dy/dt = ky where k > 0.
Exponential Growth Model.hint: Set up the differential equation by recognizing that 'rate proportional to amount' means dy/dt equals k times y.

Exponential Decay Model.description: A model where the rate of change of a quantity is negatively proportional to its current value, expressed as dy/dt = -ky where k > 0.
Exponential Decay Model.hint: Use negative k for decay; the solution will have e raised to a negative exponent causing the function to decrease.

Separating Variables for Exponential Equ.description: The technique of rewriting dy/dt = ky as dy/y = k dt to solve the differential equation by integration.
Separating Variables for Exponential Equ.hint: Move all y terms to one side with dy and all t terms to the other side with dt, then integrate both sides.

General Solution y = Ce^(kt).description: The general solution to dy/dt = ky, where C is determined by initial conditions and represents y(0).
General Solution y = Ce^(kt).hint: After separating and integrating, exponentiate both sides to solve for y; use y(0) to find C.

Half-Life in Decay Problems.description: The time required for a decaying quantity to reduce to half its initial value, related to decay constant by t_half = ln(2)/k.
Half-Life in Decay Problems.hint: Set y(t_half) equal to half the initial amount and solve for k using the exponential decay formula.

Doubling Time in Growth Problems.description: The time required for a growing quantity to double its initial value, related to growth constant by t_double = ln(2)/k.
Doubling Time in Growth Problems.hint: Set y(t_double) equal to twice the initial amount and solve for k using the exponential growth formula.

Initial Value Problems for Exponential M.description: Problems requiring determination of the constant C in y = Ce^(kt) using a condition y(t_0) = y_0.
Initial Value Problems for Exponential M.hint: Substitute the given initial time and value into y = Ce^(kt) and solve for C before finding other unknowns.

Newton's Law of Cooling.description: A model stating that the rate of temperature change is proportional to the difference between object temperature and ambient temperature: dT/dt = -k(T - T_ambient).
Newton's Law of Cooling.hint: Let u = T - T_ambient to transform this into a standard exponential decay equation for u.

Hyperbolic Sine and Cosine Definitions.description: sinh(x) = (e^x - e^(-x))/2 and cosh(x) = (e^x + e^(-x))/2 define the basic hyperbolic functions.
Hyperbolic Sine and Cosine Definitions.hint: Write sinh and cosh in terms of exponentials first, then use exponential differentiation rules.

Derivatives of Hyperbolic Functions.description: d/dx[sinh(x)] = cosh(x) and d/dx[cosh(x)] = sinh(x) are the fundamental derivative formulas.
Derivatives of Hyperbolic Functions.hint: Notice the pattern mirrors trig derivatives but without sign changes for cosh.

Hyperbolic Tangent and Other Functions.description: tanh(x) = sinh(x)/cosh(x), and similarly sech, csch, coth complete the hyperbolic function family.
Hyperbolic Tangent and Other Functions.hint: Use quotient rule to find derivatives after expressing in terms of sinh and cosh.

Hyperbolic Identity cosh\u00b2x - sinh\u.description: The fundamental hyperbolic identity relating cosh and sinh, analogous to cos²x + sin²x = 1.
Hyperbolic Identity cosh\u00b2x - sinh\u.hint: Derive this by squaring the exponential definitions and simplifying.

Integrals of Hyperbolic Functions.description: ∫sinh(x)dx = cosh(x) + C and ∫cosh(x)dx = sinh(x) + C are the basic antiderivatives.
Integrals of Hyperbolic Functions.hint: These are just the reverse of the derivative formulas you already know.

Inverse Hyperbolic Functions.description: sinh⁻¹(x), cosh⁻¹(x), and tanh⁻¹(x) are defined as inverses of hyperbolic functions with appropriate domains.
Inverse Hyperbolic Functions.hint: Express inverse hyperbolic functions as natural logarithms using the exponential definitions.

Derivatives of Inverse Hyperbolic Functi.description: d/dx[sinh⁻¹(x)] = 1/√(x²+1) and similar formulas involve algebraic expressions with radicals.
Derivatives of Inverse Hyperbolic Functi.hint: Use implicit differentiation on y = sinh(x) where x = sinh⁻¹(y), then swap variables.

Catenary Curve Application.description: The curve y = a·cosh(x/a) describes a hanging chain or cable under its own weight.
Catenary Curve Application.hint: The catenary minimizes potential energy and naturally involves cosh due to the shape's differential equation.

Integration by Parts Formula.description: The formula ∫u dv = uv - ∫v du, derived from the product rule for differentiation.
Integration by Parts Formula.hint: Identify which part of the integrand to call u (usually the part that simplifies when differentiated) and which to call dv (the part you can integrate).

Choosing u and dv Strategically.description: The selection process for u and dv using priority rules like LIATE (Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential).
Choosing u and dv Strategically.hint: Use LIATE as a guide: choose u as the function that appears first in this list, and let dv be the rest.

Repeated Integration by Parts.description: Applying integration by parts multiple times when a single application doesn't yield an elementary integral.
Repeated Integration by Parts.hint: After the first application, treat the new integral the same way, keeping track of your u and dv choices consistently.

Integration by Parts with Polynomials.description: Using integration by parts when the integrand contains a polynomial factor multiplied by an exponential or trigonometric function.
Integration by Parts with Polynomials.hint: Let u be the polynomial (it simplifies when differentiated) and dv be the exponential or trigonometric part.

Tabular Integration Method.description: A systematic tabular approach for repeated integration by parts, especially efficient for polynomial times exponential or trigonometric functions.
Tabular Integration Method.hint: Create columns for derivatives of u and integrals of dv, alternating signs, then combine diagonally to get your answer.

Integration by Parts with Logarithms.description: Applying integration by parts to integrals involving logarithmic functions, typically setting u = ln(x).
Integration by Parts with Logarithms.hint: When you see ln(x) alone or multiplied by a polynomial, let u = ln(x) since its derivative 1/x is simpler.

Integration by Parts with Inverse Trigon.description: Using integration by parts for integrals containing inverse trigonometric functions like arctan(x) or arcsin(x).
Integration by Parts with Inverse Trigon.hint: Let u equal the inverse trig function because its derivative is algebraic and often simpler to work with.

Cyclic Integration by Parts.description: A technique where repeated integration by parts eventually returns to the original integral, allowing algebraic solution.
Cyclic Integration by Parts.hint: If after two applications you see the original integral reappear, move it to the left side and solve algebraically.

Powers of Sine and Cosine.description: Integrals of the form ∫sin^m(x)cos^n(x)dx where m and n are positive integers.
Powers of Sine and Cosine.hint: If one exponent is odd, strip off one factor and use the Pythagorean identity to convert the rest; if both are even, use power-reducing formulas.

Pythagorean Identity for Integration.description: Using sin²x + cos²x = 1 to convert between sine and cosine powers during integration.
Pythagorean Identity for Integration.hint: When you have an odd power, save one factor for du and convert the remaining even power using the identity.

Power-Reducing Formulas.description: Identities like sin²x = (1-cos(2x))/2 and cos²x = (1+cos(2x))/2 used to integrate even powers.
Power-Reducing Formulas.hint: Apply these formulas when both sine and cosine have even powers to convert to first powers of double angles.

Powers of Tangent and Secant.description: Integrals of the form ∫tan^m(x)sec^n(x)dx requiring strategic use of trigonometric identities.
Powers of Tangent and Secant.hint: If secant has even power, strip off sec²x for du and convert remaining secants using 1+tan²x = sec²x; if tangent is odd, strip off sec(x)tan(x).

Tangent-Secant Pythagorean Identity.description: Using 1 + tan²x = sec²x to convert between tangent and secant powers during integration.
Tangent-Secant Pythagorean Identity.hint: This identity lets you express secants in terms of tangents or vice versa when setting up u-substitution.

Reduction Formula for Trigonometric Powe.description: Recursive formulas that express ∫sin^n(x)dx or ∫sec^n(x)dx in terms of integrals with lower powers.
Reduction Formula for Trigonometric Powe.hint: Use integration by parts to derive these formulas, then apply them repeatedly to reduce the exponent to a manageable base case.

Products of Sine and Cosine.description: Integrals like ∫sin(mx)cos(nx)dx solved using product-to-sum formulas.
Products of Sine and Cosine.hint: Convert the product to a sum using identities like sin(A)cos(B) = [sin(A+B)+sin(A-B)]/2, then integrate term by term.

Trigonometric Substitution Selection.description: Choosing when to use trigonometric methods versus other integration techniques for trigonometric integrands.
Trigonometric Substitution Selection.hint: Look at the form and powers first: odd powers suggest u-substitution, even powers suggest power-reducing, mixed products suggest product-to-sum.

Trigonometric Substitution for Square Ro.description: A technique using trigonometric identities to eliminate square roots of the form √(a²-x²), √(a²+x²), or √(x²-a²) from integrands.
Trigonometric Substitution for Square Ro.hint: Match the radical form to determine whether to use sine, tangent, or secant substitution based on the sign pattern.

Sine Substitution for \u221a(a\u00b2-x\u.description: The substitution x = a sin(θ) transforms √(a²-x²) into a cos(θ), eliminating the radical.
Sine Substitution for \u221a(a\u00b2-x\u.hint: Draw a right triangle with opposite side x and hypotenuse a to visualize why √(a²-x²) equals the adjacent side a cos(θ).

Tangent Substitution for \u221a(a\u00b2+.description: The substitution x = a tan(θ) transforms √(a²+x²) into a sec(θ) using the identity 1 + tan²(θ) = sec²(θ).
Tangent Substitution for \u221a(a\u00b2+.hint: Remember that dx = a sec²(θ) dθ, and use the tangent-secant identity to simplify the radical.

Secant Substitution for \u221a(x\u00b2-a.description: The substitution x = a sec(θ) transforms √(x²-a²) into a tan(θ) using the identity sec²(θ) - 1 = tan²(θ).
Secant Substitution for \u221a(x\u00b2-a.hint: Ensure x ≥ a or x ≤ -a for the square root to be defined, and restrict θ accordingly.

Reference Triangle for Back-Substitution.description: A right triangle constructed from the trigonometric substitution to convert the final answer back to the original variable x.
Reference Triangle for Back-Substitution.hint: Label the triangle sides based on your substitution equation, then read off the relationships you need for converting back.

Completing the Square Before Substitutio.description: Rewriting quadratic expressions ax² + bx + c in the form a(x-h)² + k to match standard substitution patterns.
Completing the Square Before Substitutio.hint: Factor out the coefficient of x² first, then complete the square on the remaining expression before identifying the substitution.

Trigonometric Identity Application in In.description: Using Pythagorean identities to simplify expressions after trigonometric substitution before integrating.
Trigonometric Identity Application in In.hint: After substitution, look for opportunities to apply sin²θ + cos²θ = 1, 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ, or sec²θ - 1 = tan²θ.

Domain Restrictions in Trig Substitution.description: Appropriate restriction of θ to ensure the inverse trigonometric function is well-defined when back-substituting.
Domain Restrictions in Trig Substitution.hint: For sine use [-π/2, π/2], for tangent use (-π/2, π/2), for secant use [0, π/2) ∪ (π/2, π] depending on sign of x.

Rational Function Decomposition.description: A rational function P(x)/Q(x) can be expressed as a sum of simpler fractions when degree of P is less than degree of Q.
Rational Function Decomposition.hint: First check if the numerator's degree is less than the denominator's; if not, perform polynomial long division first.

Proper vs Improper Rational Functions.description: A rational function is proper if degree of numerator is less than degree of denominator, otherwise improper.
Proper vs Improper Rational Functions.hint: Compare the highest power terms in numerator and denominator to determine if long division is needed.

Linear Factor Decomposition.description: For each linear factor (ax+b) in the denominator, include a term A/(ax+b) in the partial fraction decomposition.
Linear Factor Decomposition.hint: Each distinct linear factor gets its own constant numerator in the decomposition.

Repeated Linear Factor Terms.description: A repeated linear factor (ax+b)^n requires n terms: A₁/(ax+b) + A₂/(ax+b)² + ... + Aₙ/(ax+b)^n.
Repeated Linear Factor Terms.hint: For a factor raised to power n, you need n separate fractions with that factor raised to powers 1 through n.

Irreducible Quadratic Factor Terms.description: For each irreducible quadratic factor ax²+bx+c, include a term (Ax+B)/(ax²+bx+c) with linear numerator.
Irreducible Quadratic Factor Terms.hint: Irreducible quadratics need a full linear expression Ax+B in the numerator, not just a constant.

Clearing Denominators Method.description: Multiply both sides by the common denominator to create an equation of polynomials for solving unknown coefficients.
Clearing Denominators Method.hint: After multiplying through by the LCD, expand everything and collect like terms to match coefficients.

Strategic Value Substitution.description: Choose strategic x-values that make specific terms zero to quickly solve for unknown coefficients.
Strategic Value Substitution.hint: Substitute x-values that make factors equal zero (like roots of the denominator) to isolate individual constants.

Coefficient Comparison Method.description: Equate coefficients of like powers of x on both sides of the polynomial equation to create a system of equations.
Coefficient Comparison Method.hint: Match the coefficient of x², then x¹, then x⁰ on both sides to build equations for your unknowns.

Integration After Decomposition.description: Once decomposed, integrate each partial fraction term separately using logarithmic and algebraic integration rules.
Integration After Decomposition.hint: Linear denominators integrate to logarithms; use u-substitution when the denominator has a coefficient other than 1.

Integration Strategy Selection.description: The process of analyzing an integral to determine which technique (substitution, parts, trigonometric, partial fractions) is most appropriate.
Integration Strategy Selection.hint: Look for patterns: products suggest parts, compositions suggest substitution, radicals with squares suggest trigonometric substitution, and rational functions suggest partial fractions.

Simplification Before Integration.description: Algebraically manipulating an integrand using identities, factoring, or expansion to make it easier to integrate.
Simplification Before Integration.hint: Try expanding products, factoring expressions, or applying algebraic identities before choosing an integration technique.

Combination of Integration Techniques.description: Using multiple integration methods sequentially on the same problem, such as substitution followed by integration by parts.
Combination of Integration Techniques.hint: After applying one technique, reassess the new integral—it may require a different method to complete.

Integral Tables and Reference.description: Using pre-computed integral formulas from tables to evaluate standard or complex integrals directly.
Integral Tables and Reference.hint: Match your integral's form to table entries by first simplifying or making substitutions to fit standard patterns.

Computer Algebra System Integration.description: Using software tools like Mathematica or online calculators to evaluate integrals symbolically or numerically.
Computer Algebra System Integration.hint: Use CAS to verify your analytical work or to evaluate integrals that are too complex for standard techniques.

Recognizing Non-Elementary Integrals.description: Identifying integrals that cannot be expressed in terms of elementary functions, such as ∫e^(-x²)dx or ∫(sin x)/x dx.
Recognizing Non-Elementary Integrals.hint: If multiple techniques fail and the integral seems resistant to all methods, it may be non-elementary and require numerical or series methods.

Integration Strategy Hierarchy.description: A systematic approach to attempting integration methods in order: simplification, substitution, standard forms, advanced techniques, then tables or CAS.
Integration Strategy Hierarchy.hint: Start simple: check for direct substitution first, then progress to more complex techniques only if needed.

Trapezoidal Rule.description: Approximating definite integrals using trapezoids formed by connecting consecutive function values with line segments.
Trapezoidal Rule.hint: Remember the formula uses (b-a)/2n times the sum of first plus last values plus twice all middle values.

Simpson's Rule.description: Approximating definite integrals using parabolic arcs through groups of three consecutive points, requiring an even number of subintervals.
Simpson's Rule.hint: Apply the 1-4-2-4-2-4...-2-4-1 coefficient pattern and remember n must be even.

Error Bounds for Numerical Integration.description: Maximum possible error formulas for approximation methods involving derivatives of the integrand and interval width.
Error Bounds for Numerical Integration.hint: Find the maximum of the appropriate derivative on the interval to bound the error term.

Convergence of Approximation Methods.description: How errors decrease as the number of subintervals increases, with different rates for different methods.
Convergence of Approximation Methods.hint: Simpson's Rule converges faster than Trapezoidal Rule, which converges faster than basic Riemann sums.

Choosing Subinterval Count.description: Determining how many subintervals are needed to achieve a desired accuracy using error bound formulas.
Choosing Subinterval Count.hint: Set the error bound less than or equal to your desired accuracy and solve for n.

Numerical Integration for Non-Elementary.description: Applying numerical methods when antiderivatives cannot be expressed in elementary functions.
Numerical Integration for Non-Elementary.hint: If you can't find an antiderivative analytically, numerical methods may be your only practical option.

Improper Integral Definition.description: An integral with infinite limits of integration or an integrand with infinite discontinuities in the integration interval.
Improper Integral Definition.hint: Identify whether the problem is infinite limits or a discontinuous integrand before proceeding.

Type 1 Improper Integrals.description: Improper integrals with one or both limits of integration being infinite (∫[a,∞), ∫(-∞,b], or ∫(-∞,∞)).
Type 1 Improper Integrals.hint: Replace the infinite limit with a variable like t, evaluate the definite integral, then take the limit as t approaches infinity.

Type 2 Improper Integrals.description: Improper integrals where the integrand has a vertical asymptote or discontinuity within the integration interval.
Type 2 Improper Integrals.hint: Split the integral at the point of discontinuity and approach it with a limit from each side.

Convergence of Improper Integrals.description: An improper integral converges if its limit exists and is finite; otherwise it diverges.
Convergence of Improper Integrals.hint: After setting up the limit, evaluate it carefully—if you get a finite number, it converges; if infinite or undefined, it diverges.

Comparison Test for Integrals.description: If 0 ≤ f(x) ≤ g(x) and ∫g converges, then ∫f converges; if ∫f diverges, then ∫g diverges.
Comparison Test for Integrals.hint: Find a simpler function that bounds your integrand and whose convergence you already know.

Limit Comparison Test for Integrals.description: If lim[x→∞] f(x)/g(x) = L where 0 < L < ∞, then ∫f and ∫g either both converge or both diverge.
Limit Comparison Test for Integrals.hint: Choose a comparison function g(x) with similar behavior to f(x) as x approaches infinity, then compute the limit of their ratio.

p-Integral Test.description: The integral ∫[1,∞] 1/x^p dx converges if p > 1 and diverges if p ≤ 1.
p-Integral Test.hint: Rewrite your integrand in the form 1/x^p to quickly determine convergence by comparing p to 1.

Evaluating Limits at Infinity.description: Computing lim[t→∞] F(t) - F(a) or similar limits that arise when converting improper integrals to limit form.
Evaluating Limits at Infinity.hint: Apply L'Hôpital's rule if you encounter indeterminate forms, or use dominant term analysis for rational functions.

Differential Equation Definition.description: An equation relating a function to one or more of its derivatives.
Differential Equation Definition.hint: Look for equations containing y, y', y'', etc., not just algebraic equations in y alone.

Order of Differential Equation.description: The order of the highest derivative appearing in the differential equation.
Order of Differential Equation.hint: Find the highest derivative (y', y'', y''', etc.) and count the number of primes or derivative order.

General Solution of Differential Equatio.description: A family of solutions containing arbitrary constants, representing all possible solutions.
General Solution of Differential Equatio.hint: After solving, your answer should contain constants like C, C₁, C₂ that can take any value.

Particular Solution from Initial Conditi.description: A specific solution obtained by using given initial values to determine arbitrary constants.
Particular Solution from Initial Conditi.hint: Substitute the given point (x₀, y₀) into your general solution to solve for the constant C.

Verification of Differential Equation So.description: Confirming a proposed solution by substituting it and its derivatives into the original equation.
Verification of Differential Equation So.hint: Differentiate your proposed solution, substitute both y and y' into the equation, and check if both sides equal.

Initial Value Problem.description: A differential equation paired with specified values of the function and possibly its derivatives at a given point.
Initial Value Problem.hint: Look for conditions like y(0)=3 or y'(1)=2 given alongside the differential equation.

Direction Field for First-Order DE.description: A graphical representation showing slope segments at grid points to visualize solution behavior.
Direction Field for First-Order DE.hint: At each point (x,y), draw a short line segment with slope equal to f(x,y) from the equation y'=f(x,y).

Separable Differential Equations.description: First-order equations that can be written as g(y)dy = f(x)dx allowing separation of variables.
Separable Differential Equations.hint: Try to algebraically manipulate the equation so all y terms are with dy and all x terms are with dx.

Slope Field Construction.description: A graphical representation where small line segments show the slope dy/dx at grid points in the xy-plane for a differential equation.
Slope Field Construction.hint: At each point (x,y), draw a short segment with slope equal to f(x,y) from your differential equation dy/dx = f(x,y).

Solution Curve Sketching from Direction.description: Drawing curves through a direction field that are tangent to the slope segments at every point they pass through.
Solution Curve Sketching from Direction.hint: Start at your initial condition and follow the direction of the slope segments, ensuring your curve is always tangent to them.

Euler's Method.description: A numerical approximation technique that uses tangent line segments to step from one point to the next, approximating solutions to dy/dx = f(x,y).
Euler's Method.hint: Use the formula y_{n+1} = y_n + h·f(x_n, y_n) where h is your step size and repeatedly apply it starting from your initial condition.

Step Size Selection in Euler's Method.description: Choosing the increment h for the independent variable that balances computational effort with accuracy in Euler's method.
Step Size Selection in Euler's Method.hint: Smaller step sizes give better accuracy but require more calculations; try halving h to see if your answer stabilizes.

Local Truncation Error.description: The error introduced in a single step of a numerical method, typically proportional to h² for Euler's method.
Local Truncation Error.hint: The error at each step grows with the square of your step size, so halving h reduces single-step error by a factor of four.

Global Error Accumulation.description: The total accumulated error after many steps in a numerical method, typically proportional to h for Euler's method.
Global Error Accumulation.hint: Even though local error is O(h²), errors accumulate over approximately 1/h steps, giving total error proportional to h.

Isocline Interpretation.description: Curves along which the direction field has constant slope, formed by setting f(x,y) equal to a constant in dy/dx = f(x,y).
Isocline Interpretation.hint: To find where slopes equal a value like 2, solve f(x,y) = 2; all solution curves cross this curve with the same slope.

Equilibrium Solution Identification.description: Constant solutions y = c where dy/dx = 0 for all x, appearing as horizontal lines in the direction field.
Equilibrium Solution Identification.hint: Set f(x,y) = 0 and solve for y; these values give horizontal solution curves that other solutions may approach or avoid.

Separation of Variables Technique.description: Rearranging a differential equation so all terms with one variable are on one side and all terms with the other variable are on the other side.
Separation of Variables Technique.hint: Move all y terms (including dy) to one side and all x terms (including dx) to the other side before integrating.

Testing Separability Condition.description: Determining whether a differential equation can be written in the form g(y)dy = f(x)dx.
Testing Separability Condition.hint: Check if you can factor the equation into a product of a function of y only times a function of x only.

Integration of Both Sides.description: Integrating the separated equation with respect to each variable independently to find the general solution.
Integration of Both Sides.hint: After separating, place integral signs on both sides and integrate each side with respect to its own variable.

Implicit Solution Form.description: A solution to a differential equation where the dependent variable is not isolated but relates to the independent variable through an equation.
Implicit Solution Form.hint: If you cannot solve for y explicitly after integrating, leave your answer as an equation relating x and y.

Constant of Integration in DEs.description: The arbitrary constant arising from indefinite integration that represents the family of all solutions to a differential equation.
Constant of Integration in DEs.hint: Add the constant C after integrating both sides, or combine two constants into one on either side.

Solving for Explicit Solution.description: Isolating the dependent variable to express it as a function of the independent variable after integration.
Solving for Explicit Solution.hint: After integrating, use algebra to solve for y in terms of x when possible.

Lost Solutions from Division.description: Solutions that may be eliminated when dividing both sides of an equation by an expression containing the dependent variable.
Lost Solutions from Division.hint: Before dividing by a function of y, check if setting that function to zero gives an equilibrium solution.

Applying Initial Conditions to Separable.description: Using given initial values to determine the specific constant and find a particular solution to a separable equation.
Applying Initial Conditions to Separable.hint: Substitute the initial condition values into your general solution to solve for the constant C.

Logistic Differential Equation.description: A first-order differential equation of the form dP/dt = rP(1 - P/K) modeling growth with carrying capacity.
Logistic Differential Equation.hint: Identify the growth rate r and carrying capacity K from the equation structure before attempting to solve.

Carrying Capacity K.description: The maximum population size that an environment can sustain indefinitely in the logistic model.
Carrying Capacity K.hint: Look for the value that makes the growth rate zero when P equals that value.

Intrinsic Growth Rate r.description: The parameter representing the maximum per capita growth rate when population is small in the logistic model.
Intrinsic Growth Rate r.hint: This is the coefficient that determines how quickly the population approaches carrying capacity.

Partial Fraction Decomposition for Logis.description: The technique of decomposing 1/(P(1-P/K)) into partial fractions to separate and integrate the logistic equation.
Partial Fraction Decomposition for Logis.hint: Write 1/(P(K-P)) as A/P + B/(K-P) and solve for coefficients A and B.

Logistic Solution Formula.description: The explicit solution P(t) = K/(1 + Ae^(-rt)) derived from solving the logistic differential equation.
Logistic Solution Formula.hint: Use the initial condition P(0) = P₀ to find A = (K - P₀)/P₀, then substitute back.

Inflection Point in Logistic Growth.description: The point where population growth rate changes from increasing to decreasing, occurring at P = K/2.
Inflection Point in Logistic Growth.hint: Find where d²P/dt² = 0 by differentiating the logistic equation; maximum growth occurs at half the carrying capacity.

Logistic Growth Phase Analysis.description: Understanding the three phases: slow initial growth, rapid exponential-like growth near K/2, and slow approach to K.
Logistic Growth Phase Analysis.hint: Sketch the S-shaped curve and identify where dP/dt is largest by examining the factor P(1-P/K).

Equilibrium Solutions of Logistic Equati.description: The constant solutions P = 0 and P = K where dP/dt = 0, representing stable and unstable equilibria.
Equilibrium Solutions of Logistic Equati.hint: Set dP/dt = 0 and solve; P = K is stable (solutions approach it) while P = 0 is unstable.

Standard Form of First-Order Linear DE.description: A first-order differential equation written as dy/dx + P(x)y = Q(x), where P and Q are functions of x only.
Standard Form of First-Order Linear DE.hint: Rearrange your equation so all y-terms are on the left with dy/dx, and move everything else to the right side.

Integrating Factor Method.description: A technique using μ(x) = e^(∫P(x)dx) to convert a linear DE into an exact form that can be integrated directly.
Integrating Factor Method.hint: First find P(x) from standard form, then compute the integrating factor by integrating P and exponentiating the result.

Computing the Integrating Factor.description: The process of calculating μ(x) = e^(∫P(x)dx) where the constant of integration can be omitted.
Computing the Integrating Factor.hint: You can ignore the constant C when finding ∫P(x)dx because it cancels out in the exponential.

Product Rule Recognition in Linear DEs.description: Identifying that μ(x)(dy/dx) + μ(x)P(x)y equals d/dx[μ(x)y] after multiplying by the integrating factor.
Product Rule Recognition in Linear DEs.hint: After multiplying by μ(x), the left side should look like the derivative of a product—that's the key insight.

Solving After Integrating Factor.description: The process of integrating both sides of d/dx[μ(x)y] = μ(x)Q(x) and solving for y.
Solving After Integrating Factor.hint: After recognizing the product derivative form, integrate both sides with respect to x and don't forget the constant of integration.

Linear vs Nonlinear DE Classification.description: Determining if a DE is linear by checking if y and its derivatives appear to the first power only, with no products or nonlinear functions of y.
Linear vs Nonlinear DE Classification.hint: Check if y appears in products like y·y', inside functions like sin(y), or with powers like y²—any of these make it nonlinear.

Initial Condition Application for Linear.description: Using a given point (x₀, y₀) to determine the constant of integration after solving a first-order linear equation.
Initial Condition Application for Linear.hint: Substitute your initial values into the general solution and solve for C algebraically.

Bernoulli Equation Transformation.description: Converting a Bernoulli equation dy/dx + P(x)y = Q(x)y^n to linear form using the substitution v = y^(1-n).
Bernoulli Equation Transformation.hint: First identify n from the y^n term, then substitute v = y^(1-n) and use the chain rule to transform dy/dx.

Sequence Definition.description: A sequence is an ordered list of numbers a₁, a₂, a₃, ... defined by a function from positive integers to real numbers.
Sequence Definition.hint: Think of a sequence as a function where the input is n (1, 2, 3, ...) and the output is aₙ.

Explicit Formula for Sequences.description: A formula that directly expresses the nth term aₙ in terms of n, like aₙ = 1/n or aₙ = 2ⁿ.
Explicit Formula for Sequences.hint: To find a₅, simply substitute n=5 into your formula aₙ.

Recursive Formula for Sequences.description: A formula that defines each term using previous terms, like aₙ₊₁ = 2aₙ with a₁ = 3.
Recursive Formula for Sequences.hint: Start with the initial term and apply the recursion rule repeatedly to build later terms.

Limit of a Sequence.description: A sequence {aₙ} converges to limit L if for every ε > 0, there exists N such that |aₙ - L| < ε for all n ≥ N.
Limit of a Sequence.hint: Ask yourself: what value does aₙ approach as n gets arbitrarily large?

Convergent vs Divergent Sequences.description: A sequence converges if its limit exists and is finite; otherwise it diverges (including oscillation or approaching infinity).
Convergent vs Divergent Sequences.hint: Calculate lim(n→∞) aₙ; if you get a finite number, the sequence converges.

Bounded Sequences.description: A sequence is bounded if there exist numbers M and m such that m ≤ aₙ ≤ M for all n.
Bounded Sequences.hint: Check if all terms stay within some fixed interval [m, M]; graph several terms to visualize.

Monotonic Sequences.description: A sequence is monotonic if it is either always increasing (aₙ₊₁ ≥ aₙ) or always decreasing (aₙ₊₁ ≤ aₙ).
Monotonic Sequences.hint: Compare consecutive terms or check if aₙ₊₁ - aₙ has constant sign.

Monotone Convergence Theorem.description: A bounded monotonic sequence always converges to a finite limit.
Monotone Convergence Theorem.hint: If your sequence is both bounded and monotonic, you can guarantee convergence without finding the exact limit.

Sequence Limit Laws.description: Limits of sequences follow algebraic rules: lim(aₙ + bₙ) = lim(aₙ) + lim(bₙ), and similar for products and quotients.
Sequence Limit Laws.hint: Break complex sequences into simpler parts whose limits you know, then combine using limit laws.

Infinite Series Definition.description: An infinite series is the sum of the terms of an infinite sequence, written as Σ(n=1 to ∞) aₙ.
Infinite Series Definition.hint: Think of a series as adding up all terms of a sequence one after another without stopping.

Partial Sum of Series.description: The nth partial sum Sₙ is the sum of the first n terms of a series: Sₙ = Σ(k=1 to n) aₖ.
Partial Sum of Series.hint: Calculate S₁, S₂, S₃ by hand to see the pattern before looking at the limit.

Convergence of Infinite Series.description: A series Σaₙ converges if the sequence of partial sums {Sₙ} converges to a finite limit L.
Convergence of Infinite Series.hint: Check if your partial sums approach a specific number as n increases without bound.

Geometric Series.description: A geometric series has the form Σ(n=0 to ∞) arⁿ and converges to a/(1-r) when |r| < 1.
Geometric Series.hint: Identify the first term a and common ratio r, then check if |r| < 1 before using the formula.

nth Term Test for Divergence.description: If lim(n→∞) aₙ ≠ 0 or does not exist, then the series Σaₙ diverges.
nth Term Test for Divergence.hint: Always check this test first—it's the easiest way to prove divergence, but can't prove convergence.

Harmonic Series.description: The harmonic series Σ(n=1 to ∞) 1/n diverges despite its terms approaching zero.
Harmonic Series.hint: This classic counterexample shows that lim aₙ = 0 does not guarantee convergence.

Telescoping Series.description: A telescoping series has partial sums where intermediate terms cancel, leaving only first and last terms.
Telescoping Series.hint: Write out several partial sums explicitly to identify which terms cancel in the middle.

Properties of Convergent Series.description: If Σaₙ and Σbₙ converge, then Σ(aₙ ± bₙ) converges and Σ(caₙ) converges for any constant c.
Properties of Convergent Series.hint: You can add convergent series term-by-term and factor out constants, just like finite sums.

Divergence Test for Series.description: If the limit of the nth term is not zero, then the series diverges.
Divergence Test for Series.hint: Check if lim(n→∞) aₙ = 0; if not, the series must diverge immediately.

Integral Test for Series Convergence.description: A series converges if and only if the corresponding improper integral converges, when the function is positive, continuous, and decreasing.
Integral Test for Series Convergence.hint: Replace the sum with an integral from 1 to infinity and check if that improper integral converges.

Conditions for Integral Test.description: The function f(x) must be positive, continuous, and decreasing on [1,∞) for the integral test to apply.
Conditions for Integral Test.hint: Verify all three conditions before applying the integral test: check positivity, continuity, and take the derivative to confirm decreasing.

p-Series Test.description: The series Σ(1/n^p) converges if p > 1 and diverges if p ≤ 1.
p-Series Test.hint: Identify the exponent p in the denominator; convergence depends solely on whether p exceeds 1.

Estimating Series with Integrals.description: The remainder of a series can be bounded using integrals when the integral test conditions hold.
Estimating Series with Integrals.hint: Use the integral of f from n+1 to infinity to bound the tail of the series after n terms.

Improper Integral Evaluation for Series.description: Evaluating the improper integral ∫₁^∞ f(x)dx determines whether the corresponding series converges.
Improper Integral Evaluation for Series.hint: Set up the limit as b→∞ of the definite integral from 1 to b, then evaluate the antiderivative.

Comparison of Series and Integrals.description: A series can be visualized as rectangles approximating the area under the curve of its corresponding function.
Comparison of Series and Integrals.hint: Sketch the decreasing function and rectangles to see why the series and integral have the same convergence behavior.

Direct Comparison Test.description: If 0 ≤ aₙ ≤ bₙ for all n, then Σbₙ converges implies Σaₙ converges, and Σaₙ diverges implies Σbₙ diverges.
Direct Comparison Test.hint: Find a larger series that converges or a smaller series that diverges, then verify the inequality holds for all terms.

Choosing Comparison Series.description: Selecting an appropriate known series (geometric, p-series, or harmonic) to compare with the given series.
Choosing Comparison Series.hint: Identify the dominant term in aₙ as n→∞, then construct a geometric or p-series that matches that behavior.

Verifying Inequality Conditions.description: Proving algebraically that 0 ≤ aₙ ≤ bₙ holds for all n beyond some N.
Verifying Inequality Conditions.hint: Factor, simplify, or estimate each term carefully, and remember you only need the inequality to hold eventually, not necessarily for small n.

Limit Comparison Test.description: If lim(n→∞) aₙ/bₙ = L where 0 < L < ∞, then Σaₙ and Σbₙ either both converge or both diverge.
Limit Comparison Test.hint: When terms are too complicated to compare directly, compute the limit of their ratio instead of establishing inequalities.

Handling Limit Forms in LCT.description: Evaluating limits of ratios aₙ/bₙ using algebraic manipulation or L'Hôpital's rule for corresponding functions.
Handling Limit Forms in LCT.hint: Divide numerator and denominator by the highest power of n, or convert to continuous functions and apply L'Hôpital's rule.

Selecting Dominant Terms.description: Identifying the fastest-growing terms in numerator and denominator to simplify series comparison.
Selecting Dominant Terms.hint: As n→∞, polynomial terms dominate constants, exponentials dominate polynomials, and factorials dominate exponentials.

Comparison Test Inconclusive Cases.description: Recognizing when comparison tests fail: when L=0 or L=∞ in LCT, or when inequality goes the wrong direction in DCT.
Comparison Test Inconclusive Cases.hint: If your comparison gives L=0 or L=∞, try a different comparison series; you need 0 < L < ∞ for the Limit Comparison Test.

Rational Function Series Strategy.description: For series with rational functions, comparing to p-series based on the degree difference between numerator and denominator.
Rational Function Series Strategy.hint: If numerator has degree m and denominator has degree n, compare to 1/n^(n-m); it converges when n-m > 1.

Alternating Series Definition.description: An infinite series whose terms alternate in sign, typically written as ∑(-1)^n·b_n or ∑(-1)^(n+1)·b_n where b_n > 0.
Alternating Series Definition.hint: Look for the (-1)^n factor that flips the sign of each successive term.

Alternating Series Test.description: A series ∑(-1)^n·b_n converges if b_n is decreasing, positive, and lim(n→∞) b_n = 0.
Alternating Series Test.hint: Check all three conditions: positive terms, decreasing sequence, and limit zero—all must hold.

Decreasing Sequence Verification.description: Showing b_(n+1) ≤ b_n for all n, often using derivatives or algebraic manipulation.
Decreasing Sequence Verification.hint: Try comparing b_(n+1) to b_n directly, or use f'(x) < 0 where f(n) = b_n.

Alternating Series Remainder.description: The error |S - S_N| when approximating alternating series sum S by partial sum S_N is bounded by |b_(N+1)|.
Alternating Series Remainder.hint: The first omitted term gives you the maximum error—no need to compute all remaining terms.

Absolute Convergence.description: A series ∑a_n converges absolutely if ∑|a_n| converges.
Absolute Convergence.hint: Remove all negative signs and test the resulting positive series with comparison, ratio, or other tests.

Conditional Convergence.description: A series ∑a_n converges conditionally if it converges but ∑|a_n| diverges.
Conditional Convergence.hint: First verify the original series converges (often by Alternating Series Test), then show the absolute version diverges.

Absolute vs Conditional Distinction.description: Understanding that absolute convergence implies convergence, but conditional convergence is weaker.
Absolute vs Conditional Distinction.hint: If ∑|a_n| converges, you're done—the original series converges; otherwise check if it's conditionally convergent.

Alternating Harmonic Series.description: The series ∑(-1)^(n+1)/n = 1 - 1/2 + 1/3 - 1/4 + ... which converges conditionally to ln(2).
Alternating Harmonic Series.hint: This is the classic example: converges by Alternating Series Test but diverges absolutely since it's the harmonic series.

Ratio Test for Series.description: A convergence test that examines the limit of the ratio of consecutive terms |a_{n+1}/a_n| to determine if a series converges absolutely, diverges, or is inconclusive.
Ratio Test for Series.hint: Calculate L = lim(n→∞)|a_{n+1}/a_n|: if L<1 converges absolutely, L>1 diverges, L=1 inconclusive.

Root Test for Series.description: A convergence test that examines the limit of the nth root of absolute terms lim(n→∞)|a_n|^(1/n) to determine absolute convergence or divergence.
Root Test for Series.hint: Calculate L = lim(n→∞)|a_n|^(1/n): if L<1 converges absolutely, L>1 diverges, L=1 inconclusive.

Factorial Terms in Series.description: Series containing factorial expressions n! in numerator or denominator, which grow extremely rapidly and often make the Ratio Test effective.
Factorial Terms in Series.hint: When you see factorials, use Ratio Test because (n+1)!/n! simplifies to (n+1).

Exponential Terms in Series.description: Series containing exponential expressions like r^n where the base changes with n, making the Ratio or Root Test particularly effective.
Exponential Terms in Series.hint: For series with r^n where r varies with n, try Root Test to extract the base directly.

Ratio Test Inconclusive Cases.description: When the Ratio Test yields L=1, the test provides no information and another convergence test must be applied.
Ratio Test Inconclusive Cases.hint: If Ratio Test gives L=1, try Comparison Test, Integral Test, or p-Series Test instead.

Simplifying Consecutive Term Ratios.description: The algebraic technique of simplifying |a_{n+1}/a_n| by canceling common factors and reducing expressions before taking limits.
Simplifying Consecutive Term Ratios.hint: Before taking the limit, cancel factorials using (n+1)!/n! = n+1 and simplify exponentials using laws of exponents.

Root Test for Exponential Growth.description: The Root Test is particularly efficient for series where terms have the form (f(n))^n because the nth root directly extracts f(n).
Root Test for Exponential Growth.hint: If your term looks like (expression)^n, Root Test will reduce |a_n|^(1/n) to just |expression|.

Choosing Between Ratio and Root Tests.description: The strategic decision of which test to apply based on series structure: Ratio Test for factorials and products, Root Test for exponential forms.
Choosing Between Ratio and Root Tests.hint: Use Ratio Test for factorials or products of terms; use Root Test when the entire term is raised to the nth power.

Power Series Definition.description: An infinite series of the form Σ(n=0 to ∞) cₙ(x-a)ⁿ where cₙ are coefficients and a is the center.
Power Series Definition.hint: Start by identifying the coefficients cₙ and the center point a in your series.

Radius of Convergence.description: The value R such that a power series converges for |x-a| < R and diverges for |x-a| > R.
Radius of Convergence.hint: Apply the ratio test or root test to |cₙ(x-a)ⁿ| to find where the limit is less than 1.

Interval of Convergence.description: The set of all x-values for which a power series converges, including endpoint behavior.
Interval of Convergence.hint: After finding the radius R, separately test convergence at both endpoints x = a-R and x = a+R.

Power Series as Functions.description: A power series defines a function f(x) = Σcₙ(x-a)ⁿ on its interval of convergence.
Power Series as Functions.hint: Evaluate the function by substituting specific x-values within the interval of convergence.

Term-by-Term Differentiation of Power Se.description: A power series can be differentiated term-by-term: d/dx[Σcₙ(x-a)ⁿ] = Σn·cₙ(x-a)ⁿ⁻¹ within its radius of convergence.
Term-by-Term Differentiation of Power Se.hint: Differentiate each term separately using the power rule, noting the radius of convergence stays the same.

Term-by-Term Integration of Power Series.description: A power series can be integrated term-by-term: ∫Σcₙ(x-a)ⁿdx = C + Σ[cₙ/(n+1)](x-a)ⁿ⁺¹ within its radius of convergence.
Term-by-Term Integration of Power Series.hint: Integrate each term using the power rule for integrals, remembering to add a constant of integration.

Endpoint Convergence Testing.description: The process of separately testing convergence at x = a-R and x = a+R using appropriate series tests.
Endpoint Convergence Testing.hint: Substitute each endpoint value into the series and apply tests like alternating series test or p-series test.

Centered Power Series at x=0.description: A power series of the form Σcₙxⁿ centered at the origin (Maclaurin-type form).
Centered Power Series at x=0.hint: When the center a=0, simplify by writing the series as Σcₙxⁿ and test |x| < R.

Algebra of Power Series.description: Power series can be added, subtracted, and multiplied term-by-term within their common interval of convergence.
Algebra of Power Series.hint: Find where both series converge, then combine coefficients of like powers of x.

Convergence Preservation in Operations.description: Algebraic operations on power series preserve convergence within the smaller radius of convergence.
Convergence Preservation in Operations.hint: The resulting series converges at least where both original series converge simultaneously.

Substitution in Power Series.description: Replacing the variable in a power series with another expression to generate new series representations.
Substitution in Power Series.hint: Substitute the expression for x everywhere, then simplify and check the new interval of convergence.

Differentiation Preserves Radius.description: Term-by-term differentiation of a power series maintains the same radius of convergence but may affect endpoint behavior.
Differentiation Preserves Radius.hint: The radius stays the same, but always recheck convergence at the endpoints after differentiating.

Integration Preserves Radius.description: Term-by-term integration of a power series maintains the same radius of convergence but may affect endpoint behavior.
Integration Preserves Radius.hint: The radius stays the same, but always recheck convergence at the endpoints after integrating.

Finding Function Representations.description: Using known power series and algebraic operations to represent new functions as power series.
Finding Function Representations.hint: Start with geometric series or known expansions, then apply substitution, differentiation, or integration.

Uniqueness of Power Series.description: If a function equals a power series on an interval, the coefficients are uniquely determined by the function's derivatives.
Uniqueness of Power Series.hint: Two different power series cannot represent the same function on an interval around their center.

Combining Series Representations.description: Using addition, multiplication, and composition to build complex function representations from simpler power series.
Combining Series Representations.hint: Break the target function into operations on functions with known series, then combine appropriately.

Taylor Series Definition.description: An infinite series representation of a function as a sum of terms calculated from the function's derivatives at a single point.
Taylor Series Definition.hint: Start with the general formula: f(x) = Σ(f^(n)(a)/n!)(x-a)^n and identify what each part means for your specific function.

Maclaurin Series Definition.description: A Taylor series centered at a=0, given by f(x) = Σ(f^(n)(0)/n!)(x^n).
Maclaurin Series Definition.hint: This is just a Taylor series with a=0; compute derivatives at zero to find coefficients.

Computing Taylor Coefficients.description: Finding the nth coefficient of a Taylor series by evaluating the nth derivative at the center point and dividing by n factorial.
Computing Taylor Coefficients.hint: Calculate f'(a), f''(a), f'''(a), etc., then divide each by the corresponding factorial to get each coefficient.

Common Maclaurin Series.description: Standard Maclaurin series for elementary functions including e^x, sin(x), cos(x), and 1/(1-x).
Common Maclaurin Series.hint: Memorize the basic series for e^x, sin(x), cos(x), and geometric series; you'll use them to build more complex ones.

Taylor Polynomial Approximation.description: Using the first n+1 terms of a Taylor series as a polynomial approximation T_n(x) to the original function.
Taylor Polynomial Approximation.hint: Truncate the infinite series after the x^n term to get a manageable polynomial that approximates your function near the center.

Taylor Remainder Theorem.description: A formula for the error R_n(x) between a function and its nth Taylor polynomial, often expressed using the (n+1)st derivative.
Taylor Remainder Theorem.hint: The remainder tells you how far off your polynomial approximation is; bound it by finding the maximum of |f^(n+1)| on your interval.

Manipulating Known Series.description: Creating new Taylor series by performing algebraic operations, substitution, differentiation, or integration on known series.
Manipulating Known Series.hint: Start with a series you know (like e^x or geometric series), then substitute, differentiate, or integrate term-by-term to match your target function.

Interval of Convergence for Taylor Serie.description: Determining the set of x-values for which a Taylor series converges to the function it represents.
Interval of Convergence for Taylor Serie.hint: Use the ratio test or other convergence tests you already know, then check endpoints separately if needed.

Substituting into Known Taylor Series.description: Replacing the variable in a known Taylor series with another expression to find new series representations.
Substituting into Known Taylor Series.hint: Identify the known series pattern, substitute your expression for x, then simplify each term carefully.

Taylor Series for Composite Functions.description: Finding Taylor series by substituting expressions like x² or -x into standard series like eˣ or sin(x).
Taylor Series for Composite Functions.hint: Start with the standard series, replace every occurrence of x with your new expression, then adjust the radius of convergence.

Multiplying Taylor Series.description: Finding the product of two power series by multiplying terms and collecting like powers.
Multiplying Taylor Series.hint: Multiply the first few terms from each series, group terms with the same power of x, and look for a pattern.

Dividing Taylor Series.description: Finding the quotient of two power series by long division or algebraic manipulation.
Dividing Taylor Series.hint: Use long division of polynomials technique, or multiply numerator by the reciprocal series if you know it.

Term-by-Term Differentiation for New Ser.description: Finding Taylor series by differentiating a known series representation term by term.
Term-by-Term Differentiation for New Ser.hint: Differentiate each term of the known series, remember to adjust coefficients and powers according to the power rule.

Term-by-Term Integration for New Series.description: Finding Taylor series by integrating a known series representation term by term and adding a constant.
Term-by-Term Integration for New Series.hint: Integrate each term separately, increase each power by 1 and divide by the new power, then determine the constant from initial conditions.

Taylor Series for Definite Integrals.description: Evaluating definite integrals by integrating a Taylor series term by term within its interval of convergence.
Taylor Series for Definite Integrals.hint: Replace the integrand with its Taylor series, integrate term by term, then substitute the limits into the resulting series.

Solving Differential Equations with Seri.description: Finding power series solutions to differential equations by substituting a series and matching coefficients.
