Solving Differential Equations with Seri.hint: Assume a solution is a power series, substitute it and its derivatives into the equation, then equate coefficients of like powers.

Parametric Equations Definition.description: A pair of equations x=f(t) and y=g(t) that express coordinates as functions of a parameter t.
Parametric Equations Definition.hint: Think of t as time and trace how a point moves as t changes from one value to another.

Parameter Elimination.description: Solving for t in one parametric equation and substituting into the other to obtain a Cartesian equation y=F(x).
Parameter Elimination.hint: Isolate t from the simpler equation first, then substitute that expression into the other equation.

Parametric Curve Orientation.description: The direction a curve is traced as the parameter t increases.
Parametric Curve Orientation.hint: Plot points for increasing values of t (like t=0, 1, 2) and draw arrows showing the path direction.

Parametric Equations for Circles.description: Standard form x=h+r·cos(t), y=k+r·sin(t) describes a circle with center (h,k) and radius r.
Parametric Equations for Circles.hint: Use the Pythagorean identity cos²(t)+sin²(t)=1 to verify the curve is a circle.

Parametric Equations for Lines.description: Linear parametric form x=x₀+at, y=y₀+bt describes a line through (x₀,y₀) with direction vector ⟨a,b⟩.
Parametric Equations for Lines.hint: Eliminate t to find slope m=b/a if a≠0, or recognize vertical line if a=0.

Multiple Parametrizations Same Curve.description: Different parametric equations can describe the same geometric curve with different speeds or orientations.
Multiple Parametrizations Same Curve.hint: After elimination, check if you get the same Cartesian equation and compare orientations for specific t values.

Domain Restrictions in Parametric Form.description: The interval for parameter t determines which portion of a curve is traced.
Domain Restrictions in Parametric Form.hint: Evaluate the parametric equations at the endpoints of the t-interval to find where the curve starts and stops.

Converting Cartesian to Parametric.description: Expressing a Cartesian equation as parametric equations by choosing x=t or y=t and solving for the other variable.
Converting Cartesian to Parametric.hint: Let x=t, then solve for y in terms of t, or choose a parametrization that simplifies the given equation.

Derivatives of Parametric Equations.description: Finding dy/dx for curves defined by x=f(t), y=g(t) using the formula dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt).
Derivatives of Parametric Equations.hint: Differentiate both x and y with respect to t separately, then divide dy/dt by dx/dt.

Second Derivative of Parametric Curves.description: Computing d²y/dx² for parametric curves using the formula d²y/dx² = (d/dt(dy/dx))/(dx/dt).
Second Derivative of Parametric Curves.hint: First find dy/dx, then differentiate that result with respect to t and divide by dx/dt again.

Arc Length of Parametric Curves.description: Finding the length of a parametric curve from t=a to t=b using the integral √[(dx/dt)² + (dy/dt)²] dt.
Arc Length of Parametric Curves.hint: Square both derivatives, add them, take the square root, and integrate over the parameter interval.

Surface Area of Revolution Parametric.description: Computing surface area when revolving a parametric curve around an axis using 2π times radius times arc length element.
Surface Area of Revolution Parametric.hint: Identify which variable (x or y) is the radius, multiply by the parametric arc length formula, and integrate.

Tangent Lines to Parametric Curves.description: Finding equations of tangent lines at specific parameter values using the parametric derivative dy/dx.
Tangent Lines to Parametric Curves.hint: Evaluate both x(t) and y(t) at the given t to get a point, then find dy/dx at that t for the slope.

Horizontal and Vertical Tangents Paramet.description: Identifying where parametric curves have horizontal tangents (dy/dt=0, dx/dt≠0) or vertical tangents (dx/dt=0, dy/dt≠0).
Horizontal and Vertical Tangents Paramet.hint: Horizontal tangents occur when the numerator dy/dt equals zero; vertical when the denominator dx/dt equals zero.

Concavity of Parametric Curves.description: Determining concavity using the sign of d²y/dx² for parametric equations.
Concavity of Parametric Curves.hint: If d²y/dx² is positive, the curve is concave up; if negative, concave down at that parameter value.

Polar Coordinate System.description: A coordinate system where each point is determined by a distance r from the origin and an angle θ from the positive x-axis.
Polar Coordinate System.hint: Draw a point, measure its distance from origin (r) and angle counterclockwise from the positive x-axis (θ).

Converting Polar to Cartesian.description: Using the formulas x = r cos(θ) and y = r sin(θ) to convert polar coordinates to rectangular coordinates.
Converting Polar to Cartesian.hint: Remember that x equals r times cosine of theta, and y equals r times sine of theta.

Converting Cartesian to Polar.description: Using r² = x² + y² and tan(θ) = y/x to convert rectangular coordinates to polar form.
Converting Cartesian to Polar.hint: Find r using the Pythagorean theorem, then find θ using arctangent while checking the quadrant.

Polar Equations of Curves.description: Equations of the form r = f(θ) that describe curves in the polar coordinate system.
Polar Equations of Curves.hint: Plug in different θ values to find corresponding r values and plot points to see the curve shape.

Symmetry in Polar Graphs.description: Testing polar equations for symmetry about the x-axis, y-axis, or origin by substituting -θ, π-θ, or -r.
Symmetry in Polar Graphs.hint: Replace θ with -θ to test x-axis symmetry; if the equation stays the same, the graph is symmetric.

Common Polar Curves.description: Standard polar curves including circles (r = a), cardioids (r = a(1 ± cos θ)), and rose curves (r = a cos(nθ)).
Common Polar Curves.hint: For cardioids, the plus or minus determines direction; for roses, n petals if n is odd, 2n if n is even.

Polar Derivatives dy/dx.description: Computing dy/dx for polar curves using dy/dx = (dr/dθ sin θ + r cos θ)/(dr/dθ cos θ - r sin θ).
Polar Derivatives dy/dx.hint: Use the chain rule with x = r cos θ and y = r sin θ to find dy/dθ and dx/dθ first.

Tangent Lines to Polar Curves.description: Finding slopes and equations of tangent lines to curves given in polar form at specific points.
Tangent Lines to Polar Curves.hint: Calculate dy/dx at the given θ value using the polar derivative formula, then use point-slope form.

Area Formula in Polar Coordinates.description: The area of a region bounded by a polar curve r=f(θ) from θ=α to θ=β is A = (1/2)∫[α to β] r² dθ.
Area Formula in Polar Coordinates.hint: Square the radius function r(θ) first, then integrate; the 1/2 factor comes from the sector area formula.
Area Formula in Polar Coordinates.section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

Area Between Two Polar Curves.description: The area between outer curve r=f(θ) and inner curve r=g(θ) is A = (1/2)∫[α to β] (f(θ)² - g(θ)²) dθ.
Area Between Two Polar Curves.hint: Subtract the inner radius squared from the outer radius squared before integrating, keeping the 1/2 factor outside.
Area Between Two Polar Curves.section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

Finding Polar Integration Limits.description: Determining the bounds α and β by analyzing where the curve begins and ends or where curves intersect.
Finding Polar Integration Limits.hint: Sketch the polar curve and identify the starting and ending angles; for intersections, solve f(θ) = g(θ).
Finding Polar Integration Limits.section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

Arc Length in Polar Coordinates.description: The arc length of polar curve r=f(θ) from θ=α to θ=β is L = ∫[α to β] √(r² + (dr/dθ)²) dθ.
Arc Length in Polar Coordinates.hint: Compute dr/dθ first, then form the expression under the square root; this formula comes from the parametric arc length formula.
Arc Length in Polar Coordinates.section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

Computing dr/d\u03b8 for Polar Functions.description: Finding the derivative of the radius function r(θ) with respect to the parameter θ.
Computing dr/d\u03b8 for Polar Functions.hint: Treat θ as the variable and differentiate r(θ) using standard differentiation rules.
Computing dr/d\u03b8 for Polar Functions.section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

Area of One Petal or Loop.description: Computing the area of a single petal in rose curves or one loop in limaçons by identifying appropriate integration bounds.
Area of One Petal or Loop.hint: One petal typically spans from where r=0 to the next zero; for r=sin(nθ), one petal covers π/n radians.
Area of One Petal or Loop.section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

Polar Area with Symmetry.description: Using symmetry properties to compute total area by integrating over one symmetric portion and multiplying.
Polar Area with Symmetry.hint: If the curve has symmetry about the polar axis or θ=π/2, integrate over one section and multiply by the number of symmetric parts.
Polar Area with Symmetry.section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

Intersection Points of Polar Curves.description: Finding where two polar curves meet by solving r₁(θ) = r₂(θ) and checking for the pole (origin).
Intersection Points of Polar Curves.hint: Remember the origin can be represented by r=0 at any angle; check both algebraic intersections and when each curve passes through the pole.
Intersection Points of Polar Curves.section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

Standard Form of Parabola.description: A parabola has equation (x-h)²=4p(y-k) or (y-k)²=4p(x-h) with vertex (h,k) and focal parameter p.
Standard Form of Parabola.hint: Identify which variable is squared to determine if the parabola opens vertically or horizontally.

Standard Form of Ellipse.description: An ellipse has equation (x-h)²/a² + (y-k)²/b² = 1 with center (h,k), semi-major axis a, and semi-minor axis b.
Standard Form of Ellipse.hint: The larger denominator determines the major axis direction; use c²=a²-b² to find foci.

Standard Form of Hyperbola.description: A hyperbola has equation (x-h)²/a² - (y-k)²/b² = 1 or (y-k)²/a² - (x-h)²/b² = 1 with center (h,k).
Standard Form of Hyperbola.hint: The positive term determines the transverse axis direction; use c²=a²+b² for hyperbolas.

Eccentricity of Conic Sections.description: Eccentricity e measures conic shape: e=0 for circles, 0<e<1 for ellipses, e=1 for parabolas, e>1 for hyperbolas.
Eccentricity of Conic Sections.hint: Calculate e=c/a where c is the focal distance and a is the semi-major axis length.

Polar Equations of Conics.description: Conics with focus at origin have polar form r = ed/(1 ± e·cosθ) or r = ed/(1 ± e·sinθ) where e is eccentricity.
Polar Equations of Conics.hint: The denominator form (cosθ or sinθ, plus or minus) determines directrix orientation and position.

Directrix of Conic Sections.description: A directrix is a fixed line such that the ratio of distances (point to focus)/(point to directrix) equals eccentricity e.
Directrix of Conic Sections.hint: For polar conics, the constant d in the equation represents the distance from focus to directrix.

Completing the Square for Conics.description: Transform general conic equation Ax²+Cy²+Dx+Ey+F=0 to standard form by completing the square in x and y.
Completing the Square for Conics.hint: Group x-terms and y-terms separately, complete the square for each, then isolate the constant on the right.

Identifying Conic Type from Equation.description: Determine conic type from Ax²+Bxy+Cy²+Dx+Ey+F=0 using discriminant B²-4AC or coefficient pattern.
Identifying Conic Type from Equation.hint: If B=0: same sign coefficients mean ellipse, opposite signs mean hyperbola, one squared term means parabola.

Converting Cartesian Conics to Polar.description: Transform standard Cartesian conic equations into polar form using x=r·cosθ and y=r·sinθ substitutions.
Converting Cartesian Conics to Polar.hint: After substituting, solve for r as a function of θ by factoring r from all terms.

Vector as Directed Line Segment.description: A vector is a mathematical object with magnitude and direction, represented geometrically as an arrow from initial point to terminal point.
Vector as Directed Line Segment.hint: Draw the arrow from the starting point to the ending point; the arrow's length and direction fully describe the vector.

Component Form of Vectors.description: A vector in the plane is written as ⟨a, b⟩ where a and b are the horizontal and vertical components.
Component Form of Vectors.hint: Subtract initial point coordinates from terminal point coordinates to find components: ⟨x₂-x₁, y₂-y₁⟩.

Magnitude of a Vector.description: The magnitude (or length) of vector v = ⟨a, b⟩ is ||v|| = √(a² + b²).
Magnitude of a Vector.hint: Use the Pythagorean theorem on the components to find the distance from the origin to the point (a, b).

Vector Addition and Subtraction.description: Vectors are added or subtracted component-wise: ⟨a₁, b₁⟩ ± ⟨a₂, b₂⟩ = ⟨a₁ ± a₂, b₁ ± b₂⟩.
Vector Addition and Subtraction.hint: Add or subtract corresponding components separately; geometrically, place vectors tip-to-tail for addition.

Scalar Multiplication of Vectors.description: Multiplying a vector by a scalar k scales its magnitude: k⟨a, b⟩ = ⟨ka, kb⟩.
Scalar Multiplication of Vectors.hint: Multiply each component by the scalar; negative scalars reverse direction while positive ones preserve it.

Unit Vectors and Normalization.description: A unit vector has magnitude 1; any vector v can be normalized to u = v/||v||.
Unit Vectors and Normalization.hint: Divide each component by the vector's magnitude to get a unit vector in the same direction.

Standard Unit Vectors i and j.description: The standard unit vectors are i = ⟨1, 0⟩ and j = ⟨0, 1⟩, allowing any vector to be written as v = ai + bj.
Standard Unit Vectors i and j.hint: Express vectors as combinations of i and j: the coefficients are just the x and y components.

Zero Vector Properties.description: The zero vector 0 = ⟨0, 0⟩ has magnitude zero and indeterminate direction, serving as the additive identity.
Zero Vector Properties.hint: The zero vector is the only vector with magnitude zero; adding it to any vector leaves that vector unchanged.

Three-Dimensional Coordinate System.description: A system using three perpendicular axes (x, y, z) to locate points in space with ordered triples (x, y, z).
Three-Dimensional Coordinate System.hint: Draw the axes with x and y in the horizontal plane and z pointing upward, forming right angles.

Vectors in Three Dimensions.description: Directed line segments in space represented as ordered triples ⟨a, b, c⟩ or ai + bj + ck.
Vectors in Three Dimensions.hint: Extend your 2D vector thinking by adding a third component for the z-direction.

Magnitude in Three Dimensions.description: The length of a 3D vector v = ⟨a, b, c⟩ calculated as ||v|| = √(a² + b² + c²).
Magnitude in Three Dimensions.hint: Use the Pythagorean theorem twice: first in the xy-plane, then include the z-component.

Standard Unit Vectors i j k.description: The vectors i = ⟨1, 0, 0⟩, j = ⟨0, 1, 0⟩, and k = ⟨0, 0, 1⟩ pointing along the coordinate axes.
Standard Unit Vectors i j k.hint: These are just the 3D extension of i and j, with k pointing along the z-axis.

Vector Operations in 3D.description: Addition, subtraction, and scalar multiplication performed component-wise on three-dimensional vectors.
Vector Operations in 3D.hint: Apply the same rules as 2D vectors but include the third component in all calculations.

Distance Formula in Three Dimensions.description: The distance between points (x₁, y₁, z₁) and (x₂, y₂, z₂) is √[(x₂-x₁)² + (y₂-y₁)² + (z₂-z₁)²].
Distance Formula in Three Dimensions.hint: This is the magnitude of the vector from one point to the other.

Midpoint Formula in 3D.description: The midpoint between (x₁, y₁, z₁) and (x₂, y₂, z₂) is ((x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2, (z₁+z₂)/2).
Midpoint Formula in 3D.hint: Average each coordinate separately, just like in 2D but with a third coordinate.

Equation of a Sphere.description: A sphere with center (h, k, l) and radius r has equation (x-h)² + (y-k)² + (z-l)² = r².
Equation of a Sphere.hint: This is the 3D version of a circle equation using the distance formula from the center.

Dot Product Definition.description: The dot product of vectors u and v is u·v = u₁v₁ + u₂v₂ + u₃v₃, yielding a scalar.
Dot Product Definition.hint: Multiply corresponding components and add them together to get a single number, not a vector.

Geometric Interpretation of Dot Product.description: The dot product equals |u||v|cos(θ), where θ is the angle between vectors u and v.
Geometric Interpretation of Dot Product.hint: Use this form when you know magnitudes and angle instead of components.

Angle Between Vectors Formula.description: The angle θ between vectors satisfies cos(θ) = (u·v)/(|u||v|), where 0 ≤ θ ≤ π.
Angle Between Vectors Formula.hint: Calculate the dot product and magnitudes first, then use inverse cosine to find the angle.

Orthogonal Vectors Test.description: Two nonzero vectors are orthogonal (perpendicular) if and only if their dot product equals zero.
Orthogonal Vectors Test.hint: Check if u·v = 0; if yes, the vectors form a 90-degree angle.

Projection of Vector onto Vector.description: The scalar projection of u onto v is comp_v(u) = (u·v)/|v|, and the vector projection is proj_v(u) = [(u·v)/|v|²]v.
Projection of Vector onto Vector.hint: Find how much of vector u points in the direction of v by computing the dot product divided by v's magnitude.

Properties of Dot Product.description: The dot product is commutative (u·v = v·u), distributive over addition, and satisfies c(u·v) = (cu)·v for scalar c.
Properties of Dot Product.hint: Use these algebraic properties to simplify expressions before computing components.

Work Done by Constant Force.description: Work W equals F·d, the dot product of force vector F and displacement vector d.
Work Done by Constant Force.hint: Only the component of force in the direction of motion contributes to work.

Direction Cosines.description: For a vector v, the direction cosines are cos(α), cos(β), cos(γ), where α, β, γ are angles with the positive x, y, z axes.
Direction Cosines.hint: Calculate direction cosines using dot products with standard unit vectors i, j, k.

Cross Product Definition.description: The cross product of two vectors in three dimensions produces a third vector perpendicular to both input vectors.
Cross Product Definition.hint: Use the determinant formula with i, j, k unit vectors or memorize the component formula: (a₂b₃-a₃b₂)i - (a₁b₃-a₃b₁)j + (a₁b₂-a₂b₁)k.

Right-Hand Rule for Cross Product.description: A method to determine the direction of the cross product vector using the right hand.
Right-Hand Rule for Cross Product.hint: Point your fingers along the first vector, curl them toward the second vector, and your thumb points in the direction of the cross product.

Magnitude of Cross Product.description: The magnitude |u × v| equals |u||v|sin(θ) where θ is the angle between the vectors.
Magnitude of Cross Product.hint: This formula connects to area: the magnitude gives the area of the parallelogram formed by the two vectors.

Cross Product Properties.description: The cross product is anticommutative (u × v = -(v × u)), distributive, and u × u = 0.
Cross Product Properties.hint: Remember order matters: swapping vectors reverses the direction of the result.

Parallel Vectors via Cross Product.description: Two vectors are parallel if and only if their cross product equals the zero vector.
Parallel Vectors via Cross Product.hint: Check if u × v = 0; this happens when the vectors point in the same or opposite directions.

Area of Parallelogram Using Cross Produc.description: The area of a parallelogram spanned by vectors u and v equals |u × v|.
Area of Parallelogram Using Cross Produc.hint: Compute the cross product first, then find its magnitude to get the area.

Area of Triangle Using Cross Product.description: The area of a triangle with sides formed by vectors u and v equals (1/2)|u × v|.
Area of Triangle Using Cross Product.hint: A triangle is half a parallelogram, so take half the magnitude of the cross product.

Torque as Cross Product.description: Torque τ equals r × F, where r is the position vector and F is the force vector.
Torque as Cross Product.hint: The magnitude of torque is |r||F|sin(θ), representing the rotational effect of the force.

Triple Scalar Product.description: The scalar u · (v × w) gives the volume of the parallelepiped formed by three vectors.
Triple Scalar Product.hint: Compute the cross product v × w first, then take the dot product with u.

Vector Equation of a Line in Space.description: A line in 3D space represented as r(t) = r₀ + tv, where r₀ is a position vector to a point on the line and v is a direction vector.
Vector Equation of a Line in Space.hint: Start with a known point on the line and add a scalar multiple of the direction vector to reach any other point.
Vector Equation of a Line in Space.section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

Parametric Equations of a Line.description: Component form of a line in space written as x = x₀ + at, y = y₀ + bt, z = z₀ + ct, where (a,b,c) is the direction vector.
Parametric Equations of a Line.hint: Extract each component from the vector equation separately to get three scalar equations.
Parametric Equations of a Line.section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

Symmetric Equations of a Line.description: Eliminating the parameter t from parametric equations to get (x-x₀)/a = (y-y₀)/b = (z-z₀)/c, valid when direction components are nonzero.
Symmetric Equations of a Line.hint: Solve each parametric equation for t and set them equal to eliminate the parameter.
Symmetric Equations of a Line.section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

Normal Vector to a Plane.description: A vector perpendicular to every vector lying in a given plane, used to determine the plane's orientation in space.
Normal Vector to a Plane.hint: Use the cross product of two vectors in the plane to find a normal vector.
Normal Vector to a Plane.section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

Scalar Equation of a Plane.description: A plane represented as ax + by + cz = d, where (a,b,c) is a normal vector to the plane.
Scalar Equation of a Plane.hint: Use the dot product of the normal vector with a general point (x,y,z) minus a known point on the plane, then expand.
Scalar Equation of a Plane.section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

Distance from Point to Plane.description: The perpendicular distance from a point to a plane, calculated using the formula |ax₁ + by₁ + cz₁ - d|/√(a² + b² + c²).
Distance from Point to Plane.hint: Substitute the point coordinates into the plane equation, take absolute value, and divide by the magnitude of the normal vector.
Distance from Point to Plane.section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

Angle Between Two Planes.description: The angle between two planes equals the angle between their normal vectors, found using the dot product formula.
Angle Between Two Planes.hint: Extract normal vectors from each plane equation and use the dot product angle formula.
Angle Between Two Planes.section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

Parallel and Perpendicular Lines in Spac.description: Two lines in space are parallel if their direction vectors are scalar multiples, and perpendicular if their direction vectors have zero dot product.
Parallel and Perpendicular Lines in Spac.hint: Compare direction vectors using scalar multiplication for parallel or dot product for perpendicular.
Parallel and Perpendicular Lines in Spac.section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

Distance Between Skew Lines.description: The shortest distance between two non-intersecting, non-parallel lines in space, calculated using the scalar triple product formula.
Distance Between Skew Lines.hint: Use the formula |((P₂ - P₁) · (v₁ × v₂))/|v₁ × v₂|| where P₁, P₂ are points on each line and v₁, v₂ are direction vectors.
Distance Between Skew Lines.section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

Quadric Surface Definition.description: A three-dimensional surface defined by a second-degree equation in x, y, and z.
Quadric Surface Definition.hint: Look for squared terms in all three variables with no products like xy or xz.

Ellipsoid Standard Form.description: A quadric surface with equation (x²/a²) + (y²/b²) + (z²/c²) = 1, bounded in all directions.
Ellipsoid Standard Form.hint: All terms are positive and equal to 1; slice with planes parallel to coordinate planes to see ellipses.

Hyperboloid of One Sheet.description: A quadric surface with form (x²/a²) + (y²/b²) - (z²/c²) = 1, having one negative squared term.
Hyperboloid of One Sheet.hint: One minus sign creates a connected surface; horizontal slices are ellipses that grow with |z|.

Hyperboloid of Two Sheets.description: A quadric surface with form -(x²/a²) - (y²/b²) + (z²/c²) = 1, having two negative squared terms and two separate parts.
Hyperboloid of Two Sheets.hint: Two minus signs create two disconnected sheets; exists only where the positive term is large enough.

Elliptic Paraboloid.description: A quadric surface with form z = (x²/a²) + (y²/b²), shaped like an upward or downward bowl.
Elliptic Paraboloid.hint: One variable is linear while two are squared with the same sign; cross-sections parallel to xy-plane are ellipses.

Hyperbolic Paraboloid.description: A saddle-shaped quadric surface with form z = (x²/a²) - (y²/b²), having opposite-signed squared terms.
Hyperbolic Paraboloid.hint: Also called a saddle surface; trace curves in xz and yz planes have opposite concavity.

Elliptic Cone.description: A quadric surface with form (x²/a²) + (y²/b²) = (z²/c²), forming a double cone with vertex at origin.
Elliptic Cone.hint: Right side has only z² with no constant; horizontal slices are ellipses whose size grows linearly with |z|.

Traces of Quadric Surfaces.description: Curves formed by intersecting a quadric surface with coordinate planes or planes parallel to them.
Traces of Quadric Surfaces.hint: Set one variable to a constant and analyze the resulting 2D curve to understand the 3D shape.

Classifying Quadric Surfaces from Equati.description: The process of identifying quadric surface type by examining signs, degrees, and presence of constant terms.
Classifying Quadric Surfaces from Equati.hint: Count how many variables are squared, check if equation equals constant or variable, and note signs of terms.

Cylindrical Coordinates Definition.description: A point in space is represented by (r, θ, z) where r is distance from z-axis, θ is angle from positive x-axis, and z is height.
Cylindrical Coordinates Definition.hint: Draw the projection onto the xy-plane first, then add the z-coordinate as vertical height.

Conversion from Cylindrical to Rectangul.description: Transform cylindrical coordinates (r, θ, z) to rectangular using x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ, and z = z.
Conversion from Cylindrical to Rectangul.hint: Use the same trigonometric relationships as polar coordinates for x and y, then keep z unchanged.

Conversion from Rectangular to Cylindric.description: Transform rectangular coordinates (x, y, z) to cylindrical using r = √(x² + y²), tan θ = y/x, and z = z.
Conversion from Rectangular to Cylindric.hint: Calculate r using the distance formula in the xy-plane, find θ as you would in polar coordinates, and preserve z.

Spherical Coordinates Definition.description: A point in space is represented by (ρ, θ, φ) where ρ is distance from origin, θ is azimuthal angle, and φ is angle from positive z-axis.
Spherical Coordinates Definition.hint: Remember ρ is always nonnegative, φ ranges from 0 to π, and θ ranges from 0 to 2π.

Conversion from Spherical to Rectangular.description: Transform spherical coordinates (ρ, θ, φ) to rectangular using x = ρ sin φ cos θ, y = ρ sin φ sin θ, and z = ρ cos φ.
Conversion from Spherical to Rectangular.hint: Start with z = ρ cos φ, then use ρ sin φ as the projection radius in the xy-plane for x and y.

Conversion from Rectangular to Spherical.description: Transform rectangular coordinates (x, y, z) to spherical using ρ = √(x² + y² + z²), tan θ = y/x, and cos φ = z/ρ.
Conversion from Rectangular to Spherical.hint: Calculate ρ using the 3D distance formula, find φ from the z-component, then determine θ in the xy-plane.

Cylindrical Surfaces and Equations.description: Equations in cylindrical coordinates where one variable is constant or absent describe surfaces like circular cylinders (r = constant) or vertical planes (θ = constant).
Cylindrical Surfaces and Equations.hint: If r is constant, visualize a cylinder around the z-axis; if θ is constant, imagine a half-plane extending from the z-axis.

Spherical Surfaces and Equations.description: Equations in spherical coordinates where one variable is constant describe surfaces like spheres (ρ = constant), cones (φ = constant), or half-planes (θ = constant).
Spherical Surfaces and Equations.hint: If ρ is constant, you get a sphere; if φ is constant, you get a cone opening from the origin.

Conversion Between Cylindrical and Spher.description: Transform between cylindrical (r, θ, z) and spherical (ρ, θ, φ) using r = ρ sin φ and z = ρ cos φ, with θ the same in both.
Conversion Between Cylindrical and Spher.hint: Notice θ is identical in both systems; relate r and z to ρ and φ using right triangle relationships.

Vector-Valued Function Definition.description: A function that maps a real parameter t to a vector in space, typically written as r(t) = ⟨f(t), g(t), h(t)⟩ or r(t) = f(t)i + g(t)j + h(t)k.
Vector-Valued Function Definition.hint: Start by identifying the component functions f(t), g(t), and h(t) that give the x, y, and z coordinates respectively.

Space Curve from Vector Function.description: The set of all terminal points of position vectors r(t) as the parameter t varies, forming a curve traced through three-dimensional space.
Space Curve from Vector Function.hint: Think of t as time: as t increases, the tip of the vector r(t) traces out a path in space.

Domain of Vector-Valued Function.description: The set of all parameter values t for which all component functions f(t), g(t), h(t) are defined, typically the intersection of their individual domains.
Domain of Vector-Valued Function.hint: Find the domain of each component separately, then take the intersection of all three domains.

Limit of Vector-Valued Function.description: The limit of r(t) as t approaches a exists if the limits of all component functions exist, calculated component-wise: lim r(t) = ⟨lim f(t), lim g(t), lim h(t)⟩.
Limit of Vector-Valued Function.hint: Evaluate the limit separately for each component function using standard limit techniques.

Continuity of Vector-Valued Function.description: A vector-valued function r(t) is continuous at t = a if lim(t→a) r(t) = r(a), which occurs when all component functions are continuous at a.
Continuity of Vector-Valued Function.hint: Check continuity of each component function individually; r(t) is continuous only if all components are continuous.

Helix as Vector Function.description: A space curve of form r(t) = ⟨a cos(t), a sin(t), bt⟩ that spirals around a cylinder with constant radius a and vertical rate b.
Helix as Vector Function.hint: The xy-components trace a circle while the z-component increases linearly, creating a spiral staircase shape.

Parameterization of Space Curves.description: The process of expressing a curve in space using a parameter t, where different parameterizations can describe the same geometric curve.
Parameterization of Space Curves.hint: Multiple parameter choices can trace the same curve; consider how changing t affects the speed and direction of traversal.

Derivative of Vector-Valued Function.description: The limit of the difference quotient for vector-valued functions, computed component-wise.
Derivative of Vector-Valued Function.hint: Differentiate each component function (i, j, k) separately using single-variable calculus rules.

Tangent Vector to Space Curve.description: The derivative vector r'(t) that points in the direction of motion along a space curve.
Tangent Vector to Space Curve.hint: Evaluate the derivative at the specific parameter value to get the tangent vector at that point.

Unit Tangent Vector.description: The normalized tangent vector T(t) = r'(t)/||r'(t)|| with magnitude one.
Unit Tangent Vector.hint: Divide the tangent vector by its own magnitude to get a direction without considering speed.

Higher-Order Derivatives of Vector Funct.description: Second and higher derivatives r''(t), r'''(t) obtained by repeatedly differentiating component-wise.
Higher-Order Derivatives of Vector Funct.hint: Apply the derivative operator multiple times to each component function independently.

Indefinite Integral of Vector Function.description: The antiderivative of a vector-valued function, computed component-wise with constant vectors.
Indefinite Integral of Vector Function.hint: Integrate each component separately and add a constant vector C with arbitrary components.

Definite Integral of Vector Function.description: The integral from a to b of r(t)dt, evaluated component-wise using fundamental theorem.
Definite Integral of Vector Function.hint: Integrate each component from a to b separately, then combine into a single vector result.

Differentiation Rules for Vector Functio.description: Product rules for scalar-vector, dot product, and cross product derivatives of vector functions.
Differentiation Rules for Vector Functio.hint: For dot and cross products, apply product rule but preserve order for cross products (non-commutative).

Smooth Space Curve.description: A space curve where r'(t) is continuous and nonzero on its domain.
Smooth Space Curve.hint: Check that the derivative vector exists everywhere and never becomes the zero vector.

Arc Length of Space Curve.description: The distance along a smooth curve from one point to another, computed using the integral of the magnitude of the velocity vector.
Arc Length of Space Curve.hint: Start by finding the derivative r'(t), then compute its magnitude, and integrate over the interval.

Arc Length Parameter.description: A special parameterization s of a curve where distance along the curve equals the parameter value, making ||r'(s)|| = 1.
Arc Length Parameter.hint: To convert to arc length parameter, compute s(t) = ∫[a,t] ||r'(u)|| du, then solve for t(s) and substitute.

Curvature Definition.description: A measure of how sharply a curve bends at a given point, denoted κ (kappa), representing the rate of change of the unit tangent vector with respect to arc length.
Curvature Definition.hint: Curvature is zero for straight lines and larger for tighter curves; think about how the tangent vector direction changes.

Curvature Formula for Space Curves.description: The formula κ = ||dT/ds|| = ||T'(t)||/||r'(t)|| or equivalently κ = ||r'(t) × r''(t)||/||r'(t)||³ for computing curvature.
Curvature Formula for Space Curves.hint: Use the cross product formula when working with explicit parameterizations to avoid computing arc length parameter.

Principal Unit Normal Vector.description: The unit vector N(t) = T'(t)/||T'(t)|| that points in the direction the curve is turning, perpendicular to the tangent.
Principal Unit Normal Vector.hint: First find the unit tangent T, differentiate it, then normalize the result; N always points toward the concave side of the curve.

Binormal Vector.description: The unit vector B(t) = T(t) × N(t) that is perpendicular to both the tangent and normal vectors, completing the TNB frame.
Binormal Vector.hint: Use the cross product of T and N; the binormal is automatically a unit vector since T and N are orthononal unit vectors.

Osculating Plane.description: The plane containing the unit tangent and principal unit normal vectors at a point on a curve, representing the plane that best approximates the curve locally.
Osculating Plane.hint: The osculating plane has the binormal vector B as its normal vector; it's where the curve appears to lie instantaneously.

Radius of Curvature.description: The radius ρ = 1/κ of the osculating circle that best approximates the curve at a given point.
Radius of Curvature.hint: Larger radius means gentler curve; when curvature is small, the radius of curvature is large.

Position Vector Function.description: A vector-valued function r(t) that describes the location of a particle in space at time t.
Position Vector Function.hint: Write r(t) with components for x, y, and z coordinates, each as a function of time.

Velocity Vector in Space.description: The derivative of the position vector r'(t) = v(t), representing instantaneous velocity with direction and magnitude.
Velocity Vector in Space.hint: Differentiate each component of the position vector separately to find velocity.

Acceleration Vector in Space.description: The derivative of velocity v'(t) = a(t), or the second derivative of position r''(t), representing rate of change of velocity.
Acceleration Vector in Space.hint: Take the derivative of the velocity vector, or the second derivative of position.

Speed in Space Motion.description: The magnitude of the velocity vector ||v(t)||, a scalar representing how fast the particle moves.
Speed in Space Motion.hint: Calculate the magnitude of the velocity vector using the square root of the sum of squared components.

Projectile Motion in Space.description: Motion under constant gravitational acceleration, described by position vector with quadratic vertical component and linear horizontal components.
Projectile Motion in Space.hint: Use initial position and velocity vectors, then add the acceleration term -½gt²k to the vertical component.

Tangential and Normal Components of Acce.description: Decomposition of acceleration into aₜ (tangent to path, changes speed) and aₙ (normal to path, changes direction).
Tangential and Normal Components of Acce.hint: The tangential component aₜ = v'(t) changes speed; the normal component aₙ = κv² changes direction using curvature.

Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion.description: Three laws describing orbital motion: elliptical orbits, equal area in equal time, and period-distance relationship.
Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion.hint: Apply conservation of angular momentum and inverse-square gravitational force to derive orbital properties.

Function of Several Variables.description: A function that takes two or more independent variables as input and produces a single real number output, written as f(x,y) or f(x,y,z).
Function of Several Variables.hint: Think of temperature at different locations: each point (x,y) on a map has one temperature value.
Function of Several Variables.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Domain of Multivariable Function.description: The set of all ordered pairs or triples for which a multivariable function is defined and produces real output values.
Domain of Multivariable Function.hint: Check where denominators are zero or where expressions under square roots become negative, then exclude those points.
Domain of Multivariable Function.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Range of Multivariable Function.description: The set of all possible output values that a multivariable function can produce from its domain.
Range of Multivariable Function.hint: Find the minimum and maximum values the function can take by analyzing its behavior over its entire domain.
Range of Multivariable Function.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Graph of Two-Variable Function.description: A surface in three-dimensional space representing all points (x,y,z) where z equals f(x,y).
Graph of Two-Variable Function.hint: Plot points by choosing (x,y) values in the domain and computing z = f(x,y), then connect them to visualize the surface.
Graph of Two-Variable Function.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Level Curves.description: Curves in the xy-plane where a function f(x,y) equals a constant value k, obtained by setting f(x,y) = k.
Level Curves.hint: Like contour lines on a topographic map, connect all points at the same 'height' or function value.
Level Curves.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Level Surfaces.description: Surfaces in three-dimensional space where a function f(x,y,z) equals a constant value k, obtained by setting f(x,y,z) = k.
Level Surfaces.hint: For each constant k, solve f(x,y,z) = k to get a surface; different k values produce a family of surfaces.
Level Surfaces.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Vertical Trace of Surface.description: The curve obtained by intersecting the graph of f(x,y) with a vertical plane such as x = a or y = b.
Vertical Trace of Surface.hint: Hold one variable constant and graph the resulting single-variable function to see a cross-section of the surface.
Vertical Trace of Surface.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Function of Three Variables.description: A function w = f(x,y,z) that assigns a real number to each point in three-dimensional space within its domain.
Function of Three Variables.hint: Since we cannot visualize four dimensions directly, use level surfaces f(x,y,z) = k to understand the function's behavior.
Function of Three Variables.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Limit of Multivariable Function.description: The value L that f(x,y) approaches as (x,y) approaches (a,b) along all possible paths.
Limit of Multivariable Function.hint: Check the limit along multiple paths (straight lines, parabolas); if you get different values, the limit does not exist.
Limit of Multivariable Function.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Path-Dependent Limits.description: A limit that yields different values when approached along different paths, proving the limit does not exist.
Path-Dependent Limits.hint: Try approaching along y=0, x=0, and y=mx to test for path dependence.
Path-Dependent Limits.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Continuity of Multivariable Function.description: A function f is continuous at (a,b) if the limit equals f(a,b) and f(a,b) is defined.
Continuity of Multivariable Function.hint: Verify three conditions: f(a,b) exists, limit exists, and they are equal.
Continuity of Multivariable Function.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Interior Point of Domain.description: A point in a set where a small disk around it lies entirely within the set.
Interior Point of Domain.hint: Check if you can draw a small circle around the point that stays inside the domain.
Interior Point of Domain.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Boundary Point of Domain.description: A point where every disk around it contains points both inside and outside the set.
Boundary Point of Domain.hint: Look for points on the edge of the domain where the function definition changes.
Boundary Point of Domain.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Open and Closed Sets.description: An open set contains only interior points; a closed set contains all its boundary points.
Open and Closed Sets.hint: Check whether boundary points (like equality in inequalities) are included in the domain.
Open and Closed Sets.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Delta-Epsilon for Multivariable Limits.description: For any ε>0, there exists δ>0 such that if 0<√((x-a)²+(y-b)²)<δ, then |f(x,y)-L|<ε.
Delta-Epsilon for Multivariable Limits.hint: Use the distance formula to measure how close (x,y) is to (a,b), not separate conditions on x and y.
Delta-Epsilon for Multivariable Limits.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Squeeze Theorem for Multivariable Functi.description: If g(x,y)≤f(x,y)≤h(x,y) and both g and h approach L, then f approaches L.
Squeeze Theorem for Multivariable Functi.hint: Bound your function above and below by simpler expressions that have the same limit.
Squeeze Theorem for Multivariable Functi.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Partial Derivative Definition.description: The derivative of a multivariable function with respect to one variable while holding all other variables constant.
Partial Derivative Definition.hint: Treat all other variables as constants and differentiate with respect to only the chosen variable.
Partial Derivative Definition.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Partial Derivative Notation.description: Symbols ∂f/∂x, f_x, or ∂_x f used to denote partial derivatives with respect to variable x.
Partial Derivative Notation.hint: The rounded ∂ symbol distinguishes partial derivatives from ordinary derivatives denoted by d.
Partial Derivative Notation.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Geometric Interpretation of Partial Deri.description: The partial derivative represents the slope of the tangent line to the curve formed by intersecting the surface with a plane parallel to a coordinate plane.
Geometric Interpretation of Partial Deri.hint: Fix one variable to get a vertical slice of the surface, then find the slope in that slice.
Geometric Interpretation of Partial Deri.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Higher-Order Partial Derivatives.description: Partial derivatives taken multiple times, such as ∂²f/∂x² or mixed partials like ∂²f/∂x∂y.
Higher-Order Partial Derivatives.hint: Apply partial differentiation successively, taking the derivative of a partial derivative.
Higher-Order Partial Derivatives.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Clairaut's Theorem on Mixed Partials.description: If mixed partial derivatives are continuous, then the order of differentiation does not matter: f_xy = f_yx.
Clairaut's Theorem on Mixed Partials.hint: When functions are smooth enough, you can swap the order of partial differentiation.
Clairaut's Theorem on Mixed Partials.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Partial Derivatives of Functions of Thre.description: Computing ∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y, and ∂f/∂z for functions f(x,y,z) by holding two variables constant at a time.
Partial Derivatives of Functions of Thre.hint: The same principle applies: hold two variables constant and differentiate with respect to the third.
Partial Derivatives of Functions of Thre.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Rate of Change in Specific Direction.description: Using partial derivatives to find how a function changes when moving parallel to a coordinate axis.
Rate of Change in Specific Direction.hint: The partial derivative with respect to x gives the rate of change when moving in the x-direction only.
Rate of Change in Specific Direction.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Tangent Plane to Surface.description: A plane that touches a surface at a point and approximates the surface near that point, analogous to a tangent line for curves.
Tangent Plane to Surface.hint: Find the point of tangency first, then use partial derivatives to determine the plane's orientation at that point.
Tangent Plane to Surface.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Equation of Tangent Plane.description: The formula z - z₀ = fₓ(x₀,y₀)(x - x₀) + fᵧ(x₀,y₀)(y - y₀) that defines the tangent plane at point (x₀,y₀,z₀).
Equation of Tangent Plane.hint: Evaluate both partial derivatives at the point of tangency, then substitute into the tangent plane formula.
Equation of Tangent Plane.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Normal Vector to Surface.description: A vector perpendicular to the tangent plane at a point, given by ⟨fₓ, fᵧ, -1⟩ for surface z = f(x,y).
Normal Vector to Surface.hint: Compute the gradient of F(x,y,z) = f(x,y) - z to find the normal vector at any point.
Normal Vector to Surface.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Linear Approximation of Two-Variable Fun.description: The approximation L(x,y) = f(x₀,y₀) + fₓ(x₀,y₀)(x - x₀) + fᵧ(x₀,y₀)(y - y₀) that estimates function values near (x₀,y₀).
Linear Approximation of Two-Variable Fun.hint: Use the tangent plane's z-value as your linear approximation—it's the same formula.
Linear Approximation of Two-Variable Fun.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Differentiability of Multivariable Funct.description: A function is differentiable at a point if it can be closely approximated by its tangent plane near that point.
Differentiability of Multivariable Funct.hint: Check that both partial derivatives exist and are continuous at the point—this guarantees differentiability.
Differentiability of Multivariable Funct.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Differential of Multivariable Function.description: The expression dz = fₓ dx + fᵧ dy representing the change in z based on small changes dx and dy in the inputs.
Differential of Multivariable Function.hint: Think of dz as the linear approximation of Δz—multiply each partial derivative by the corresponding input change.
Differential of Multivariable Function.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Total Differential.description: For w = f(x,y,z), the total differential dw = fₓ dx + fᵧ dy + f_z dz represents approximate change in all variables.
Total Differential.hint: Extend the two-variable differential by adding a term for each additional independent variable.
Total Differential.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Error Approximation Using Differentials.description: Using differentials to estimate the propagation of measurement errors through a function calculation.
Error Approximation Using Differentials.hint: Replace dx, dy with the actual measurement errors (uncertainties) to estimate the error in the output.
Error Approximation Using Differentials.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Chain Rule for Multivariable Composition.description: The chain rule extends to composite functions where intermediate variables depend on multiple independent variables, requiring summation over all paths of differentiation.
Chain Rule for Multivariable Composition.hint: Draw a tree diagram showing all paths from the independent variable to the dependent variable, then sum the products of derivatives along each path.
Chain Rule for Multivariable Composition.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Chain Rule with One Independent Variable.description: When z depends on intermediate variables that all depend on a single parameter t, dz/dt equals the sum of partial derivatives times ordinary derivatives along each path.
Chain Rule with One Independent Variable.hint: If z = f(x(t), y(t)), use dz/dt = (∂z/∂x)(dx/dt) + (∂z/∂y)(dy/dt) by summing over both intermediate variables.
Chain Rule with One Independent Variable.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Chain Rule with Multiple Independent Var.description: When z depends on intermediate variables that depend on multiple independent variables, compute each partial derivative by summing over all intermediate paths.
Chain Rule with Multiple Independent Var.hint: For ∂z/∂s, hold other independent variables constant and sum (∂z/∂x)(∂x/∂s) + (∂z/∂y)(∂y/∂s) over all intermediate variables.
Chain Rule with Multiple Independent Var.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Tree Diagram for Chain Rule.description: A visual diagram showing the dependency structure from dependent variable through intermediate variables to independent variables, used to identify all differentiation paths.
Tree Diagram for Chain Rule.hint: Draw branches from each variable to those it directly depends on, then multiply derivatives along each branch and sum all paths between your two variables.
Tree Diagram for Chain Rule.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Implicit Differentiation for Multivariab.description: Using the multivariable chain rule to find derivatives when variables are related implicitly through equations like F(x,y,z) = 0.
Implicit Differentiation for Multivariab.hint: Apply the chain rule to both sides of the implicit equation, treating the dependent variable as a function of independent variables.
Implicit Differentiation for Multivariab.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Generalized Chain Rule Formula.description: For w = f(x₁,...,xₘ) where each xᵢ = gᵢ(t₁,...,tₙ), the partial derivative ∂w/∂tⱼ equals the sum of (∂w/∂xᵢ)(∂xᵢ/∂tⱼ) over all i.
Generalized Chain Rule Formula.hint: Count intermediate variables carefully and write one term in the sum for each intermediate variable.
Generalized Chain Rule Formula.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Directional Derivative Definition.description: The rate of change of a multivariable function in the direction of a given unit vector.
Directional Derivative Definition.hint: Start by finding partial derivatives, then combine them using the components of your direction vector.
Directional Derivative Definition.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Unit Direction Vector.description: A vector with magnitude 1 that specifies the direction for computing a directional derivative.
Unit Direction Vector.hint: Normalize your direction vector by dividing by its magnitude before computing the directional derivative.
Unit Direction Vector.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Gradient Vector.description: The vector of all first-order partial derivatives of a scalar function, denoted ∇f or grad f.
Gradient Vector.hint: The gradient is ⟨∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y⟩ for two variables or ⟨∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y, ∂f/∂z⟩ for three variables.
Gradient Vector.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Directional Derivative Formula with Grad.description: The directional derivative equals the dot product of the gradient with the unit direction vector: D_u f = ∇f · u.
Directional Derivative Formula with Grad.hint: Compute the gradient first, then take its dot product with your unit direction vector.
Directional Derivative Formula with Grad.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Direction of Maximum Increase.description: A function increases most rapidly in the direction of its gradient vector.
Direction of Maximum Increase.hint: The gradient always points in the direction where the function grows fastest.
Direction of Maximum Increase.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Maximum Rate of Change.description: The magnitude of the gradient gives the maximum rate of change of the function at a point.
Maximum Rate of Change.hint: Calculate ||∇f|| to find how steep the function is in its steepest direction.
Maximum Rate of Change.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Gradient Perpendicular to Level Curves.description: The gradient vector is always perpendicular to level curves (or level surfaces) of the function.
Gradient Perpendicular to Level Curves.hint: If you move along a level curve, the function value doesn't change, so the directional derivative is zero there.
Gradient Perpendicular to Level Curves.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Directional Derivative in Three Variable.description: Extension of directional derivatives to functions of three variables using the three-dimensional gradient.
Directional Derivative in Three Variable.hint: Use the same formula D_u f = ∇f · u, but now ∇f and u are three-dimensional vectors.
Directional Derivative in Three Variable.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Critical Point of Multivariable Function.description: A point where all first-order partial derivatives are zero or undefined.
Critical Point of Multivariable Function.hint: Set both ∂f/∂x = 0 and ∂f/∂y = 0, then solve the system simultaneously.
Critical Point of Multivariable Function.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Second Partial Derivative Test.description: A test using the discriminant D = f_xx·f_yy - (f_xy)² to classify critical points as local maxima, minima, or saddle points.
Second Partial Derivative Test.hint: Calculate D at the critical point: if D > 0 and f_xx > 0, it's a local minimum; if D > 0 and f_xx < 0, it's a local maximum.
Second Partial Derivative Test.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Saddle Point.description: A critical point that is neither a local maximum nor minimum, where the function increases in some directions and decreases in others.
Saddle Point.hint: If D < 0 in the second derivative test, you have a saddle point regardless of f_xx.
Saddle Point.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Absolute Extrema on Closed Bounded Regio.description: The global maximum and minimum values of a continuous function on a closed and bounded domain.
Absolute Extrema on Closed Bounded Regio.hint: Check all critical points in the interior AND all points on the boundary, then compare function values.
Absolute Extrema on Closed Bounded Regio.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Lagrange Multiplier Method.description: A technique for finding extrema of a function subject to equality constraints using the condition ∇f = λ∇g.
Lagrange Multiplier Method.hint: Set up the system ∇f = λ∇g and g = constraint value, then solve for all variables including λ.
Lagrange Multiplier Method.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Constraint Equation in Optimization.description: An equality condition that restricts the domain of an optimization problem, such as g(x,y) = c.
Constraint Equation in Optimization.hint: Express one variable in terms of the other using the constraint, or use Lagrange multipliers if substitution is difficult.
Constraint Equation in Optimization.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Boundary Analysis for Extrema.description: The process of examining function values along the edges of a closed region to find absolute extrema.
Boundary Analysis for Extrema.hint: Parameterize each boundary curve separately and use single-variable calculus to find extrema on each piece.
Boundary Analysis for Extrema.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Discriminant for Classification.description: The value D = f_xx·f_yy - (f_xy)² evaluated at a critical point to determine its nature.
Discriminant for Classification.hint: Always compute all three second derivatives at the critical point before calculating D.
Discriminant for Classification.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Constrained Optimization Problem.description: Finding extrema of a function subject to one or more constraint equations.
Constrained Optimization Problem.hint: Identify your objective function and constraint separately before applying any method.
Constrained Optimization Problem.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Lagrange Multiplier Variable.description: An auxiliary variable λ introduced to convert constrained optimization into a system of equations.
Lagrange Multiplier Variable.hint: Treat λ as an unknown variable to be solved for simultaneously with x and y.
Lagrange Multiplier Variable.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Lagrange Function Construction.description: Building L(x,y,λ) = f(x,y) - λ(g(x,y) - c) where g(x,y) = c is the constraint.
Lagrange Function Construction.hint: Subtract λ times the constraint equation (moved to equal zero) from the objective function.
Lagrange Function Construction.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

System of Lagrange Equations.hint: You must solve all three equations simultaneously; the λ-equation gives back your constraint.
System of Lagrange Equations.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Geometric Interpretation of Lagrange Mul.description: At extrema, ∇f is parallel to ∇g, meaning gradient of objective is perpendicular to constraint curve.
Geometric Interpretation of Lagrange Mul.hint: Visualize that level curves of f must be tangent to the constraint curve at optimal points.
Geometric Interpretation of Lagrange Mul.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Two Constraints Lagrange Method.description: Optimizing f(x,y,z) subject to g(x,y,z) = c₁ and h(x,y,z) = c₂ using two multipliers λ and μ.
Two Constraints Lagrange Method.hint: Construct L with two multiplier terms and solve five equations from ∇L = 0.
Two Constraints Lagrange Method.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Interpreting Lagrange Multiplier Value.description: The value λ approximates the rate of change of the optimal value with respect to the constraint constant.
Interpreting Lagrange Multiplier Value.hint: If λ = 5, increasing the constraint bound by 1 unit changes the optimal value by approximately 5.
Interpreting Lagrange Multiplier Value.chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

Double Integral over Rectangle.description: The iterated integral of a two-variable function over a rectangular region in the xy-plane.
Double Integral over Rectangle.hint: Start by identifying the constant bounds for x and y, then decide which variable to integrate first.

Rectangular Region Definition.description: A region in the plane defined by a ≤ x ≤ b and c ≤ y ≤ d with constant bounds.
Rectangular Region Definition.hint: Draw the rectangle showing the four corner points (a,c), (b,c), (a,d), (b,d) to visualize your integration region.

Fubini's Theorem for Rectangles.description: States that a double integral over a rectangle can be computed as an iterated integral in either order if the function is continuous.
Fubini's Theorem for Rectangles.hint: If the integral looks hard in one order, try switching the order of integration—both give the same answer.

Iterated Integral Notation.description: Writing a double integral as nested single integrals with specified bounds for each variable.
Iterated Integral Notation.hint: Read from inside out: the innermost integral is computed first while treating the other variable as constant.

Volume Under Surface Interpretation.description: A double integral represents the signed volume between the surface z = f(x,y) and the xy-plane over the region.
Volume Under Surface Interpretation.hint: Sketch the surface and region to see that you're accumulating height times area to get volume.

Riemann Sum for Double Integrals.description: The limit of sums of function values times areas of rectangular subrectangles as partition becomes finer.
Riemann Sum for Double Integrals.hint: Think of approximating volume by stacking rectangular boxes of height f(x,y) and base area ΔA.

Order of Integration Choice.description: Deciding whether to integrate first with respect to x or y when evaluating an iterated integral.
Order of Integration Choice.hint: Choose the order that makes the inner integral easier to evaluate, especially if one involves a simpler antiderivative.

Properties of Double Integrals.description: Linearity, additivity over regions, and comparison properties that hold for double integrals.
Properties of Double Integrals.hint: Use linearity to split complicated integrands into simpler pieces you can integrate separately.

Type I Region Definition.description: A region bounded by vertical lines x=a and x=b and curves y=g₁(x) and y=g₂(x) where g₁(x) ≤ g₂(x).
Type I Region Definition.hint: Sketch the region and identify which boundary curves depend on x; integrate dy first, then dx.

Type II Region Definition.description: A region bounded by horizontal lines y=c and y=d and curves x=h₁(y) and x=h₂(y) where h₁(y) ≤ h₂(y).
Type II Region Definition.hint: Sketch the region and identify which boundary curves depend on y; integrate dx first, then dy.

Double Integral over General Region.description: An integral ∬_D f(x,y) dA where D is a non-rectangular region defined by variable boundary curves.
Double Integral over General Region.hint: Extend f to be zero outside D, or use limits that follow the boundary curves directly.

Iterated Integral with Variable Limits.description: An integral where inner limits depend on the outer variable, like ∫[a to b]∫[g₁(x) to g₂(x)] f(x,y) dy dx.
Iterated Integral with Variable Limits.hint: Evaluate the inner integral treating the outer variable as constant, substitute limits before integrating outer.

Converting Between Type I and Type II.description: Rewriting a double integral by switching the order of integration and adjusting limits to match region boundaries.
Converting Between Type I and Type II.hint: Sketch the region, identify new constant bounds for the outer integral, then express the inner bounds as functions.

Volume Over Non-Rectangular Base.description: The volume between a surface z=f(x,y) and a general planar region D, computed as ∬_D f(x,y) dA.
Volume Over Non-Rectangular Base.hint: Set up the double integral with limits matching the region's shape; interpret as signed volume if f is negative.

Determining Integration Order.description: Choosing which variable to integrate first based on region boundaries and integrand complexity.
Determining Integration Order.hint: Try both orders on paper; pick the one where inner bounds are simpler functions or integrand integrates easily.

Properties of Double Integrals over Gene.description: Linearity, additivity over non-overlapping subregions, and comparison properties extend to general domains.
Properties of Double Integrals over Gene.hint: When a region splits naturally, compute separate integrals over each piece and add results.

Polar to Rectangular Conversion.description: Transformations x = r cos(θ) and y = r sin(θ) that convert polar coordinates to Cartesian coordinates.
Polar to Rectangular Conversion.hint: Draw a right triangle with r as hypotenuse to visualize why x and y involve cosine and sine.

Area Element in Polar Coordinates.description: The differential area element dA = r dr dθ accounts for the stretching of area in polar coordinates.
Area Element in Polar Coordinates.hint: The extra r factor appears because sectors farther from origin cover more area for the same angle change.

Double Integral in Polar Form.description: An integral ∫∫_R f(r,θ) r dr dθ where the integrand includes the Jacobian factor r.
Double Integral in Polar Form.hint: Never forget the r in 'r dr dθ' — it's part of the area element, not optional.

Polar Region Type.description: Regions described by r ranging from r₁(θ) to r₂(θ) and θ ranging between two angles.
Polar Region Type.hint: Sketch the region first; identify which rays bound it and which curves bound the radial extent.

Converting Cartesian Integrals to Polar.description: The process of rewriting ∫∫ f(x,y) dA as ∫∫ f(r cos θ, r sin θ) r dr dθ when symmetry suggests polar.
Converting Cartesian Integrals to Polar.hint: Look for circles, circular sectors, or expressions like x² + y² that simplify to r² in polar.

Circular Region Integration.description: Integration over disks or annuli where 0 ≤ r ≤ a and 0 ≤ θ ≤ 2π gives natural polar limits.
Circular Region Integration.hint: For a full circle centered at origin, r goes from 0 to the radius and θ goes from 0 to 2π.

Jacobian for Polar Transformation.description: The determinant r of the transformation matrix ∂(x,y)/∂(r,θ) that scales area elements.
Jacobian for Polar Transformation.hint: Compute the 2×2 matrix of partial derivatives and take its determinant to verify the r factor.

Triple Integral Definition.description: An integral of a function of three variables over a three-dimensional region, extending the double integral concept to one more dimension.
Triple Integral Definition.hint: Think of triple integrals as stacking infinitely many double integrals, just as double integrals stack single integrals.

Rectangular Box Region.description: A three-dimensional region defined by constant bounds in x, y, and z coordinates, forming a rectangular prism.
Rectangular Box Region.hint: Write the region as {(x,y,z) : a ≤ x ≤ b, c ≤ y ≤ d, e ≤ z ≤ f} with all bounds constant.

Fubini's Theorem for Triple Integrals.description: For continuous functions over rectangular boxes, the triple integral can be computed as three successive single integrals in any order.
Fubini's Theorem for Triple Integrals.hint: If the region is a box and the function is continuous, evaluate the three integrals one at a time in any order you choose.

Type I Three-Dimensional Region.description: A region where z is bounded between two functions of x and y, while (x,y) varies over a region in the xy-plane.
Type I Three-Dimensional Region.hint: Integrate with respect to z first using variable bounds z = g₁(x,y) to z = g₂(x,y), then handle the xy-region.

Order of Integration in Triple Integrals.description: The six possible sequences (dzdydx, dzdxdy, dydzdx, dydxdz, dxdzdy, dxdydz) for evaluating a triple integral.
Order of Integration in Triple Integrals.hint: Choose the order that makes your bounds simplest; sketch the region and identify which variable should be innermost.

Volume Calculation with Triple Integrals.description: The volume of a three-dimensional region is computed as the triple integral of 1 over that region.
Volume Calculation with Triple Integrals.hint: To find volume only, set f(x,y,z) = 1 in your triple integral; the bounds define the region's shape.

Cylindrical Coordinates for Triple Integ.description: A coordinate system using (r, θ, z) where r and θ are polar coordinates in the xy-plane and z is the vertical coordinate, with volume element r dr dθ dz.
Cylindrical Coordinates for Triple Integ.hint: Use cylindrical coordinates when the region or function has circular symmetry about the z-axis; don't forget the extra r factor.

Spherical Coordinates for Triple Integra.description: A coordinate system using (ρ, θ, φ) where ρ is distance from origin, θ is azimuthal angle, and φ is angle from positive z-axis, with volume element ρ² sin φ dρ dθ dφ.
Spherical Coordinates for Triple Integra.hint: Use spherical coordinates for regions like spheres or cones; the volume element ρ² sin φ is essential and easily forgotten.

Cylindrical Coordinate System.description: A three-dimensional coordinate system using radius r, angle θ, and height z to locate points.
Cylindrical Coordinate System.hint: Remember that cylindrical coordinates extend polar coordinates by adding a z-component for height.
Cylindrical Coordinate System.section: 5.5 Triple Integrals in Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinates

Cylindrical to Rectangular Conversion.description: Converting cylindrical coordinates (r, θ, z) to Cartesian coordinates using x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ, z = z.
Cylindrical to Rectangular Conversion.hint: Use the same conversion formulas as polar coordinates for x and y, keeping z unchanged.
Cylindrical to Rectangular Conversion.section: 5.5 Triple Integrals in Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinates

Volume Element in Cylindrical Coordinate.description: The differential volume element dV = r dr dθ dz in cylindrical coordinates, where r is the Jacobian factor.
Volume Element in Cylindrical Coordinate.hint: The extra factor r comes from the Jacobian of the transformation, similar to polar coordinates but extended to 3D.
Volume Element in Cylindrical Coordinate.section: 5.5 Triple Integrals in Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinates

Triple Integral in Cylindrical Form.description: Expressing a triple integral as an iterated integral with limits in r, θ, and z coordinates with volume element r dr dθ dz.
Triple Integral in Cylindrical Form.hint: Always include the r factor in your integrand when converting to cylindrical coordinates.
Triple Integral in Cylindrical Form.section: 5.5 Triple Integrals in Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinates

Spherical Coordinate System.description: A coordinate system using radius ρ from origin, azimuthal angle θ, and polar angle φ from positive z-axis.
Spherical Coordinate System.hint: Think of ρ as distance from origin, φ as angle down from north pole, and θ as rotation around z-axis.
Spherical Coordinate System.section: 5.5 Triple Integrals in Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinates

Spherical to Rectangular Conversion.description: Converting spherical coordinates (ρ, θ, φ) to Cartesian using x = ρ sin φ cos θ, y = ρ sin φ sin θ, z = ρ cos φ.
Spherical to Rectangular Conversion.hint: Remember that φ is measured from the positive z-axis, so z uses cos φ while x and y use sin φ.
Spherical to Rectangular Conversion.section: 5.5 Triple Integrals in Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinates

Volume Element in Spherical Coordinates.description: The differential volume element dV = ρ² sin φ dρ dθ dφ in spherical coordinates, with Jacobian ρ² sin φ.
Volume Element in Spherical Coordinates.hint: The factor ρ² sin φ comes from the Jacobian determinant; don't forget it when setting up integrals.
Volume Element in Spherical Coordinates.section: 5.5 Triple Integrals in Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinates

Triple Integral in Spherical Form.description: Expressing a triple integral as an iterated integral with limits in ρ, θ, and φ coordinates with volume element ρ² sin φ dρ dθ dφ.
Triple Integral in Spherical Form.hint: Always include the ρ² sin φ factor in your integrand when converting to spherical coordinates.
Triple Integral in Spherical Form.section: 5.5 Triple Integrals in Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinates

Choosing Coordinate System for Integrati.description: Selecting cylindrical, spherical, or rectangular coordinates based on the symmetry of the region and integrand.
Choosing Coordinate System for Integrati.hint: Use cylindrical for regions with z-axis symmetry, spherical for regions centered at origin with radial symmetry.
Choosing Coordinate System for Integrati.section: 5.5 Triple Integrals in Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinates

Mass from Density Function.description: The total mass is computed by integrating the density function over the region or solid.
Mass from Density Function.hint: Set up a double or triple integral of the density function ρ(x,y) or ρ(x,y,z) over your region.
Mass from Density Function.section: 5.6 Calculating Centers of Mass and Moments of Inertia

Center of Mass Coordinates.description: The center of mass (x̄, ȳ, z̄) is the weighted average position where an object's mass is concentrated.
Center of Mass Coordinates.hint: Use the formulas x̄ = Mx/m, ȳ = My/m, z̄ = Mz/m where m is total mass and Mx, My, Mz are moments.
Center of Mass Coordinates.section: 5.6 Calculating Centers of Mass and Moments of Inertia

First Moment about Axis.description: The moment about an axis measures the tendency of mass to rotate about that axis, computed by integrating position times density.
First Moment about Axis.hint: For moment about the x-axis, integrate y·ρ(x,y) over the region; for y-axis, integrate x·ρ(x,y).
First Moment about Axis.section: 5.6 Calculating Centers of Mass and Moments of Inertia

Moment of Inertia Definition.description: Moment of inertia measures resistance to rotational acceleration about an axis, computed by integrating squared distance times density.
Moment of Inertia Definition.hint: For rotation about the x-axis, compute Ix = ∫∫y²ρ(x,y) dA; use squared distance from the axis.
Moment of Inertia Definition.section: 5.6 Calculating Centers of Mass and Moments of Inertia

Radius of Gyration.description: The radius of gyration is the distance from the axis where the entire mass could be concentrated to produce the same moment of inertia.
Radius of Gyration.hint: Calculate using Rx = √(Ix/m) where Ix is moment of inertia about x-axis and m is total mass.
Radius of Gyration.section: 5.6 Calculating Centers of Mass and Moments of Inertia

Lamina Mass Distribution.description: A lamina is a thin flat plate with mass distributed according to a two-dimensional density function.
Lamina Mass Distribution.hint: Use double integrals over the planar region to compute mass and moments for flat objects.
Lamina Mass Distribution.section: 5.6 Calculating Centers of Mass and Moments of Inertia

Three-Dimensional Center of Mass.description: For solid objects, the center of mass is found using triple integrals of x·ρ, y·ρ, and z·ρ divided by total mass.
Three-Dimensional Center of Mass.hint: Set up three separate triple integrals for moments Myz, Mxz, and Mxy, then divide each by total mass.
Three-Dimensional Center of Mass.section: 5.6 Calculating Centers of Mass and Moments of Inertia

General Coordinate Transformation.description: A mapping from one coordinate system (u,v) to another (x,y) via continuous functions x=g(u,v) and y=h(u,v).
General Coordinate Transformation.hint: Write explicit formulas for x and y in terms of the new variables u and v, ensuring the transformation is smooth and invertible.
General Coordinate Transformation.section: 5.7 Change of Variables in Multiple Integrals

Jacobian Determinant.description: The determinant of partial derivatives ∂(x,y)/∂(u,v) that measures how area scales under coordinate transformation.
Jacobian Determinant.hint: Compute the 2×2 matrix of first partial derivatives, then find its determinant; remember the absolute value matters for integration.
Jacobian Determinant.section: 5.7 Change of Variables in Multiple Integrals

Change of Variables Formula.description: The formula ∬_R f(x,y)dA = ∬_S f(g(u,v),h(u,v))|J|dudv for transforming double integrals between coordinate systems.
Change of Variables Formula.hint: Replace x and y with their expressions in u and v, multiply by the absolute Jacobian, and integrate over the transformed region S.
Change of Variables Formula.section: 5.7 Change of Variables in Multiple Integrals

Region Transformation.description: The process of mapping a region R in the xy-plane to a simpler region S in the uv-plane using coordinate transformation.
Region Transformation.hint: Apply the inverse transformation to boundary curves of R to find the corresponding boundaries in the uv-plane.
Region Transformation.section: 5.7 Change of Variables in Multiple Integrals

Three-Dimensional Jacobian.description: The determinant ∂(x,y,z)/∂(u,v,w) that measures volume scaling for transformations in three dimensions.
Three-Dimensional Jacobian.hint: Construct the 3×3 matrix of all nine first partial derivatives, then compute its determinant using cofactor expansion.
Three-Dimensional Jacobian.section: 5.7 Change of Variables in Multiple Integrals

Change of Variables in Triple Integrals.description: The formula ∭_D f(x,y,z)dV = ∭_E f(g(u,v,w),h(u,v,w),k(u,v,w))|J|dudvdw for transforming triple integrals.
Change of Variables in Triple Integrals.hint: Substitute all three coordinate expressions, multiply by the absolute three-dimensional Jacobian, and integrate over the transformed region E.
Change of Variables in Triple Integrals.section: 5.7 Change of Variables in Multiple Integrals

Elliptical Region Transformation.description: A technique using transformation (x,y)=(au,bv) to convert elliptical regions into circular regions for easier integration.
Elliptical Region Transformation.hint: For ellipse x²/a²+y²/b²=1, use u=x/a and v=y/b to transform it into the unit circle u²+v²=1.
Elliptical Region Transformation.section: 5.7 Change of Variables in Multiple Integrals

Jacobian Verification for Standard Coord.description: Computing Jacobians for polar, cylindrical, and spherical coordinates to verify known volume elements r, r, and ρ²sin(φ).
