Jacobian Verification for Standard Coord.hint: Calculate the Jacobian from first principles using the standard coordinate conversion formulas to confirm the area or volume element.
Jacobian Verification for Standard Coord.section: 5.7 Change of Variables in Multiple Integrals

Vector Field Definition.description: A function that assigns a vector to each point in a region of space, written as F(x,y) = ⟨P(x,y), Q(x,y)⟩ in 2D or F(x,y,z) = ⟨P(x,y,z), Q(x,y,z), R(x,y,z)⟩ in 3D.
Vector Field Definition.hint: Think of assigning an arrow (with direction and magnitude) to every point in your domain, like wind velocity at each location on a map.

Component Functions of Vector Field.description: The scalar functions P, Q, and R that define the x, y, and z components respectively of a vector field F = ⟨P, Q, R⟩.
Component Functions of Vector Field.hint: Extract each component separately: if F(x,y) = ⟨x²y, xy³⟩, then P(x,y) = x²y and Q(x,y) = xy³.

Gradient Vector Field.description: A vector field of the form F = ∇f where f is a scalar function, making F = ⟨∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y, ∂f/∂z⟩.
Gradient Vector Field.hint: To verify a field is a gradient field, find a potential function f whose partial derivatives match each component of F.

Vector Field Visualization.description: A graphical representation showing vectors drawn at sample points in the domain, with arrow length indicating magnitude and direction showing field direction.
Vector Field Visualization.hint: Plot vectors at a grid of points; use shorter arrows for smaller magnitudes to see patterns like sources, sinks, or circulation.

Radial Vector Field.description: A vector field where all vectors point directly toward or away from a central point, typically of the form F = ⟨x, y⟩ or F = r/||r|| where r is the position vector.
Radial Vector Field.hint: Check if F(x,y) is proportional to the position vector ⟨x, y⟩; vectors should point radially outward or inward from the origin.

Rotational Vector Field.description: A vector field where vectors rotate around a central point or axis, such as F(x,y) = ⟨-y, x⟩ which rotates counterclockwise around the origin.
Rotational Vector Field.hint: Look for components that are perpendicular to the position vector; F = ⟨-y, x⟩ gives circulation since F·⟨x,y⟩ = 0.

Unit Vector Field.description: A vector field where every vector has magnitude 1, obtained by normalizing a vector field: F/||F||.
Unit Vector Field.hint: Divide each component by the magnitude √(P² + Q² + R²) to normalize; this shows only direction, not magnitude.

Conservative Vector Field.description: A vector field F that is the gradient of some scalar potential function, meaning F = ∇f for some f, which implies the field is path-independent.
Conservative Vector Field.hint: Test if ∂P/∂y = ∂Q/∂x in 2D; if yes, integrate P with respect to x to find potential function f, then verify ∂f/∂y = Q.

Scalar Line Integral.description: An integral of a scalar function along a curve, computing accumulation of values along a path.
Scalar Line Integral.hint: Parametrize the curve, then integrate f(r(t)) times the speed ||r'(t)|| with respect to t.

Parametrization of Curves.description: A vector function r(t) that traces out a curve as the parameter t varies over an interval.
Parametrization of Curves.hint: Express x, y, z as functions of a single parameter t, ensuring the curve is traced exactly once.

Arc Length Element ds.description: The differential element ds = ||r'(t)|| dt representing infinitesimal distance along a curve.
Arc Length Element ds.hint: Compute the magnitude of the velocity vector r'(t) to find ds in terms of dt.

Vector Line Integral.description: An integral computing work done by a vector field F along a curve, written as integral of F·dr.
Vector Line Integral.hint: Substitute the parametrization into F, compute F(r(t))·r'(t), then integrate with respect to t.

Orientation of Curve.description: The direction in which a curve is traversed, affecting the sign of line integrals.
Orientation of Curve.hint: Reversing orientation changes the sign of vector line integrals but not scalar line integrals.

Piecewise Smooth Curve.description: A curve composed of finitely many smooth pieces joined end-to-end.
Piecewise Smooth Curve.hint: Integrate over each smooth piece separately, then sum the results.

Work as Line Integral.description: Physical work done by force field F moving object along curve C, computed as integral of F·dr.
Work as Line Integral.hint: Work equals the line integral of the tangential component of force along the path.

Line Integral with Respect to Coordinate.description: Integrals of form integral P dx + Q dy, representing components of work in each coordinate direction.
Line Integral with Respect to Coordinate.hint: Use dx = x'(t)dt and dy = y'(t)dt to convert to a standard integral in t.

Path Independence.description: A line integral whose value depends only on the endpoints, not the path taken between them.
Path Independence.hint: If two different paths give the same integral value for all choices, the field is path-independent.

Conservative Vector Field Characterizati.description: A vector field F is conservative if and only if it is the gradient of some scalar potential function.
Conservative Vector Field Characterizati.hint: Look for a function φ where F = ∇φ; this means F is conservative.

Potential Function.description: A scalar function φ whose gradient equals a given conservative vector field F.
Potential Function.hint: To find φ, integrate each component of F and combine results, ensuring consistency.

Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals.description: For conservative F = ∇φ, the line integral from A to B equals φ(B) - φ(A).
Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals.hint: Just evaluate the potential function at endpoints like the regular Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

Closed Curve.description: A curve where the initial and terminal points coincide.
Closed Curve.hint: A closed curve returns to its starting point, making the endpoints identical.

Line Integral over Closed Path.description: For conservative fields, the line integral around any closed curve equals zero.
Line Integral over Closed Path.hint: If the integral around every closed loop is zero, the field is conservative.

Cross-Partial Test.description: For F = ⟨P, Q⟩, if ∂P/∂y = ∂Q/∂x on a simply connected domain, then F is conservative.
Cross-Partial Test.hint: Check if the mixed partial derivatives match; if yes and domain is nice, the field is conservative.

Simply Connected Domain.description: A region in which every closed curve can be continuously shrunk to a point without leaving the region.
Simply Connected Domain.hint: Think of regions without holes; a disk is simply connected, but an annulus is not.

Finding Potential Functions by Integrati.description: The method of integrating component functions and matching terms to construct the potential function.
Finding Potential Functions by Integrati.hint: Integrate P with respect to x, then differentiate to find what Q adds, and combine carefully.

Three-Dimensional Cross-Partial Test.description: For F = ⟨P, Q, R⟩, F is conservative if ∂P/∂y = ∂Q/∂x, ∂P/∂z = ∂R/∂x, and ∂Q/∂z = ∂R/∂y.
Three-Dimensional Cross-Partial Test.hint: Check all three pairs of mixed partials; all must match for the field to be conservative in 3D.

Green's Theorem Statement.description: Relates a line integral around a simple closed curve to a double integral over the region it encloses: ∮_C (P dx + Q dy) = ∬_R (∂Q/∂x - ∂P/∂y) dA.
Green's Theorem Statement.hint: Identify P and Q from your vector field F = ⟨P, Q⟩, then compute the partial derivatives ∂Q/∂x and ∂P/∂y.

Circulation Form of Green's Theorem.description: Expresses circulation around a closed curve as the integral of curl over the enclosed region: ∮_C F·dr = ∬_R curl(F)·k dA.
Circulation Form of Green's Theorem.hint: The curl in 2D is the scalar ∂Q/∂x - ∂P/∂y, which measures the rotation tendency of the field.

Flux Form of Green's Theorem.description: Relates outward flux across a closed curve to the divergence over the enclosed region: ∮_C F·n ds = ∬_R div(F) dA.
Flux Form of Green's Theorem.hint: The normal vector n is perpendicular to the tangent; if C is parametrized by r(t), then n points outward when rotated 90° clockwise from the tangent.

Positive Orientation of Boundary.description: A curve is positively oriented if the region lies to the left as you traverse the boundary counterclockwise.
Positive Orientation of Boundary.hint: For simple regions, counterclockwise orientation is positive; always verify the region stays on your left as you walk the path.

Simply Connected Region Requirement.description: Green's Theorem requires the region to be simply connected (no holes) or requires modification for regions with holes.
Simply Connected Region Requirement.hint: If your region has holes, apply Green's Theorem with outer boundary positive and inner boundaries negative oriented.

Area Calculation via Green's Theorem.description: The area of a region R can be computed as A = (1/2)∮_C (x dy - y dx) using Green's Theorem.
Area Calculation via Green's Theorem.hint: Choose P = -y/2 and Q = x/2 so that ∂Q/∂x - ∂P/∂y = 1, making the double integral equal the area.

Converting Line to Double Integral.description: Green's Theorem converts a potentially difficult line integral into a double integral that may be easier to evaluate.
Converting Line to Double Integral.hint: If the line integral is complex but the region is simple, use Green's Theorem to switch to a double integral over the region.

Two-Dimensional Divergence.description: For a 2D vector field F = ⟨P, Q⟩, the divergence is div(F) = ∂P/∂x + ∂Q/∂y, measuring net outflow per unit area.
Two-Dimensional Divergence.hint: Compute each partial derivative separately: differentiate P with respect to x, then Q with respect to y, and add them.

Two-Dimensional Curl Scalar.description: For a 2D vector field F = ⟨P, Q⟩, the scalar curl is ∂Q/∂x - ∂P/∂y, measuring counterclockwise rotation per unit area.
Two-Dimensional Curl Scalar.hint: The curl is the difference of cross-partials; a positive value indicates counterclockwise circulation in the field.

Three-Dimensional Divergence Operator.description: The divergence of vector field F = ⟨P, Q, R⟩ is div F = ∂P/∂x + ∂Q/∂y + ∂R/∂z, measuring outward flow rate per unit volume.
Three-Dimensional Divergence Operator.hint: Compute each partial derivative separately, then add them; divergence is a scalar that measures field expansion or compression.

Three-Dimensional Curl Operator.description: The curl of vector field F = ⟨P, Q, R⟩ is curl F = ⟨∂R/∂y - ∂Q/∂z, ∂P/∂z - ∂R/∂x, ∂Q/∂x - ∂P/∂y⟩, measuring rotational tendency.
Three-Dimensional Curl Operator.hint: Use the determinant formula with i, j, k in first row, ∂/∂x, ∂/∂y, ∂/∂z in second row, and P, Q, R in third row.

Del Operator Notation.description: The del operator ∇ = ⟨∂/∂x, ∂/∂y, ∂/∂z⟩ allows writing divergence as ∇·F and curl as ∇×F using dot and cross products.
Del Operator Notation.hint: Treat ∇ like a vector of partial derivative operators; use dot product for divergence and cross product for curl.

Divergence-Free Vector Field.description: A vector field with divergence equal to zero everywhere is called divergence-free or incompressible, indicating no net sources or sinks.
Divergence-Free Vector Field.hint: Check if the sum of all three partial derivatives equals zero; physically, this means fluid density remains constant.

Curl-Free Vector Field.description: A vector field with curl equal to zero everywhere is called curl-free or irrotational, which is equivalent to being conservative.
Curl-Free Vector Field.hint: If curl F = 0, the field has a potential function; check that all three components of curl vanish.

Physical Interpretation of Divergence.description: Divergence measures the rate of outward flux per unit volume at a point, indicating sources (positive) or sinks (negative) in the field.
Physical Interpretation of Divergence.hint: Positive divergence means field lines are spreading out from the point; negative means converging into it.

Physical Interpretation of Curl.description: Curl measures the infinitesimal rotation of a vector field, with direction given by right-hand rule and magnitude indicating rotation speed.
Physical Interpretation of Curl.hint: Imagine a tiny paddle wheel in the flow; curl direction is the axis of rotation, magnitude is rotational speed.

Vector Field Identity div(curl F) = 0.description: For any vector field F with continuous second partial derivatives, the divergence of its curl is always zero.
Vector Field Identity div(curl F) = 0.hint: Expand div(curl F) using mixed partial derivatives; terms cancel by equality of mixed partials.

Parametric Surface Definition.description: A surface defined by a vector function r(u,v) mapping a region D in the uv-plane to three-dimensional space.
Parametric Surface Definition.hint: Write r(u,v) = ⟨x(u,v), y(u,v), z(u,v)⟩ with clear bounds for parameters u and v.

Tangent Vectors to Parametric Surface.description: The partial derivatives r_u and r_v represent tangent vectors along parameter curves on the surface.
Tangent Vectors to Parametric Surface.hint: Compute ∂r/∂u and ∂r/∂v by differentiating each component separately.

Cross Product for Surface Normal.description: The cross product r_u × r_v produces a normal vector to the parametric surface at each point.
Cross Product for Surface Normal.hint: Use the determinant formula with i, j, k in the first row and components of r_u and r_v in rows two and three.

Scalar Surface Integral.description: The integral ∬_S f(x,y,z) dS evaluates a scalar function over a surface S.
Scalar Surface Integral.hint: Convert to ∬_D f(r(u,v)) |r_u × r_v| du dv and integrate over the parameter domain D.

Oriented Surface.description: A surface with a continuously chosen normal vector direction at each point, determining positive orientation.
Oriented Surface.hint: Check if your normal vector r_u × r_v points in the desired direction; if not, swap parameters or negate.

Vector Surface Integral (Flux).description: The integral ∬_S F·n dS measures the flux of vector field F through oriented surface S.
Vector Surface Integral (Flux).hint: Compute F·(r_u × r_v) and integrate over the parameter domain; no magnitude needed here.

Graph Surface Parametrization.description: A surface z = g(x,y) can be parametrized as r(x,y) = ⟨x, y, g(x,y)⟩ with standard tangent vectors.
Graph Surface Parametrization.hint: For z = g(x,y), use r_x × r_y = ⟨-g_x, -g_y, 1⟩ to simplify dS = √(1 + g_x² + g_y²) dx dy.

Flux Through Closed Surface.description: The total flux ∬_S F·n dS through a closed surface measures net outward flow of the vector field.
Flux Through Closed Surface.hint: For closed surfaces, ensure the normal vector points outward consistently; this connects to the divergence theorem.

Stokes' Theorem Statement.description: Relates the surface integral of curl F over a surface S to the line integral of F around the boundary curve C: ∬_S (curl F)·n dS = ∮_C F·dr.
Stokes' Theorem Statement.hint: Make sure the orientation of the boundary curve matches the surface normal using the right-hand rule.

Orientation Compatibility of Surface and.description: The boundary curve orientation must match the surface normal via the right-hand rule: fingers curl along C, thumb points along n.
Orientation Compatibility of Surface and.hint: If your fingers curl in the direction of the boundary curve, your thumb should point in the direction of the surface normal.

Stokes' Theorem for Closed Surfaces.description: For a closed surface with no boundary, Stokes' theorem gives ∬_S (curl F)·n dS = 0 since there is no boundary curve.
Stokes' Theorem for Closed Surfaces.hint: A closed surface like a sphere has no boundary curve, so the line integral part vanishes automatically.

Computing Circulation via Stokes' Theore.description: Calculate circulation ∮_C F·dr by instead computing the flux of curl F through any surface bounded by C.
Computing Circulation via Stokes' Theore.hint: Choose the simplest surface that has your curve as its boundary—often a flat disk is easiest.

Independence of Surface in Stokes' Theor.description: The circulation around C equals the flux of curl F through any surface having C as boundary, regardless of which surface is chosen.
Independence of Surface in Stokes' Theor.hint: If computing flux through one surface is hard, try a different surface with the same boundary curve.

Verifying Stokes' Theorem by Direct Comp.description: Confirm Stokes' theorem by computing both ∮_C F·dr directly and ∬_S (curl F)·n dS and showing they are equal.
Verifying Stokes' Theorem by Direct Comp.hint: Calculate the line integral using parametrization of C, then calculate the surface integral using parametrization of S, and check equality.

Stokes' Theorem as 3D Green's Theorem.description: Stokes' theorem generalizes Green's theorem from planar regions to curved surfaces in three-dimensional space.
Stokes' Theorem as 3D Green's Theorem.hint: Green's theorem is the special case when the surface is flat and lies in the xy-plane.

Application to Conservative Fields via S.description: If F is conservative (curl F = 0 everywhere), then ∮_C F·dr = 0 for any closed curve C by Stokes' theorem.
Application to Conservative Fields via S.hint: Use Stokes' theorem with curl F = 0 to show the line integral vanishes around any closed loop.

Divergence Theorem Statement.description: The flux of a vector field through a closed surface equals the triple integral of its divergence over the enclosed volume.
Divergence Theorem Statement.hint: Write the theorem as ∬_S F·n dS = ∭_E div F dV where S is the closed boundary of solid E.

Simple Solid Region.description: A solid region bounded by a closed piecewise smooth surface where the Divergence Theorem applies.
Simple Solid Region.hint: Check that your region E has a well-defined boundary surface S that completely encloses it without gaps.

Outward Orientation Convention.description: For closed surfaces in Divergence Theorem, the normal vector points outward from the enclosed solid.
Outward Orientation Convention.hint: When computing flux, always use the outward-pointing normal vector for the Divergence Theorem to hold.

Converting Flux to Volume Integral.description: Using Divergence Theorem to replace a difficult surface integral with a triple integral over the enclosed volume.
Converting Flux to Volume Integral.hint: Compute div F first, then set up the triple integral ∭_E div F dV in appropriate coordinates (Cartesian, cylindrical, or spherical).

Verifying Divergence Theorem.description: Confirming the theorem by computing both the surface flux and volume integral independently and showing they are equal.
Verifying Divergence Theorem.hint: Calculate the flux ∬_S F·n dS directly by parametrizing each surface piece, then separately compute ∭_E div F dV.

Divergence Theorem for Multiple Surfaces.description: Applying the theorem when the boundary consists of several distinct smooth surface pieces that together form a closed surface.
Divergence Theorem for Multiple Surfaces.hint: Break the closed surface into parametrizable pieces (like top, bottom, sides), then sum the flux contributions or use the volume integral.

Physical Interpretation of Divergence Th.description: The net outward flow through a boundary equals the total generation of flow within the region.
Physical Interpretation of Divergence Th.hint: Think of divergence as a source density: positive divergence creates outward flux, negative divergence creates inward flux.

Gauss's Law Application.description: Using the Divergence Theorem to derive Gauss's Law in electrostatics relating electric flux to enclosed charge.
Gauss's Law Application.hint: For electric field E with div E = ρ/ε₀, the Divergence Theorem shows total flux equals Q_enclosed/ε₀.

Second-Order Linear Differential Equatio.description: A differential equation of the form a(x)y'' + b(x)y' + c(x)y = r(x) where a(x) is not identically zero.
Second-Order Linear Differential Equatio.hint: Identify the coefficients of y'', y', and y to recognize the standard form.

Homogeneous Linear Equation.description: A second-order linear equation where the right-hand side r(x) equals zero.
Homogeneous Linear Equation.hint: If there's no term without y or its derivatives, the equation is homogeneous.

Nonhomogeneous Linear Equation.description: A second-order linear equation where the right-hand side r(x) is a non-zero function.
Nonhomogeneous Linear Equation.hint: The presence of a forcing term r(x) makes the equation nonhomogeneous.

Linear Independence of Solutions.description: Two solutions y₁ and y₂ are linearly independent if neither is a constant multiple of the other.
Linear Independence of Solutions.hint: Check if y₂/y₁ is constant; if not, the solutions are linearly independent.

General Solution of Homogeneous Equation.description: The complete solution y = c₁y₁ + c₂y₂ where y₁, y₂ are linearly independent solutions and c₁, c₂ are arbitrary constants.
General Solution of Homogeneous Equation.hint: Combine two linearly independent solutions with arbitrary constants to get all solutions.

Particular Solution.description: A specific solution to a nonhomogeneous equation that satisfies the equation but contains no arbitrary constants.
Particular Solution.hint: Find any single function that satisfies the nonhomogeneous equation.

Superposition Principle.description: If y₁ and y₂ are solutions to a homogeneous linear equation, then c₁y₁ + c₂y₂ is also a solution for any constants c₁, c₂.
Superposition Principle.hint: Linear combinations of solutions are solutions because the equation is linear.

Initial Value Problem for Second-Order O.description: A second-order differential equation with two initial conditions specifying y and y' at a point x₀.
Initial Value Problem for Second-Order O.hint: Use both y(x₀) and y'(x₀) conditions to solve for the two arbitrary constants.

Method of Undetermined Coefficients.description: A technique for finding particular solutions by guessing a solution form based on the nonhomogeneous term and determining coefficients by substitution.
Method of Undetermined Coefficients.hint: Match your guess to the form of the right-hand side, then substitute into the equation and solve for unknown coefficients.

Complementary Solution.description: The general solution to the associated homogeneous equation, denoted yc, which forms part of the complete solution.
Complementary Solution.hint: Solve the homogeneous version first by setting the right-hand side to zero.

General Solution of Nonhomogeneous Equat.description: The complete solution y = yc + yp, combining the complementary solution and any particular solution.
General Solution of Nonhomogeneous Equat.hint: Add your homogeneous solution to any particular solution you find.

Modification Rule for Repeated Terms.description: When the guessed form duplicates a term in the complementary solution, multiply by x (or higher powers) until linearly independent.
Modification Rule for Repeated Terms.hint: If your guess matches a homogeneous solution term, multiply by x and try again.

Variation of Parameters Formula.description: A general method using integrals of known homogeneous solutions multiplied by the nonhomogeneous term to construct particular solutions.
Variation of Parameters Formula.hint: Use the Wronskian of your two homogeneous solutions and integrate to find the varying coefficients.

Exponential Input Function Form.description: For nonhomogeneous terms of form Ce^(αx), guess yp = Ae^(αx) unless α matches a characteristic root.
Exponential Input Function Form.hint: Try yp = Ae^(αx) first; if α is a characteristic root, multiply by x.

Polynomial Input Function Form.description: For nonhomogeneous terms that are polynomials of degree n, guess a polynomial of the same degree with undetermined coefficients.
Polynomial Input Function Form.hint: Match the polynomial degree: if right side is degree 2, try Ax² + Bx + C.

Sinusoidal Input Function Form.description: For nonhomogeneous terms involving sin(βx) or cos(βx), guess yp = A cos(βx) + B sin(βx) even if only one appears on the right side.
Sinusoidal Input Function Form.hint: Always include both sine and cosine in your guess, even if only one appears in the equation.

Wronskian in Variation of Parameters.description: The determinant W(y₁, y₂) = y₁y₂' - y₁'y₂ used in the denominator of the variation of parameters formula.
Wronskian in Variation of Parameters.hint: Calculate the Wronskian from your two homogeneous solutions before setting up the integrals.

Spring-Mass System Modeling.description: A second-order ODE mx'' + cx' + kx = F(t) models a mass on a spring with damping and external force.
Spring-Mass System Modeling.hint: Identify m (mass), c (damping coefficient), k (spring constant), and F(t) (external force) from the physical setup.

Simple Harmonic Motion.description: Undamped free vibration described by mx'' + kx = 0, resulting in sinusoidal oscillation with angular frequency ω = sqrt(k/m).
Simple Harmonic Motion.hint: When there's no damping or external force, solve the characteristic equation r² + (k/m) = 0 to find purely imaginary roots.

Damped Harmonic Motion.description: Vibration with damping mx'' + cx' + kx = 0 exhibits overdamped, critically damped, or underdamped behavior based on discriminant c² - 4mk.
Damped Harmonic Motion.hint: Calculate the discriminant: if positive (overdamped), solution decays without oscillation; if negative (underdamped), solution oscillates with decay.

Resonance in Forced Oscillations.description: When the forcing frequency matches the natural frequency of an undamped system, amplitude grows unboundedly over time.
Resonance in Forced Oscillations.hint: If solving mx'' + kx = F₀cos(ωt) and ω equals sqrt(k/m), use the modification rule to include a factor of t in your particular solution.

RLC Circuit Modeling.description: A second-order ODE LQ'' + RQ' + (1/C)Q = E(t) models charge Q in a series circuit with inductor L, resistor R, and capacitor C.
RLC Circuit Modeling.hint: The structure is identical to spring-mass systems: L corresponds to mass, R to damping, 1/C to spring constant.

Steady-State Solution.description: The particular solution that persists after transient terms decay, representing long-term behavior of a forced system.
Steady-State Solution.hint: After finding the general solution, let t approach infinity to see which terms vanish (transient) and which remain (steady-state).

Transient Solution Behavior.description: The complementary solution component that decays to zero over time in damped systems.
Transient Solution Behavior.hint: If the characteristic equation roots have negative real parts, those exponential terms decay as t increases.

Critical Damping Condition.description: When c² = 4mk, the system returns to equilibrium in minimum time without oscillating.
Critical Damping Condition.hint: Look for repeated roots in the characteristic equation; this indicates critical damping and requires a solution form c₁e^(rt) + c₂te^(rt).

Power Series Solution Method.description: A technique for solving differential equations by assuming a solution in the form of an infinite power series and determining coefficients.
Power Series Solution Method.hint: Write y as an infinite sum of terms a_n*x^n, substitute into the differential equation, and match coefficients of like powers.

Ordinary Point of Differential Equation.description: A point x_0 where the coefficient functions of a differential equation are analytic, allowing power series solutions centered at x_0.
Ordinary Point of Differential Equation.hint: Check if the coefficient of y'' is nonzero at x_0 and if ratios of coefficients are analytic there.

Radius of Convergence for Series Solutio.description: The interval around the expansion point where the power series solution converges, determined by singularities of coefficient functions.
Radius of Convergence for Series Solutio.hint: Use the ratio test on your series or find the distance to the nearest singularity of the differential equation's coefficients.

Recurrence Relation for Coefficients.description: An equation relating coefficients a_n in a power series solution, derived by matching powers of x after substitution.
Recurrence Relation for Coefficients.hint: After substituting the series into the differential equation, collect all terms with x^n and set the coefficient equal to zero.

Frobenius Method.description: A technique for finding series solutions near regular singular points using a generalized power series with a leading exponent r.
Frobenius Method.hint: Assume a solution of the form x^r times a power series, then solve the indicial equation for r.

Regular Singular Point.description: A singular point x_0 where (x-x_0)p(x) and (x-x_0)²q(x) are analytic, allowing Frobenius series solutions.
Regular Singular Point.hint: Check if xp(x) and x²q(x) remain bounded as x approaches the singular point.

Indicial Equation.description: An equation for the exponent r in Frobenius method, obtained from the lowest power term after substitution.
Indicial Equation.hint: Substitute y = x^r times series into the differential equation and set the coefficient of the lowest power of x to zero.

Two Linearly Independent Series Solution.description: The process of constructing a general solution by finding two independent power series solutions, often from different initial conditions or indicial roots.
Two Linearly Independent Series Solution.hint: For ordinary points, use two arbitrary constants a_0 and a_1; for singular points, use both roots of the indicial equation if they differ by a non-integer.
